Heterodox Roundup: Week of April 26 to May 2, 2026

Cover decoding: The candle in the darkened window centers the week’s political-violence cluster — Cole Tomas Allen’s April 26 attempt to penetrate the Washington Hilton during the White House Correspondents’ Dinner. The oil tanker silhouette under a twilight Strait of Hormuz captures the Iran cluster, where the active iran-time-direction open disagreement sharpened. The data-center complex with cooling towers and electrical pylons points to the cross-publication convergence on the politics of electrons. The leaning Greek column with hairline cracks signals the Tooze/Sternberg/UnHerd structural-decline cluster. The stylized circuit board with a small soap-bubble overhead sits inside the AI-bubble debate (Atlantic, Cowen-at-FP, Noahpinion). Background touches — faint district-line shapes and a small unsteady donkey on fractured terrain — gesture to the Callais-redistricting and Democrats-after-Trump clusters. The composition is somber-but-engaged: the week diagnosed institutional decay across many domains, but writers from across the political coordinate space still showed up to argue with each other.

By Theme

The political-violence cycle and the Cole Allen story

The week-defining event was the April 26 attempt by Cole Tomas Allen — a 31-year-old Caltech-trained mechanical engineer, a devout Christian, never on the FBI’s radar — to penetrate the Washington Hilton during the White House Correspondents’ Dinner with the goal of killing Trump and his Cabinet. The roster converged on the event from at least four non-overlapping rhetorical positions: factual reporting that complicates the early “radicalized leftist” frame (Klippenstein, in three pieces), symmetric anti-rationalization (Silver), explicit refusal to let condemnation foreclose Trump-criticism (Goldberg), and right-coded “the left structurally pushes toward violence” reading (MacIntyre, Howland, Flanagan in a softer key). Ahmari supplies a fifth: Allen as Lyotardian postmodern symptom, killing for private moral self-purification rather than any grand emancipatory project. The Israel/antisemitism cluster (Mandel on London, Jacobson on Polanski) belongs adjacent — same political-violence taxonomy, different valence and geography. The convergence-across-orientations is the week’s clearest case of an event that broke through the partisan filter; the disagreement is over what the event means and what political work that meaning is being conscripted to do.


Assassin Wasn’t on FBI’s Radar, Sources Say — Ken Klippenstein, Ken Klippenstein — 2026-04-26 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: The April 26 attempt by Cole Tomas Allen, 31, to enter the White House Correspondents’ Dinner armed; the Trump administration’s claim (echoed in major outlets) that Allen “hates Christians”; and the surfacing of a manifesto and Bluesky activity attributed to Allen.

Drawing on a senior FBI source, Allen’s resume, and former-classmate interviews, Klippenstein reports that Allen was never flagged by the FBI’s domestic counterterrorism apparatus — i.e., he is the kind of “normal guy until he wasn’t” attacker the surveillance state’s posture is supposed to catch and didn’t. Allen graduated from Caltech (Mechanical Engineering, 2017), worked at a Southern California precision-engineering firm (IJK Controls) on stabilization hardware used in drones and military targeting, built an entire video game from scratch (Bohrdom) including its physics engine and score, and was president of Caltech’s Nerf club where he reportedly pushed back against members modifying toy guns to look like real weapons. Classmates describe him as “studious, devout, and polite” and “pretty Christian and mellow.” His manifesto (per the New York Post version) invokes the Sermon on the Mount and “render unto Caesar” — but to defend the attack on grounds that turning the other cheek doesn’t extend to others’ suffering and that constitutional law overrides deference to authority. Klippenstein’s structural point: Trump’s “anti-Christian” framing dovetails with NSPM-7 (Trump’s National Security Presidential Memorandum 7, which labels anti-Christianity as a domestic terrorism indicator) and will license more surveillance funding regardless of the facts — and the question no one is asking is why “increasingly normal people” feel violence is the only available political channel.


Cole Allen Hated the Democratic Party, Too — Ken Klippenstein, Ken Klippenstein — 2026-04-28 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: The Trump administration’s framing (echoed by RNC chair Joe Gruters, acting AG Todd Blanche, and MSNOW correspondent Ken Dilanian) of accused White House Correspondents’ Association (WHCA) dinner gunman Cole Tomas Allen as a “radicalized” left-wing extremist, and to NSPM-7, Trump’s National Security Presidential Memorandum 7, which labels common left-coded beliefs as indicators of domestic terrorism.

Klippenstein pulls Allen’s Bluesky posts to show that Allen’s contempt was bipartisan, not far-left. Allen repeatedly called for replacing the Democratic Party with a third “Do Something” party, demanded a vote of no confidence in Schumer, and mocked Schumer’s hoarding tendencies as “an RPG player who hoards every single potion.” Klippenstein situates this in polling context: by early 2025, Democratic Party approval had fallen to 27% (NBC) and 29% (CNN — the lowest since 1992), with 59% of Democrats disapproving of their own congressional leadership. He argues that lumping Allen with Luigi Mangione (the UnitedHealthcare CEO shooter) and Tyler Robinson (Charlie Kirk’s assassin) as “far-left fringe” obscures what unites them: a sense of failed institutions and a willingness to embody action where institutions deliver inaction. Crucially, this reframes the official narrative as a category error — the “fringe” position (institutional contempt) is now mainstream majority opinion.


Trump’s enemies keep reaching for the gun — Auron MacIntyre, The Blaze — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: The 4/29 Cole Allen incident in West Palm Beach (a man with a long gun apprehended outside a Trump event) interpreted through the prior assassination of Charlie Kirk and the multiple 2024-25 attempts on Trump’s life.

MacIntyre treats Allen as confirmation of a structural pattern: progressives, he writes, have enjoyed “exclusive access” to domestic political violence “for at least 70 years,” from 1960s race riots through 1970s Weather Underground bombings to present-day assassinations and Supreme Court intimidation. His core analytic move is to elevate Max Weber’s monopoly-on-violence framework — usually invoked to explain failed states losing ground to gangs and cartels — and apply it to U.S. left/right asymmetry, arguing that the Trump administration’s failure to retaliate after Kirk’s killing has effectively conceded that monopoly to the left. The contrast pivot is January 6: the Biden DOJ, MacIntyre says, prosecuted “people hundreds of miles away” with tangential connections, sending an unambiguous deterrent signal — and that is the model the Trump administration must now adopt against organizations like Antifa and the SPLC. The piece closes on the July 4, 2026 sestercentennial as a soft-target risk forecast, framing inaction as an invitation to mass-casualty attacks on Trump rallies.


Political Violence Is Reprehensible. That Doesn’t Make Trump Less Depraved. — Michelle Goldberg, NYT — 2026-04-27 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: The April 25 incident in which Cole Tomas Allen was arrested attempting to storm the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner at the Washington Hilton with apparent intent to assassinate Trump, and the right-wing/centrist response (National Review, WSJ editorial board, CNN’s Dana Bash pressing Jamie Raskin) blaming “feverish opposition” and “heated rhetoric” for the attack.

Goldberg accepts a sharper premise than most liberal commentators: Allen really is the first “normie liberal terrorist” — unlike Thomas Matthew Crooks (the nihilistic Republican misfit who grazed Trump’s ear in 2024), Ryan Wesley Routh (the Ukraine-fixated would-be golf-course shooter), or Tyler Robinson (charged in Charlie Kirk’s killing, radicalized in online fetish subcultures), Allen’s reposted social-media history shows ordinary anti-ICE, pro-Ukraine, pro-impeachment views, even some hostility to Hasan Piker and pro-Palestine protesters. That conventional profile is what makes him “irresistible” as a rhetorical cudgel for conservatives. But — and this is the hinge — Goldberg refuses the bargain on offer: the fact that Trump has been targeted cannot become a reason to euphemize his depravity, only a reason to reiterate that depravity does not justify violence. She concedes Allen’s manifesto contains a real overreach (calling Trump a “pedophile” rather than the more defensible Epstein-coverup framing) and cites CSIS data that both sides overestimate the other’s appetite for violence, creating a polarization ratchet. She closes with the strategic argument familiar from the 1970s left-terrorism-to-Reagan and Oklahoma-City-to-Clinton precedents: violence helps the target, as Dana White’s “awesome” reaction at the dinner tacitly confirmed.


Can we please stop rationalizing political violence? — Nate Silver, Nate Silver — 2026-04-28 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: The April 26 attempted assassination at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner by Cole Tomas Allen, who according to his manifesto targeted Trump, Vance, and other Cabinet officials by rank; and a broader pattern of political violence (Butler 2024, Charlie Kirk’s assassination by Tyler Robinson, Luigi Mangione’s killing of UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson).

Silver structures the piece around two observations from a recorded conversation with Eli McKown-Dawson (his colleague who was at the Substack New Media Party near the White House when the incident unfolded). First: the muted social-media reaction — Twitter continued discussing NBA playoffs and the wait-but-why “red and blue button” thought experiment — is itself a worrying signal. Two or three times a year there’s a political event that “stops the world in its tracks”; the Allen attempt did not, despite being an attempted presidential assassination. He invokes the near-miss / forecasting principle: a candidate who loses by 100 votes tells you nearly the same thing as one who wins by 100, so a near-miss assassination should update us toward “the nation is constantly flirting with disaster,” not “everything turned out OK.” Second: he names three substantive rhetorical moves that enable political violence without explicitly endorsing it — rationalization (Senator Chris Murphy’s “yeah, but” framing of Mangione, where the disclaimer doesn’t immunize the rationalization that follows), denial (semi-respectable Democratic commentators flirting with false-flag theories about Butler and Saturday night, or Heather Cox Richardson initially framing Tyler Robinson as a right-wing MAGA Groyper), and minimization (“the other side is worse”). He’s explicit about what he’s not claiming: not that one side is worse, not that violent metaphors should be policed, not that anyone is obligated to perform outrage.


Voters Take Keys Away From Elder Politician — Ken Klippenstein, Ken Klippenstein — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: The 4/30 announcement by Maine Governor Janet Mills (78) that she was suspending her Senate primary campaign against Graham Platner (a 41-year-old oyster farmer who outraised her 4-to-1 and led by 27+ points), and the NYT/WaPo/WSJ lede paragraphs that uncritically attributed the exit to “financial resources.”

Klippenstein reframes a story major papers told as Beltway cashflow as instead a story of voters defeating Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer’s hand-picked recruit — Mills, the first woman elected Maine governor, picked because Susan Collins’s seat is the only Republican-held one in a Harris-won state. He notes Mills ran an aggressive campaign (digging up Reddit posts of Platner’s, pledging a one-term limit) and was clearly playing to win. The fact that papers all repeated her own “ran out of money” framing without asking why the money didn’t materialize is, he argues, a symptom of what he calls “Journalism 1.0” — the convention that what an official says must be reported even when it is “obvious bullshit.” Mills would have been the oldest freshman senator in US history, 86 at the end of a first term. Klippenstein then pivots to argue that the same loss of faith in geriatric establishment institutions that voted Mills out also drives the wave of political shootings — explicitly not equating them, but locating both in one well of institutional contempt.


Cole Thomas Allen is a postmodern symptom — Sohrab Ahmari, UnHerd — 2026-04-27 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: the attempted assassination of President Trump and his Cabinet in Washington this past weekend by Cole Tomas Allen — a young credentialed professional whose published manifesto frames the killing as moral self-purification (“I am no longer willing to permit a pedophile, rapist, and traitor to coat my hands with his crimes”) rather than as the opening move of any larger political project.

Ahmari — UnHerd US editor and author of Tyranny, Inc. — uses Allen as a hook to revive Jean-François Lyotard’s 1979 The Postmodern Condition and its central diagnosis: the collapse of grand narratives (Enlightenment liberalism, scientific Positivism, Freudian psychoanalysis, Marxism). The hinge is generic-historical: 19th-century political assassins (the Russian socialist revolutionaries who killed Tsar Alexander II, anarchist Leon Czolgosz killing McKinley) committed violence as instrumental moves toward a collective emancipatory future; Allen — and Luigi Mangione before him, Ahmari pairs them — kills as a private moral act, untethered from any vision of collective change. Both Allen (Caltech) and Mangione (Penn) are credentialed professionals — the “stressed and downwardly mobile” managerial class that midcentury “late capitalism” (Jameson) elevated and that the Tech Right’s reassertion is now displacing. Ahmari’s broader claim: the asset-poor majority is now confronting the new bourgeoisie (Musk, Thiel, Karp) directly, with the mediating professional class being squeezed out, and Allen-type random violence is the symptom of that mediating class’s epistemic and political dislocation. The optimistic close: a Christian revival could anchor new grand narratives “from below.”


Plato’s Cave and the Rise of the Highly Educated Radical — Jacob Howland, Free Press — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: The April 25 attempted assassination of President Trump and his cabinet at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner by Cole Tomas Allen, a 31-year-old California Institute of Technology computer-science graduate described by his professor as “a very good student.” The piece also draws on the December Brown University shooting by a physics prodigy and Luigi Mangione’s December 2024 alleged murder of UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson — both perpetrators with elite-university credentials. Howland cites a survey showing Americans with graduate degrees are about twice as likely to support political violence as those with some-college-or-less.

Howland’s argument runs through Plato’s Allegory of the Cave from The Republic. In Plato’s image, prisoners chained to face a cave wall watch shadows cast by puppets manipulated by hidden elites; the shadows are their entire reality, and they think the shadows are the beings themselves. The puppeteers control what counts as noble and base, friend and enemy. Howland argues this is much of what passes for higher education today: professors have taught generations of students to reduce individuals to identity-group abstractions, politics to zero-sum relations of power, and truth to the fiat of those who rule. This conveniently truncated, certified, and credentialed education does not produce skeptics — it produces zealots, because the cave dweller who believes the puppeteers are the enemy doesn’t become a philosopher, he becomes a warrior in the shadow war. Cole Allen, by every measure a successful product of the modern university system, was exposed to the philosophy that one’s political opponents are abstractions of malevolence rather than people with whom one might reason. Howland adds a contemporary twist: in Plato’s cave the prisoners shared one echo chamber, but algorithmic feedback loops, social media silos, and political manipulation have divided the modern cave into innumerable echo chambers — Allen “was not poorly informed; he was simply in a particular part of the cave where the shadows on the wall told a story that ended in the Washington Hilton.” The piece concludes that Plato’s escapee is killed by the others when he tries to lead them out, just as Socrates was executed for corrupting the young — and that what happened at the Hilton was not just a security failure but an education failure. “The most dangerous man is not the one who knows nothing. It is the one who has been taught just enough to be certain.”


Caitlin Flanagan: Pray for America — Caitlin Flanagan, Free Press — 2026-04-29 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: The April 25, 2026 attempted assassination of President Trump and his cabinet at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner at the Washington Hilton, perpetrated by Cole Tomas Allen. The piece is also Caitlin Flanagan’s debut column at the Free Press following her departure from the Atlantic, introduced at length by Nellie Bowles.

Flanagan, born in 1961 (“the era of assassinations” — JFK, Malcolm X, MLK, Bobby Kennedy, Medgar Evers, Fred Hampton, the Mississippi civil-rights workers), opens with autobiography. She grew up between Berkeley and Ireland, was caught in a Derry street battle on the first anniversary of internment, watched the Symbionese Liberation Army siege at age 12 thinking she was watching Patty Hearst burn alive. Her “internal threat assessment is permanently stuck on high alert.” Yet when she saw the news of the Hilton shooting on her phone at a dinner party, she “didn’t really feel anything at all.” She reported the news to her tablemates; they all had the same flat response, no one reached for a phone for more details, no one asked about motive — “we had all absorbed the essential fact that nobody had been injured and we went on to other things.” That non-reaction, she argues, is the diagnostic: political violence is back in the realm of the unremarkable. She makes two predictive claims about its current cycle. First, the perpetrators will be young, because youth combines awareness of intolerable conditions with awareness of one’s own physical power and bravery-trembling-on-foolishness — “that’s why old people don’t fight wars; they declare them.” Second, political violence “produces romantic heroes faster than MGM in the 1940s”: Huey Newton in the peacock chair, the Che Guevara photograph, and now Luigi Mangione, whose handsome image survives even Luigi: The Musical. When she saw Cole Allen’s photo and learned he too was handsome, she knew he would join their ranks. The piece closes with a parallel: she was 20 when Reagan was shot, called her parents flippantly because they hated Reagan, and her father “cut me off at the knees: ‘This is very serious.’” Flanagan then describes Weijia Jiang’s announcement at the Correspondents’ Dinner that Trump and the cabinet were safe, and the brief sober applause from the journalists in the room — a moment “in which the office was larger than the man.” NBC posted the moment on TikTok with the applause edited out. “This is America right now, where nothing seems real and where your sympathies are open to constant manipulation.” For the first time in her life, she has been doing what the Catholic priests of her childhood asked: praying for America.


Anti-Jewish Anarchy in London — Seth Mandel, Commentary — 2026-04-29 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: a recent escalation in London street-level anti-Jewish incidents (the specific event triggering this column is not extractable from the preview but the timing aligns with the Free Press Keir Starmer piece and UnHerd Howard Jacobson piece on the same beat from this period), framed by Mandel as the moment at which the standard “wake-up call” rhetoric loses its meaning.

[Note: Commentary’s article body is paywalled and only the lead paragraph is publicly extractable. The summary below reconstructs from that lead, the title, and Mandel’s standing register on UK anti-Semitism.]

The opening move — “It’s only a wake-up call if the callee is conscious. Britain, having been on the receiving end of a steady parade of wake-up calls on anti-Semitism, is either dead or in a coma. There’s one other possibility, of course: the UK is wide awake to the threat of anti-Semitism but…” — sets up a structural argument about British political responsiveness rather than a discrete-event analysis. Mandel’s standing position on this beat: that successive UK governments (Conservative under Sunak/Truss/Johnson, now Labour under Starmer) have demonstrated a pattern of rhetorical condemnation of anti-Semitic incidents while taking inadequate enforcement action against the actors and ideologies producing them. The hinge in the preview — the unfinished “but…” sentence — almost certainly leads to a claim of deliberate accommodation: that British political and institutional actors are choosing not to confront the political coalitions producing anti-Jewish incidents because the political cost of doing so is too high. The Commentary register typically sites the cost in (a) the Labour Party’s relationship to British Muslim voters, (b) the Metropolitan Police’s institutional reluctance to enforce protest-and-public-order law against pro-Palestinian demonstrations, and (c) the BBC and broader UK media’s framing conventions around Israel-Palestine.


Why Jews fear Zack Polanski — Howard Jacobson, UnHerd — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →

Responding to: the Green Party’s surge under new leader Zack Polanski ahead of UK local elections, with the Greens running second to Labour in some polls and pulling Gaza-coded support; framed against the recent stabbings in Golders Green (a heavily Jewish north London neighbourhood) and Polanski’s “perception of unsafety” remark dismissing British Jews’ fears.

Jacobson — a Booker-winning novelist who knew the Paulden family personally in Manchester — opens with a Hamlet pastiche of Keir Starmer (the once-DPP, now Prime Minister) before pivoting: among Labour’s external rivals, the Greens are the most dangerous because they have transformed from “smug but innocuous” activists into a “smug but menacing army” presenting a “deceptively benign countenance.” The hinge is that Polanski né Paulden — a Jew himself, having changed his name from the more obviously English Paulden — gives the Jew-uncomfortable cover to vote Jew-uncomfortable, with candidates ritually invoking “our leader is a Jewish man” as immunization against antisemitism charges. Jacobson reads Polanski’s “proud Jew” self-description as the tell of a defector-in-waiting, and his “perception of unsafety” formulation — paired with simultaneous Islamophobia call-outs — as the giveaway. The pivot to attack police handling of the Golders Green suspect after first condemning the stabbings shows Polanski’s signature double-facing. The broader claim: extreme parties have learned what social media did first — to algorithmically deliver constituents the lies they already believe.
### Iran in the second phase — time direction, Hormuz, Lebanon

The active iran-time-direction open disagreement sharpens this week as commentary clusters along the fault line of who the clock favors. On the time-favors-Iran side, Aurelien extends his elite-incapacity diagnosis to argue that Western institutions have lost the kind of people and slack required to manage a converging energy-and-food crisis at all. The opposite pole is staked out by Eli Lake at the Free Press, who builds an empirical assault on Robert Pape’s “escalation trap” framework — the load-bearing intellectual scaffolding for the time-favors-Iran position in much of the DC discourse. Between them, the Atlantic publishes three pieces (Wright, Azizi, Youssef-Lemire) that mostly cluster on the “both sides think they won” / “Hormuz-leverage-is-permanent” side without endorsing Aurelien’s full collapse story. Sajjad Safaei at War on the Rocks adds a structural constraint the financial-attrition theorists have underweighted: Iran cannot abandon Lebanon without unraveling the Axis of Resistance. From the right, NR runs two pieces in different registers — McCarthy on the War Powers Act, Thorne on Hormuz as the opening of a U.S.-China “New Great Game” — that frame the legal and strategic significance of the same blockade.


It Does Now. — Aurelien, Aurelien — 2026-04-29 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Original commentary, framed as a follow-up to his 4/25 piece “To A Conclusion” on the Islamabad US-Iran “talks” — extending the diagnosis that Western elites cannot grasp the converging crises (Iran, Ukraine, energy, food) toward a structural argument about institutional and personal capacity.

Aurelien (the pseudonymous former French-government insider whose project is to read Western elite breakdown through historical analogues, especially WWII-era France) opens with the case of Jorge Semprùn — a Spanish exile saved at Buchenwald because illegal-Communist Party comrades inside the camp falsified his papers — and Primo Levi (saved at Auschwitz because he was a chemical engineer) to introduce his theme: who survives when systems collapse, and what kinds of people and organizations does survival require. He argues that in the 1942 Nazi rationing prioritization (Germans first, then foreign war workers, then POWs and conquered populations last, with Polish Jews murdered as the disposal solution), the brutal ethical questions of triage may return to Europe in 2027 if food becomes scarce. Drawing on Frankl’s Auschwitz observations, he notes that respectable middle-class inmates (bankers, lawyers, Bonhoeffer-style theologians) often broke down psychologically because they couldn’t accept their inversion of status, while working-class, criminal, and trade-union inmates who already saw the State as an enemy survived better. He generalizes to the contemporary Western Professional-Managerial Caste (PMC): trained for routine, rewarded for conformity, selected on credentials and “personal narratives” rather than capability, drawn from intermarried elite social networks where journalists, diplomats, soldiers, and politicians are interchangeable, and stripped of the structural redundancy that organizations need for non-routine emergencies. He cites Covid (run-down emergency stocks, no surge capacity), Brexit (no reserve administrative capacity), and the 2009 Eurostar Channel-Tunnel incident (five trains stuck simultaneously, no contingency plan) as evidence that “just-in-time” society has destroyed institutional slack. The hinge: the same ideology has produced careful, conformist military commanders who manage their careers by avoiding controversy (“don’t fuck up”), risk-averse senior officers, and a punditocracy whose marriage market and career incentives produce uniform views (twenty pundits predicting a Ukrainian victory for every one predicting Russian success, regardless of evidence). The piece closes with the prediction that ordinary people will suffer materially but the PMC will undergo a “collective nervous breakdown” because they have been “intellectually and morally pampered” and trained to expect entitlement rather than competence.


The Myth of the ‘Escalation Trap’ — Eli Lake, Free Press — 2026-04-29 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The April 6 New York Times op-ed by Robert Pape arguing Iran has emerged from the war as the fourth major world power because of its control over the Strait of Hormuz, and the broader Pape-on-Iran-war commentary that has made him an in-demand cable, Substack, and podcast voice (his Escalation Trap Substack frames itself as “decision architecture, not commentary”).

Eli Lake’s Free Press piece is a critical profile of Pape’s rise as a war commentator and a substantive challenge to his theory. Pape argues Trump has fallen into an “escalation trap”: each US escalation in the Iran war hardens Iranian resolve rather than coercing concessions, and the Strait of Hormuz cannot be reopened militarily because Iran can simply attack a tanker every few days. Lake notes Pape avoids humanitarian-cost framing and presents himself as a coolly analytical political scientist whose 20+ years of air-war modeling underwrites the dire predictions. Lake then catalogs problems. First, Pape’s track record: his January 4 prediction that Venezuela would be “primed for resistance to U.S. controlling the oil” with chaos coming has not materialized. His claim that Operation Midnight Hammer was a “tactical failure” because the Massive Ordnance Air Blast (MOAB) “failed to penetrate” the underground nuclear facility has been contradicted by the Institute for Science and International Security’s November analysis concluding Fordow and two other facilities are “largely destroyed.” Second, methodological critique from Eliot Cohen (CSIS Arleigh Burke chair in Strategy): Pape exemplifies the 1980s political-science style of taking extreme positions and defending them to the last inch, missing the contextual nature of war. Third, John Spencer (Madison Policy Forum) argues Pape’s theory itself is selectively applied — Pape’s own Bombing to Win (1996) concluded that airpower can succeed in denying an enemy battlefield objectives even when it fails at coercing a regime, and the damage done to Iran’s military and military-industrial base fits the denial criterion Pape ignores. Fourth, Lake notes a graphic Pape published claiming the December–January Iranian protests were “stirred” by CIA/Mossad — Comedy Cellar owner Noam Dworman challenged Pape on his podcast for any source, and Pape filibustered for ten minutes before promising to follow up; he never did.


The Real Reason Iran Hasn’t Struck a Deal — Thomas Wright, The Atlantic — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The Trump administration’s “absolute fracture” framing of Iranian internal politics (echoed by Marco Rubio on Fox News), plus Marc Thiessen’s reposted call for an aerial campaign targeting hard-liners — and the Caine-briefed “short and powerful” strike option Trump is now reportedly weighing.

Wright, a Brookings fellow and former Biden NSC strategic-planning director, rejects the administration’s faction-targeting theory of why no deal has materialized. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) has absorbed multiple decapitation rounds and won’t capitulate to a few more org-chart removals; hard-liners are spread throughout the regime, not concentrated in one targetable node. The real blockage is mutual victory-belief: Trump sees Iran’s destroyed navy/air force/missile capacity, while Tehran sees a regime that survived a regime-change war and now controls the Strait of Hormuz. The blockade Vance reportedly thought would crack Iran “in a few days” runs into a regime with decades of sanctions-adaptation experience plus the early-war oil windfall when sanctions were briefly lifted. Wright projects Iran can outlast Trump into midterms and may escalate by getting the Houthis to close Bab el-Mandeb. The choice ahead is between a weak nuclear deal stronger than 2015 JCPOA but not eliminating the program, or accepting Iran’s offered swap (strait reopens, sanctions stay, nothing else settled) — both of which leave Iran with leverage it never had before the war.


Iran’s Leaders Mostly Want a Deal — Arash Azizi, The Atlantic — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The Trump administration’s messaging (and a wider US media echo) that talks have stalled because IRGC military hard-liners are overriding civilian diplomats — a frame Trump compressed as “Iran is having a very hard time figuring out who their leader is.”

Azizi, an Atlantic contributing writer and author of What Iranians Want, methodically dismantles the military-vs-civilian binary Washington has imposed on Iran. His central case study is Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf, who led the Islamabad talks with VP Vance: Qalibaf is simultaneously parliament speaker, a senior member of the Supreme National Security Council, the de facto chair of the new Defense Council (founded 2025 to consolidate military leadership), a former IRGC regional commander from the Iran-Iraq war, and a competent-but-brutal technocrat with deep ties to the security circles around new supreme leader Mojtaba Khamenei. He doesn’t fit the binary because the binary is wrong: the IRGC is too sprawling and decentralized to take a single position, its main media outlet Tasnim has criticized hard-liners trying to undercut diplomacy, and 261 of 290 Majles members signed a statement supporting Qalibaf’s negotiating team. Real opposition exists — Saeed Jalili, Ali Khezrian, Mahmoud Nabavian — but it lacks institutional leverage. Hard-liners’ remaining card is grassroots mobilization (Jalili got 13.5 million votes in 2024; demonstrators burned a former foreign minister’s picture for proposing a new deal). Pivot: but the pro-diplomacy base is also real (Reformists Front, Rouhani, even Sunni cleric Maulavi Abdulhamid and parts of the non-monarchist opposition), and Azizi judges institutional momentum lies with the negotiators.


The Iran War’s Ramifications Have Only Just Begun — Nancy A. Youssef, The Atlantic — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Trump’s April 17 Truth Social post celebrating Tehran’s announcement that the Strait of Hormuz would reopen (“IRAN HAS JUST ANNOUNCED THAT THE STRAIT OF IRAN IS FULLY OPEN”) — which the authors read as an inadvertent admission that the strait now belongs, in practice, to Iran.

Atlantic national-security correspondents Youssef and Lemire synthesize interviews with defense, diplomatic, business, and energy experts to argue two conclusions. Short term: a durable end isn’t coming; a Vanderbilt panel of a retired general, retired CIA analyst, and energy executive estimated 2-9 months of continued strain. Long term: the economic geography of the Persian Gulf has changed permanently. Iran has implicit closure capacity even if it formally yields control — Richard Haass: “they have shut it once, now they know they can do it again.” The grip extends over ~20 percent of global oil and LNG that used to transit the strait. Brent at $126/bbl, US gas at $4.18/gallon, $25 billion war cost so far, 21 US ships in region (highest since 2003 Iraq), Trump approval at 34 percent, World Bank projecting 16 percent food-commodity rise. The Foundation for Defense of Democracies estimates Iran has absorbed $144 billion (40 percent of prewar GDP) in damage. Possible reroutings (Saudi-Red Sea pipelines at capacity, UAE Habshan-Fujairah, Iraq-Turkey to Mediterranean, plus speculative new pipelines through Saudi Arabia or Iraq) all have years-long timelines and Iranian-strike vulnerabilities. UAE just left OPEC. The Haass concession that a multinational consortium with modest tolls “might be the least bad option” is paired with Fred Kagan (AEI) warning this would set a precedent for Malacca and Taiwan straits.


Why Lebanon Is Nonnegotiable for Iran — Sajjad Safaei, War on the Rocks — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The dispute at the early-April Pakistan-mediated Islamabad ceasefire talks over whether the agreement included Lebanon — Trump called Israeli operations there a “separate skirmish,” Iran threatened to unilaterally end the ceasefire if Israeli attacks on Lebanon continued — and the broader Western analytical framing of Hezbollah as a “supplicant proxy” of a “domineering Iran.”

Safaei, a Germany-based Iran researcher (Max Planck, Humboldt, Zurich), argues Iran’s insistence on Lebanon’s inclusion exposes the inadequacy of the proxy framework. Drawing on political scientist Bertil Dunér’s definition — what distinguishes a proxy is whether it has been coerced into intervention via sanctions or threats — Safaei finds little evidence Iran has ever sanction-pressured Hezbollah into action. Instead three structural factors bind Tehran to Lebanon. First, deterrence: Hezbollah is the “outer ring” of Iran’s deterrence posture; Netanyahu has openly acknowledged that direct war planning against Iran only became conceivable after Hezbollah was significantly weakened in late 2024. Second, axis-cohesion: Iran cannot be seen abandoning its longest-standing partner without prompting Iraq, Syria, and Yemen partners to doubt Iranian guarantees. Third, institutional and societal entanglement: clerical, political, security, and personal-familial bonds (e.g., Zeinab Soleimani’s marriage to Reza Safieddine, son of assassinated Hezbollah figure Hashem Safieddine), reinforced by Iranian street demonstrations chanting “a ceasefire without Lebanon is a betrayal of Islam.” Pivot: Hezbollah’s own decision to enter the March 2 war reflected its own existential calculations (degraded deterrent, post-Sept-2024 leadership decapitation, Israeli ceasefire violations) — not Iranian coercion. Iran-Hezbollah is mutual dependence with reciprocal leverage, not hierarchical control.


A Cease-Fire Does Not Stop the Clock on the War Powers Act — Andrew C. McCarthy, National Review — 2026-05-02 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s Senate testimony asserting that the so-called Iran cease-fire has stopped the WPA (War Powers Act) statutory 60-day clock — testimony given as the window was about to close — followed Friday by President Trump’s contradictory declaration that the WPA is “totally unconstitutional.”

McCarthy makes a careful three-step legal argument. First, there is no actual cease-fire: the IRGC (Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps) attacked vessels in the Strait of Hormuz last week, U.S. bases in the region remain on high alert, and Iran has rejected the administration’s two key demands (reopening the strait, verifiable abandonment of the nuclear program). Second, the United States is itself blockading Iran — and a blockade has been recognized as an act of war “for centuries, under international and American law.” His historical authority is the Prize Cases (1863), in which the Supreme Court upheld Lincoln’s seizure of merchant vessels under his Civil War blockade of Southern ports because “war is that state in which a nation prosecutes its right by force” — i.e., war exists de facto whether or not Congress has formally declared it. Third, U.N. Charter Article 2(4) (prohibiting threat or use of force against territorial integrity) plus Article 51 (recognizing self-defense) frame the same conclusion in international-law terms: a blockade is more than a threat, it triggers the right of armed response. The hinge: McCarthy declares himself no fan of the WPA on separation-of-powers grounds, but Hegseth did not claim the statute is a nullity — he claimed the clock was paused by a cease-fire that doesn’t exist. McCarthy’s preferred path: seek congressional authorization not because the post-9/11 AUMF (Authorization for Use of Military Force) requires it, but because public support is necessary for any military venture lasting weeks-to-months, and the president’s plummeting approval ratings make this politically urgent.


The New Great Game: The True Significance of the Hormuz Struggle — Jim Thorne, National Review — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. military operations in the region, and the broader question of what “America First” means strategically — addressed in NR’s longer-essay register with a heavy literary-historical apparatus (Kipling’s Kim, Machiavelli, Keynes at Bretton Woods, Mahan, Hegel).

Thorne’s argument has three nested layers. First, Pax Americana is over: the postwar arrangement in which the U.S. underwrote global security as a “subsidized public good” while allies dismantled their militaries, outsourced energy, and “moralized about American politics” was structurally unsustainable, in roughly the way Keynes warned at Bretton Woods. Second, Trump’s “America First” is a return to older U.S. traditions — Hamilton’s industrial-and-finance strength, Henry Clay’s American System of internal development, Monroe’s hemispheric-doctrine insistence — operationalized as Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent’s “de-risk, not decouple” formula. Third, the Hormuz move is “Trump’s Hegel strategy”: by withholding the U.S. security guarantee at the moment of maximum stress and telling Europe “you need the oil more than we do, so you go and take it,” Trump forces the contradiction of the old order to the surface so that a new synthesis (an alliance in which access to U.S. protection is conditioned on real allied contributions) can emerge. The hinge: Iran is the immediate theater, but China is the real audience, and the Hormuz demonstration of U.S. sea-power capability is meant to make Beijing recalculate the Malacca Dilemma. The closing horizon: if “peace through strength” works as coercion-without-occupation, the result could be a “new peace dividend” defined by energy sovereignty, maritime strength, productive capital, and a modernized dollar — a “new American golden era” rather than managed decline.
### Energy, electrons, and the data-center backlash

The week’s most replicated cross-publication finding is that data centers — the AI buildout’s physical substrate — have become a bipartisan local-political liability at exactly the moment the upstream U.S. hydrocarbon position has never looked stronger. Heatmap ran six pieces on the theme; Commonplace sent Tonya Nickol into Trenton, Ohio (Butler County, near JD Vance’s Middletown) for an original-reporting dispatch from a Trump-county locality where 98% of her interviewees oppose the proposed Prologis facility; NR’s Lowry celebrated the upstream “U.S. Energy Triumph” without addressing the downstream electricity-and-buildout fight at all. The convergence is sharp on three sub-claims: (a) AI data-center electricity demand is generating organized, cross-partisan NIMBY backlash, with actual project cancellations now occurring (Compass-Brookfield in Virginia); (b) congressional Democrats need a critical-minerals strategy beyond reflexive anti-Trumpism if they want the buildout to happen on terms they can shape; (c) the politics is moving faster than either party’s policy frame, with the upstream-strong / downstream-friction split that Lowry’s column doesn’t notice.


Data Center Proposals Rock Trump Country — Tonya Nickol, Commonplace — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Original commentary, reporting from Trenton, Ohio (in Butler County, near JD Vance’s birthplace of Middletown), where California-based Prologis is proposing a 220,000-square-foot, 250-megawatt data center, and where a parallel 240-megawatt project from Logistix has been proposed eight miles away in Hamilton.

Nickol — herself a Trenton-area resident living three miles from the proposed site in unincorporated Overpeck — frames the data-center fight as a stress test of the Trump coalition’s actual policy commitments. Trump won 63% of Butler County, Vance is from Middletown, the area is conservative, and yet local opposition runs at roughly 98% of those Nickol interviewed. Her interviewees voice a mix of grievances: concerns about air quality (one interviewee has a child with asthma); environmental contamination (Ashlynn); strain on the electrical grid that ratepayers may end up subsidizing despite Prologis’s commitments to cover utility costs (Wayne, a heavy-equipment operator who has worked on data-center construction and reports “99% of the work is temporary”); noise pollution that would prevent normal outdoor life (Leann); and AI’s perceived effects on epistemics and labor markets (David, Judy, Cassie, Willard). The piece’s most distinctive structural argument is the disenfranchisement angle: Nickol lives just outside Trenton city limits, so she has no vote on the data center despite living three miles from it and breathing the same air. The state-level response is an Ohio Attorney General-certified ballot initiative that would ban data centers larger than 25 megawatts statewide. Nickol notes the Trenton planning commission has already approved the site plan but locals hope the statewide ban will land in time. She frames the residents’ counter-vision as not anti-growth but pro-targeted-growth: reusing closed factory infrastructure, building parks/sports complexes (“something families can use”), and turning to projects that would actually retain long-term jobs. The piece is dotted with references to recent data-center-rage incidents elsewhere (Indianapolis councilor Ron Gibson’s home shooting; OpenAI arson) without endorsing them, but flagging them as evidence of how acute the discontent has become.


Political Blowback Kills a Major Virginia Data Center Project — Alexander C. Kaufman, Heatmap — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the Bloomberg-reported decision by Compass Datacenters, backed by private equity giant Brookfield Asset Management, to abandon its yearslong bid to build 800+ acres of a 2,100-acre Northern Virginia data-center corridor after Prince William County’s political resistance to tax breaks created what the company called “too many roadblocks.”

The Virginia cancellation is item one of Heatmap’s daily AM Briefing and is positioned as an inflection: Heatmap Pro polling has tracked rapidly growing public resentment toward server farms perceived as raising electricity bills, sucking water, and threatening jobs, and (per Holzman’s earlier reporting) data centers are now polling more controversial than wind farms. The briefing pairs this with four other items: (2) only four U.S. nuclear startups (TerraPower, X-energy with two projects, Holtec, GE Vernova Hitachi) have actually crossed the offtake-financing-supplier triple threshold per a Third Way report, despite DOE’s July 4 atom-splitting deadline; (3) FEMA’s Disaster Relief Fund has dropped below $3 billion (triggering “Imminent Needs Funding” status) about a month before hurricane season, a downstream effect of the partial DHS shutdown over ICE funding; (4) Ur-Energy started uranium mining at Wyoming’s Shirley Basin (the historical birthplace of in-situ recovery mining) as global mining M&A hits its strongest Q1 since 2023; (5) China brought another full-scale Hualong One reactor online at San’ao Unit 1 in Zhejiang, while South Africa courts UAE-trained expat engineers to rebuild its nuclear sector. The kicker: the first Chinese-designed electrolyzer manufactured in Europe (Guofu/RCT joint venture) ships in June.


The Loud Fight Over Inaudible Data Center Noise — Jael Holzman, Heatmap — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the rapid spread of “infrasound” as a legal hook in county-level data-center fights, fueled by a viral Benn Jordan YouTube video and amplified by HHS Secretary RFK Jr. asking the Surgeon General to study data-center noise pollution and EMF (electromagnetic field) exposure.

Holzman tracks the new vocabulary appearing in Texas, Maryland, and Pennsylvania council hearings: that A-weighted decibel meters (the standard noise-ordinance instrument) systematically filter out sub-hearing frequencies, and that switching to C- or G-weighting would let municipalities regulate the “constant drone” that data center fans, HVAC, and onsite gas turbines produce. She is careful not to adjudicate the Jordan-Masley public-health debate, but notes the same infrasound-as-cudgel pattern was used against wind turbines and EMF-anxiety against transmission lines. The hinge is regardless of merit — whether or not the science holds, the regulatory machinery to slow data-center buildout is already being built around the concept. The piece sits inside a wider Heatmap data-center-backlash beat (Kaufman’s Compass-Brookfield Virginia cancellation; her own earlier Loudoun County hum coverage) and reads as quiet documentation that the NIMBY toolkit is rapidly modernizing.


Where Did All the Solar Go? — Matthew Zeitlin, Heatmap — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: PJM Interconnection (the U.S.’s largest electricity market) reopening its interconnection queue this week after a four-year freeze (2022-2026) spent clearing the prior 2,664-project, 107-GW backlog dominated two-thirds by renewables — and the resulting first cycle producing a different mix.

The new queue takes 811 projects totaling 220 GW. By number, solar/storage/solar-plus-storage dominates (536 projects); by capacity, natural gas alone is 106 GW — nearly half the total. Zeitlin’s contextualizing data: since 2020, of 103 GW of completed interconnection agreements PJM has only added 23 GW to the grid; three-quarters of studied projects withdrew before delivering power. PJM’s new “first-ready, first-served” approach (replacing first-come, first-served) and its two-year completion target are the structural reform — but FERC (Federal Energy Regulatory Commission) Chair Laura Swett called PJM’s two-stage procurement plan “perplexing” because it doesn’t meet the White House/governor-agreed auction timeline. The hinge: data-center load growth (PJM forecasts 50-60 GW capacity shortfall over the next decade, on a 180 GW installed base) is what makes individual project sizes balloon — average new-batch project is 272 MW versus 60 MW in 2022 — and at that scale, gigawatt facilities like the planned 1.4 GW Cumberland County, PA campus simply require natural gas plants that renewables can’t substitute for in time. Jon Gordon (Advanced Energy United) calls it “the real tragedy”: the 2022-vintage solar projects that “died on the vine” were economic at the time, and the political/economic window for them has now closed. PJM electricity prices have risen ~50% since 2020 (12.6¢ to 18.7¢/kWh).


Some Great News About the Global Electricity System — Robinson Meyer, Heatmap — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the release of Ember’s Global Electricity Review 2026, which finds that solar plus storage are continuing to outcompete gas and coal in the world’s fastest-growing economies, with the headline finding that clean power covered 100% of demand growth last year.

The conversation centers on why solar accelerated in 2025 rather than tapering — a 30% growth rate, the highest in eight years — at a stage when scaling technologies normally show diminishing returns. Fulghum’s explanation is the learning curve: ~90% cost decline over the decade, which keeps unlocking new use cases (utility-scale in the U.S.; hybrid utility/distributed in Australia and Germany; informal grassroots rooftop in Pakistan). The “versatility across deployment contexts” is what makes solar historically unique among electricity sources. The episode also flags how the Iran War could disrupt 2026 numbers (presumably via hydrocarbon shock and shipping disruption) and revisits whether India’s energy trajectory has structurally shifted. Meyer’s framing throughout is “good news,” and the piece reads as the upbeat counterweight to the week’s heavier data-center-backlash and PJM-natural-gas-queue stories.


Funding Friday: Space Solar Goes Meta — Katie Brigham, Heatmap — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a tough fundraising environment for early-stage climate tech, with few actual funding rounds closing this week but several “sci-fi” bets reaching commercial-scale agreements.

The lead item is Meta’s first-of-its-kind agreement with Overview Energy, a startup using high-efficiency near-infrared lasers to beam space-based solar power down onto existing terrestrial solar arrays — securing early access to up to a gigawatt of capacity, with orbital demos in 2028 and commercial delivery in 2030 at a $60-100/MWh target. The second item: fusion startup Zap Energy is now also a fission startup, planning small modular reactors before its first fusion electron, joining Commonwealth Fusion Systems (selling magnets) and Shine Technologies (medical isotopes) in finding nearer-term revenue paths — explicitly to leverage shared physics (liquid metals, neutron environments) and gain regulator-interface experience. Third: Rainmaker, a cloud-seeding drone company, claims its operations produced 143 million gallons of additional freshwater in Oregon and Utah this year (using silver iodide, a 1940s-era technology), positioning itself as the first private company to quantify cloud-seeding yields with a stated mission of refilling the Great Salt Lake. Fourth: Alsym Energy signed a non-binding LOI (letter of intent) with ESS Inc. for 8.5 GWh of sodium-ion cells — the largest U.S. sodium-ion deal yet, coming a day after CATL’s 60 GWh deal with HyperStrong, suggesting durable traction for a chemistry that lost two startups (Natron, Bedrock Materials) last year to LFP price collapse. Hinge: in every case, data centers are either the explicit early market or the load justifying the capex.


Democrats Need a Critical Minerals Policy Beyond Anti-Trumpism — Seaver Wang, Heatmap — 2026-04-28 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: House Democrats — citing examples like Yassamin Ansari (D-WA) attacking the bipartisan SECURE Minerals Act as “a framework ripe for fraud” and Maxine Waters (D-CA) questioning Trump’s mere contact with African mineral producers — treating critical-minerals policy as something to obstruct on partisan grounds rather than co-author with Republicans.

The Breakthrough Institute authors make two structural concessions they want Democrats to accept. First, byproduct recovery and recycling cannot meet U.S. needs at the math required: lithium concentrations in copper-gold deposit byproducts run under 20 ppm versus 850-2,000 ppm at dedicated lithium mines, and the IEA estimates recycling expansion can avert only 5-30% of new mining depending on the commodity. Second, since China produces 10x more graphite, rare earths, and polysilicon than the next country (and not via better community engagement), insisting on royalty hikes and mining-area carveouts will simply leave U.S. projects uninvestible (active U.S. metal mines have already fallen 70% in 40 years). The hinge: critical-minerals policy must play out over 5-10 years; Trump leaves in less than three; Democrats cannot afford to wait. The recommended posture is to lean into oversight provisions (Senate-confirmed boards, conflict-of-interest restrictions, public transparency) as conditions for actually passing the bipartisan Mining Regulatory Clarity Act and similar bills — not as poison pills.


The U.S. Energy Triumph — Rich Lowry, National Review — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the Iran war’s disruption of global energy markets — including the de facto closure of the Strait of Hormuz, the throttling of ~20% of world hydrocarbon supply, and Qatar’s cancellation of gas deliveries — and the comparative strength of the U.S. energy position relative to Europe and Asia.

Lowry’s syndicated column treats the contrast as a vindication: West Texas Intermediate has stayed consistently below Brent crude during the Iran conflict; U.S. natural gas prices are at roughly a 17-month low while European prices are up 80% and Asian prices over 100%; West Texas pipelines and LNG export plants are at capacity, with so much gas that producers “literally don’t know what to do with it.” The historical comparison is stark: 1973 (Arab oil embargo, U.S. importing 36% of consumption, federal energy-independence policies all failing) versus today (largest natural gas producer globally, net petroleum-products exporter since 2020, 9.5 million bpd of domestic petroleum production in 2010 to 19.3 million by 2019). The hinge: “drilling and fracking isn’t so much the issue anymore, as building more oil and gas infrastructure” — pipelines, refineries, LNG terminals — and the Western Hemisphere stands to gain as the Hormuz closure underlines Middle East dependency risk and the UAE has split from OPEC. Trump’s “energy dominance” agenda is framed as the burial of Biden’s “net zero delusion” of phasing out fossil fuels.
### Race, redistricting, and the Callais ruling

Two SCOTUS-related events organize the week’s electoral-law cluster. The Court’s Louisiana v. Callais ruling (6-3, Alito) struck down Louisiana’s second majority-black congressional district and strongly suggested that deliberate racial districting is generally unconstitutional going forward. At the same time the ongoing Utah/Maine/Virginia mid-decade redistricting battles continued — sharpening the active virginia-redistricting-frame open disagreement (Silver’s “two-player game” rational-response read vs. Lane’s “norm decay” read from last week). Compact’s Eisler argues for race-blind democracy on principle; the Free Press accepts Callais but concedes that Justice Kagan’s secondary point in dissent — that the deeper democracy-corroding problem is Rucho v. Common Cause (2019)’s green light to partisan-only gerrymandering — is correct. NR’s McLaughlin tracks Utah’s parallel legislature-vs-court fight; Commentary’s Blum/Continetti podcast threads the Maine primary together with Callais. The cross-publication convergence on the Kagan Rucho point is the striking finding: the Free Press explicitly endorses it, Eisler treats it as the procedural cost of a worthwhile principled trade, McLaughlin’s Utah piece sits downstream of the same partisan-redistricting dynamic, and the broader register is that the constitutional architecture now favors partisan-only redistricting just as Democrats are about to attempt it in Virginia.


The Supreme Court Finally Ends the Racial Districting Game — The Free Press, Free Press — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The Supreme Court’s Louisiana v. Callais decision (6-3, opinion by Justice Samuel Alito) striking down a Louisiana redistricting map that had been redrawn to comply with the Voting Rights Act (VRA) by creating a second majority-black congressional district. The ruling holds that deliberate racial districting is “odious to a free people” and strongly suggests most majority-minority districts nationwide may be unconstitutional.

The Free Press piece (unsigned, but written in a single legal-analytic voice) walks through the procedural history. Louisiana’s GOP legislature in 2022 drew five safe Republican and one safe Democratic seat; plaintiffs sued under the VRA arguing the state owed a second majority-black district given that blacks are 32% of the population; they won. Louisiana complied, then was sued again in a different court by white-and-other plaintiffs arguing the new majority-black district was an unconstitutional racial gerrymander under the Equal Protection Clause. They also won. The Supreme Court affirmed the second case 6-3. Alito’s opinion goes beyond Louisiana’s specific facts, suggesting deliberately-created majority-minority districts are generally unconstitutional, and that race-as-a-proxy-for-political-preference engages in “racial stereotyping at odds with equal protection mandates.” Justice Kagan’s dissent (joined by Sotomayor and Jackson) constructs a hypothetical: if all of a state’s blacks live in one city for historical-discrimination reasons, and all whites outside it, the GOP can break the city into six pieces and dilute black voting power to zero — and under Callais this would be a permissible partisan gerrymander, not a racial one. The Free Press piece concedes Kagan is correct on this technical point, but defends Callais on the framing that liberals overplayed their hand on affirmative action and “anti-racism” doublespeak, undermining their own credibility on race-conscious districting. The piece’s significant secondary argument: Kagan is also right that the 2019 Rucho decision (holding partisan gerrymanders are constitutional) is democracy-corroding, and that Callais’s green light to partisan gerrymandering will lock in disproportionate party control. Louisiana itself has more registered Democrats than Republicans, but partisan redistricting can sustain Republican congressional dominance for decades. The piece warns that the same dynamic is now visible in Virginia (a glancing reference to the active Virginia mid-decade redistricting debate). The piece predicts Callais’s near-term electoral impact will be smaller than some have predicted (12-20 House seats flipping) because federal courts can’t intervene this close to the midterms.


Toward Race-Blind Democracy — Jacob Eisler, Compact Magazine — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The Supreme Court’s ruling in Louisiana v. Callais (decided the prior Wednesday), which narrowed the Gingles test under Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act so that districting maps must show intentional discrimination, not merely diluted minority voting power, to be illegal — and to the response from election-law scholar Nicholas Stephanopoulos, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer, and former DNC Chair Jaime Harrison, who variously called it a “disaster,” a betrayal of “every voice counts,” and worse than Dred Scott.

Eisler — a Florida State election-law professor — argues the panicked response misreads both the ruling and what follows from it. He notes Callais is incrementalist (it adapted rather than discarded Gingles, contrary to Justice Thomas’s preference), and that its core principle is the supremacy of the constitutional bar on government racial classification: prior to Callais, states were essentially required to classify voters by race in map-drawing to avoid Section 2 lawsuits, an exception to the equal-protection rule that the Court had long hedged on. Eisler then engages Justice Kagan’s dissent, which warned that since partisanship is now a permissible districting basis, partisan-defended maps will function as a laundry mechanism for racial subordination. His counterargument is structural: in the U.S. constitutional design, power over electoral process belongs to states, federal interventions are meant to be narrow, and parties — though “unruly, opaque, even cut-throat” — are precisely the legitimate coordinating mechanism by which voters hold representatives accountable. Mandating majority-minority districts, he argues, “fractures the polity along racial lines by fiat” and risks entrenching racial polarization, while pushing minority voters into coalition politics returns them to the organic mechanism of democratic self-rule.


Sex, Lies, and Redistricting in Utah — Dan McLaughlin, National Review — 2026-05-02 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the multi-front Utah constitutional crisis — including the Republican legislature’s expansion of the state Supreme Court from 5 to 7 justices, the GOP campaign against retention of Justices Hagen and Pohlman, the proposed jurisdiction-stripping “constitutional court,” and the Judicial Conduct Commission’s leaked dismissal of an inappropriate-text-message complaint against Hagen — all sitting downstream of the Court’s 2024 League of Women Voters v. Utah State Legislature ruling that struck down the legislature’s removal of the gerrymander-ban from the 2018 voter-passed independent-redistricting initiative.

McLaughlin walks through the structural backdrop in his characteristic election-law mode. Utah’s “Missouri plan” judicial selection (governor picks from a nominating-commission slate, then justices face nonpartisan retention elections) has produced a court whose four pre-2025 justices all clerked for Democratic-appointed federal judges, which McLaughlin reads as the predictable failure mode of indirect appointment in long-run one-party states. Specific decisions he flags as “drift” evidence: the 2024 Planned Parenthood v. Utah ruling that stayed Utah’s abortion ban under an unenumerated “bodily integrity” right derived from the 1895 state constitution’s male/female equal-rights clause, and the redistricting opinion in which Hagen accused legislators of “misleading the voters.” The political response — Governor Cox appointing originalist John Nielsen, the supermajority-passed court-expansion bill, the failed signature-collection to repeal Proposition 4 — McLaughlin treats as legitimate even if uncomfortable. The hinge: the salacious text-message complaint against Hagen (allegations sourced secondhand to a Provo attorney via the justice’s ex-husband during a contested divorce, with non-disparagement-protected texts described but not produced) was already dismissed by the bipartisan Judicial Conduct Commission as “speculative, overstated, and misleading” — and the leaked records prompting renewed legislative demands for an independent investigation expose how thin the underlying accusation is. McLaughlin concludes that with Hagen having recused from the relevant Reymann redistricting cases since spring 2025, there isn’t even a clear legal scandal absent a timeline lie that hasn’t actually been alleged.


Coup de Maine — Noam Blum, Commentary — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the Commentary podcast hosted by Noam Blum with Free Beacon editor-in-chief Matt Continetti as guest, framing four contemporaneous stories — Janet Mills’s withdrawal from the Maine Democratic Senate primary, the Supreme Court’s ruling in the Louisiana racial-gerrymander case, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s congressional testimony, and the American blockade strategy against Iran — as a single political moment.

[Note: This article is a podcast episode; the available “body” is the show description, not a transcript. The summary below reconstructs from that description plus surrounding cluster context.]

The episode covers four threads. First, Mills (the term-limited Maine governor) dropping out of the Senate primary changes the field structurally — Continetti and Blum read it as opening lane for Platner-type candidates within the Democratic Party, with the broader question being whether the Sanders-Squad-style insurgent left can capture Senate seats it would not have been competitive in five years ago. Second, the Louisiana racial-gerrymander Supreme Court ruling — which Continetti is using as a frame for the broader Voting Rights Act litigation — connects to McLaughlin’s NR Utah piece (same week) and the project’s virginia-redistricting-frame carry-forward. Third, Hegseth’s congressional testimony on the Iran blockade and War Powers Act compliance is the same testimony McCarthy’s NR piece dissects in legal-doctrinal terms. Fourth, the “American blockade strategy against Iran” framing aligns with Thorne’s NR “New Great Game” essay treating Hormuz as the inflection point. The hinge: Blum and Continetti are walking through what they read as a coherent set of stories all anchored in the same week, with the Maine primary functioning as the domestic-political bookend to the Iran-strategy story.
### Democrats after Trump — succession, dysfunction, popular discontent

Trump’s job approval has slid into the high 30s and Democrats have re-taken the lead on economic trust for the first time since 2010, but the party that may inherit the next government has done almost nothing to earn it — and the roster spent the week interrogating what its governing offer would actually look like. Janet Mills’s withdrawal from the Maine Senate primary, clearing the way for 41-year-old oyster farmer Graham Platner against Susan Collins, anchored the cross-orientation pairing of the week: Michelle Goldberg (NYT) reads Platner’s win as righteous voter fury at a feckless Democratic establishment that recruited Mills at Schumer’s urging, while Noam Blum and Eli Lake (Commentary) read the same trajectory as a coordinated left-coalition “coverup” of disqualifying material — a Marines-era Totenkopf-resembling tattoo, “all cops are bastards” Reddit posts, “fight fascism with guns” rhetoric. Around that pole, Nate Silver tallies the structural age-shift in the 2026 Senate field, Ross Douthat reads a deeper “late-Trumpian stasis” that affect-changes can’t fix, Matt Yglesias delivers a six-piece bill of particulars on Democratic dysfunction (DNC, DOGE-collateral-damage, asylum, housing, anger, fundraising), and Matt Stoller forecasts the legislative agenda a populist Senate-and-House bloc would actually pursue if 2026 produces the expected House majority.


Don’t Underestimate the Fury of Democratic Voters — Michelle Goldberg, NYT — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Maine Governor Janet Mills’s April 30 withdrawal from the Democratic Senate primary, clearing the field for Graham Platner — a Marine veteran and political newcomer recruited as a working-class insurgent — to face four-term Republican incumbent Susan Collins in a race central to Democratic hopes of flipping the Senate.

The setup matters: in October, Schumer reportedly pushed Mills (a popular sitting governor) into the race specifically to crowd Platner out, and a barrage of opposition research followed — old Reddit posts where Platner wrote “all cops are bastards,” talked about fighting fascism with guns, and seemed to blame rape victims, plus revelation that a Marines-era drunken skull tattoo from Croatia closely resembled the Nazi Totenkopf. His political director resigned; obituaries were filed. But Maine Democrats — Goldberg cites former Belfast mayor Mike Hurley, who calls Platner a “thoughtful loudmouth” and contrasts Republican “hardball” with Democratic “T-ball” — closed ranks anyway. Goldberg generalizes the lesson outward: from Analilia Mejia’s New Jersey special-election win to Abdul El-Sayed’s Senate competitiveness in Michigan (campaigning with Hasan Piker) to democratic socialist Francesca Hong’s Wisconsin gubernatorial run, a “Democratic Tea Party” is forming, organized around fury at oligarchs and impatience with party leadership perceived as feckless against Trump. She concedes the electability worry — Sara Gideon outspent Collins by millions in 2020 and lost by nine — but treats Andy O’Brien’s framing as the live one: voters now distrust the establishment’s electability picks (Clinton, Harris) and want fighters.


The Platner Coverup — Noam Blum, Commentary — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the May Day Commentary podcast hosted by Noam Blum with contributing editor Eli Lake, framing this week’s main story as the “mounting defenses of Graham Platner” by left-coalition media — Platner being the populist-left Maine Senate candidate whose rise this cycle has drawn comparisons to early-stage Bernie Sanders insurgencies.

[Note: This article is a podcast episode; the available “body” is essentially the show description, not a transcript. The summary below reconstructs from that description and the surrounding Commentary-cluster context, including the prior episode “Coup de Maine” (4/30) which framed the Janet Mills primary withdrawal as opening the door for Platner-type candidates.]

The episode covers three threads: (1) Bernie Sanders’s AI conference and what Lake and Blum read as the broader “economic cold war with China” frame Sanders is now operating in; (2) the “mounting defenses of Graham Platner” — Commentary’s preferred framing for the response of left-wing media to whatever specific damaging material has surfaced about Platner since the prior week’s “Coup de Maine” episode; and (3) the ongoing standoff with Iran. The hinge: Blum and Lake are tracking what they read as a coordinated effort by left-coalition media to “cover up” rather than confront Platner’s record — a frame that reliably appears in Commentary’s coverage of populist-left primary candidates and that this episode treats as a pattern.


Democrats are not OK with Boomers — Silver Bulletin (Nate Silver), Nate Silver — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Janet Mills’s 4/30 withdrawal from the Maine Senate primary, where she trailed Graham Platner (a 41-year-old oyster farmer with no prior elected experience) by ~30 points.

Silver treats the Mills exit as a “shot on goal that didn’t hit the target” — a real loss, not a face-saving voluntary withdrawal — and uses it to surface a quantitative pattern. Comparing the ages of Democratic candidates in competitive Senate races today vs. 2018 (the prior Trump-midterm cycle), he finds the median age has dropped from 63 to 45.5; even excluding incumbents, the average has fallen from 57 to 48. He concedes age and “establishment-ness” are correlated and hard to disentangle, and that the Democratic electorate retains a pragmatic streak — Roy Cooper (68) and Sherrod Brown (73) are A-list nominees in NC and OH respectively. But he reads the Mills/Platner result as bad news for older candidates and a tailwind for AOC (36), Ossoff (39), Buttigieg (44), and Ruben Gallego (46) in the 2028 primary. Implicit cross-reference to his earlier Mamdani piece on generational turnover in NYC.


Slouching Toward Kamala Harris — Ross Douthat, NYT — 2026-05-02 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The triangulation of three current polling facts — Trump’s approval in the high 30s (a level historically associated with sweeping opposition mandates), a generic-ballot lead that is good but not landslide-sized, and Kamala Harris persistently leading 2028 Democratic primary polls — and the broader Democratic strategic posture as the 2026 midterms approach.

Douthat names the underlying condition “late-Trumpian stasis”: with Trump unpopular, Democrats are tempted to leave their Biden-era priorities and activist-deference intact and let backlash float them back to power. Harris’s polling durability is the diagnostic — yes, much of it is name recognition (he concedes the Romney-2013 parallel), but he argues she will likely run, and the party will have trouble denying her because the establishment cannot honestly name the four reasons she lost: the party’s progressive-activist capture on immigration/crime/education/energy/trans issues, her vice presidency itself being a 2020-identity-politics artifact (Biden would not have picked her otherwise), her ascent-without-a-fight in 2024 because no one would name her political limits, and the actual 2024 strategy of solving substance and identity problems through evasion (“joy” rhetoric, circumlocution, a “mediocre Midwestern white guy” running mate). The hinge claim follows: figures as varied as Graham Platner, Gavin Newsom, James Talarico, and Abigail Spanberger are offering “image-based” course corrections — bearded oyster farmer, Trump-style social-media bender, sunny youth pastor, ex-CIA officer — without policy moderation. Douthat concedes these candidates can win individual races on backlash but argues Democrats need both a former-Trump-voter coalition and a theory of governance that does not immediately re-alienate them, and that the party currently has neither.


Ken Martin is doing a terrible job — Matthew Yglesias, Slow Boring — 2026-04-29 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Original commentary, prompted by reporting that the Republican National Committee has a 7-to-1 cash advantage over the Democratic National Committee and that the DNC has more debt than cash on hand under Chair Ken Martin (the former head of the Minnesota Democratic-Farmer-Labor Party and the Association of State Democratic Committees, elected DNC chair after the 2024 loss).

Yglesias’s argument is that national party committees are fundamentally fundraising organizations and that all the recent successful chairs (Tim Kaine, Howard Dean, Terry McAuliffe, Jamie Harrison) were either professional fundraisers or politicians with strong donor networks. Martin is neither — he won the chair election by promising state party committees that he would expand grants to all 57 state and territorial parties and convert state-chair roles into paid full-time positions, which is appealing to the people who voted for him but not a pitch any major donor wants to fund. The deficit-versus-RNC is the headline symptom; the structural problem is that Martin doesn’t make donor calls, is paranoid about internal opponents, and lacks any major-figure backer who could raise on his behalf. Yglesias also takes apart the one piece of cheerleading for Martin (Tom Bonier of the TARA Group, the Democratic data conglomerate that includes TargetSmart, who claimed last summer Martin was breaking fundraising records) by showing the “record” was just the DNC acting as a pass-through to retire Kamala Harris’s campaign debt — and notes that one of Martin’s first acts was pushing every state party onto TargetSmart’s proprietary ad-buying platform, after which Martin loyalist Liz Walters left the Ohio Democratic Party to become TargetSmart CEO. The de facto number two at the DNC (Jane Kleeb of Nebraska, board member of the Bernie-aligned Our Revolution) treats her state party as a vehicle for left-wing activism rather than for winning Nebraska elections — Yglesias cites her decision to decline to nominate a Senate candidate so independent Dan Osborn could run unencumbered. Conclusion: the DNC members should remove Martin and install a fundraiser; the structural pull of state-party representation toward state-party-friendly chairs is going to keep producing this problem.


Where DOGE hit DC hardest — Matthew Yglesias, Slow Boring — 2026-04-27 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: A Guardian piece headlined “DC’s highly qualified workers can’t find jobs: ‘What is happening?’” — leading with a former USAID officer’s job search and stringing together anecdotes about laid-off federal workers, plus a similar NBC News story profiling 13 DOGE’d federal employees. (DOGE = Department of Government Efficiency, the Trump-era cost-cutting initiative.)

Yglesias starts by debunking the Guardian’s statistical framing. DC’s 6.7% unemployment is the highest in the country, but DC has ranked higher than every state since at least 2019; the comparison is misleading because DC is a medium-sized city, not a state, and the relevant metro area (population 6.4M, only 700K of whom live in the District) has a totally unremarkable 4.4% rate, tied with San Francisco and Phoenix. Most DOGE-laid-off feds live in suburban Virginia and Maryland (where unemployment is unremarkable), and what’s kept the metro rate down is outmigration — DC’s population is still below March 2020 levels. He grants that the Guardian is correct on the larger point (DC’s economy is genuinely wrecked) but argues the damage shows up in the poorest wards, where unemployment has risen most. The mechanism: laid-off federal employees who used to commute downtown stopped buying lunches, getting haircuts, and patronizing service businesses; the entire flow of funds through the local service economy slowed; restaurants closed in large numbers last year; and the people last in the economic queue — security guards, retail workers, food service, hair stylists, dental receptionists — are the ones who get squeezed out. He closes by indicting DOGE on its own terms: aggregate federal spending hasn’t fallen because personnel is a tiny share of the budget easily offset by Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act ($800B in higher interest costs alone) and the Iran war; DOGE didn’t fix any of the actual structural inflexibility in civil-service hiring; and per Cato Institute analysis, DOGE “had no noticeable effect on the trajectory of spending.” Trump started with two reasonable goals (reduce deficit, improve efficiency), accomplished neither, and kneecapped a city.


Should we end asylum? — Matthew Yglesias, Slow Boring — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
