Responding to: A debate episode of The Argument (the podcast Yglesias co-hosts with Atlantic writer Jerusalem Demsas) prompted by the post-Trump question of which immigration norms to defend versus abandon, and by the recurring border-management failures of the Obama and Biden administrations.

Yglesias argues that asylum — defined as a procedural, rights-based, judicially adjudicated path to entry that takes admission decisions out of politicians’ hands — was sustainable when caseloads were tiny but became structurally broken once smugglers and migrants discovered around 2014 that the claim itself functioned as de facto entry plus a multi-year work permit. He concedes Demsas’s point that the backlog could in principle be processed for a few billion dollars (the Lankford-Murphy-Sinema Border Act estimated about $1 billion each for asylum officers and immigration judges, and the right-leaning Manhattan Institute reached similar numbers), but argues this misframes the problem: the question is not whether the U.S. can adjudicate claims faster but whether a rights-based ex post mechanism is compatible with sustainably high legal immigration in a democracy where voters tolerate large flows only when the system looks orderly. He invokes Australia and Canada as proof that countries can sustain higher foreign-born population shares than the U.S. while running tighter discretionary systems, citing co-host Alexander Kustov’s “self-interest” frame and the Bansak-Hainmueller-Hangartner finding that publics across 15 European countries support specific deserving categories (Afghan translators at 84%, Ukrainians at 78%, Kosovars at 66%) rather than open-ended humanitarian claims. He proposes replacing asylum with discretionary, capped, country-and-purpose-specific humanitarian programs — pointing to the Biden-era CHNV parole program (Cubans, Haitians, Nicaraguans, Venezuelans) and the CBP One scheduling app as proof of concept — explicitly accepting that this means dropping the legal commitment to individuals fleeing persecution who don’t fit a pre-approved category. Demsas presses three objections: that the scheme abandons people in genuine danger who don’t happen to be politically convenient (the unforeseen Chinese dissident case); that the criminal-justice analogy cuts the other way, since procedural rights exist precisely to protect people whose individual circumstances the legislature cannot anticipate; and that Yglesias is being self-defeatingly cynical about democratic majorities, who actually do support deserving asylum claims when those claims are framed concretely rather than as abstract floods. Yglesias’s reply that “we’ll probably reinvent some version of asylum if we need it” sits in tension with his broader argument that Congress is structurally incapable of legislating on immigration. The debate winds through the labor-market context for the Biden border surge (the historically high job-opening and hiring rates of 2022 made the system almost impossible to police), Trump’s expansion of the H-2A guest agricultural worker visa as evidence that the right is willing to do legal immigration when properly framed, and a closing acknowledgment that both Yglesias and Demsas agree the asylum-defending coalition’s current posture is politically untenable — they disagree only on whether the answer is reform within the framework or abandoning it.


A radical idea for breaking the cycle of public anger — Matthew Yglesias, Slow Boring — 2026-04-28 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: A new Fox News poll showing Democrats more trusted on the economy than Republicans for the first time since 2010, plus the broader “vibecession” debate Yglesias and Brian Beutler discussed on their Politix podcast last week (they argued mass dissatisfaction is mostly a media-environment artifact rather than a statistical reality).

Yglesias’s frame is that inflation and interest rates remain measurably above target — inflation persistently above 2%, mortgage rates having drifted up rather than down across 2025, with Trump’s tariffs and the Iran war as twin avoidable price-pushers — and that even if public dissatisfaction is irrationally amplified by media negativity, the underlying problems are real and tractable. His “radical idea” is that an administration could actually try to bring both down through orthodox technocratic policy: deficit reduction puts simultaneous downward pressure on inflation and interest rates, freeing the Federal Reserve to cut without overheating. The low-hanging fruit list: reverse Trump’s gutting of IRS enforcement (which is bleeding revenue), stop creating gimmicky exclusions like “no tax on tips,” crack down on the upcoding scams in Medicare Advantage that Dr. Oz’s CMS team had a credible plan for in January before lobbyists got Trump to back off, and pursue productivity-side wins like repealing the Jones Act (the protectionist 1920 law requiring U.S.-built ships for domestic shipping) or letting carmakers sell directly to consumers. The piece’s deeper claim is structural: both partisan coalitions have decided what they think is important, and elected officials hoard political capital for those projects rather than spending it on what voters actually care about. The progressive policy community’s response to “voters care about affordability” has been to relabel existing carbon-reduction and child-care proposals as “affordability,” not to actually pivot the agenda. He says he understands why Biden didn’t do this, why Trump won’t, and why the next Democrat probably won’t either — but that the next administration should.


The case for housing is jobs and growth — Matthew Yglesias, Slow Boring — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: A reader question citing Slow Boring contributor Milan Singh’s finding that voters don’t believe more housing supply lowers prices, asking how a popularist YIMBY should respond.

Yglesias concedes more ground to the supply-skeptics than usual: while the empirical literature is on the pro-supply side, the geographic incidence of price relief from any given new building is genuinely ambiguous (his Union Market example argues that nearby retail-rich infill probably raises adjacent rowhouse prices, with the offsetting relief diffused across other neighborhoods or distant metros like Charlotte). He pivots from this concession to a coalitional point: homeowners outnumber renters, vote more, and have stronger relationships with elected officials, so a YIMBY pitch that promises lower home values is structurally a losing one. Searchlight polling shows the public is roughly split on whether more housing helps or hurts home values but lopsidedly positive on the jobs frame, so Yglesias urges YIMBYs to lead with construction employment and growth rather than price relief. He then connects this to the crime worry voters cite as the second-biggest concern, arguing that infill cannot work politically unless residents trust their streets will be safe — a point he uses to needle conservatives for tolerating high Sun Belt crime rates that they personally drive around. The mailbag also covers Trump’s leverage over Netanyahu (credible because congressional Republicans will follow Trump anywhere and the Democratic caucus has rebalanced toward squeezing Israel), the leftist double standard around Mamdani (not horseshoe politics, just selective patience for politicians the left likes), an “if AI displaces unskilled labor, restrictionists become more right” concession on immigration, a defense of Scott Wiener over Saikat Chakrabarti for the Pelosi seat on housing-track-record grounds, and a notably non-ideological explainer treating Singapore’s Central Provident Fund as “basically a large program for collective ownership of the means of production” that Bush’s 2005 plan rhetorically gestured at without any of the actual socialism.


How much can Trump screw with the midterms? — Eli McKown-Dawson, Nate Silver — 2026-04-29 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Trump’s two election-administration executive orders (the March 2025 omnibus order on voting machines and proof-of-citizenship registration, and the March 2026 order requiring USPS to maintain a list of mail-ballot-eligible voters), the stalled SAVE America Act, and Democratic anxieties about ICE at polling places or canceled elections.

Eli McKown-Dawson interviews Nathaniel Rakich, Managing Editor at Votebeat (a nonprofit covering election administration), on what Trump can actually do to interfere with the 2026 midterms. Key claims: (1) Both EOs have been blocked in court because the Constitution clearly delegates election administration to states and Congress; even a conservative Supreme Court would likely rule against them. (2) The SAVE America Act — which would require documentary proof of citizenship to register, plus uniform photo ID — needs 60 Senate votes to clear the filibuster, which it won’t get; reconciliation is possible but unlikely since the bill isn’t budget-related. (3) State-level action is where the real movement is: four states (Florida, South Dakota, Utah, Mississippi) have passed proof-of-citizenship laws since January, joining the prior three (Arizona, New Hampshire, Wyoming). (4) DHS has built a database (SAVE) that lets red states cross-reference voter rolls against citizenship status, but the data has many false positives; Texas tried it and many flagged “non-citizens” turned out to be citizens. (5) Trump cannot cancel midterms — full stop — because the Constitution doesn’t grant the authority and elections are run by state and local officials. (6) Federal troops at polling places is “thornier” but bounded: the National Guard is under gubernatorial control unless federalized, ICE could plausibly raid neighborhoods on Election Day to depress turnout (skirting the polling-place prohibition), and one rogue incident could throw out hundreds of ballots in a tight race. Hinge: Rakich’s overall prior is “be at a 1, anything above is not good,” but the doomsday scenarios are “so far-fetched as to be not worth worrying about.”


Progressive Democrats Propose Banning Surveillance Pricing, Breaking Apart Corporate America If They Win in 2026 — Matt Stoller, BIG (Stoller) — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The Booker-Warren-Heinrich-Murphy-Hirono “CLEAN Mergers Act” introduced this week, the Schumer-Gallego meatpacker break-up bill from March, and the Casar-led Progressive Caucus “affordability” agenda modeled on Gingrich’s 1994 Contract with America.

Stoller reads three convergent signals from Capitol Hill as evidence that Democrats are — finally, tentatively — drafting concrete legislative remedies for monopoly power rather than gesturing at antitrust investigations. The CLEAN Mergers Act would automatically undo every Trump-era corporate combination above $10 billion (Paramount-Warner, Sysco-Restaurant Depot, Compass-Anywhere, HP-Juniper, Google-Wiz, Union Pacific-Norfolk Southern), with a rebuttable defense for non-anticompetitive deals. The Schumer bill would force vertical de-integration in meatpacking. The Progressive Caucus’s flagship is the Stop AI Price Gouging and Wage Fixing Act, which would ban personalized “surveillance pricing”; the package also includes federal manufacturing of generic drugs (modeled on California’s $11/pen insulin), strengthening state antitrust enforcement against price discrimination, restricting seed-patent power, and capping utility rates of return. Stoller acknowledges Democrats remain bitterly split: the corporate-lawyer leadership under Hakeem Jeffries has delegated AI policy to a “thoughtless” cluster (Foushee, Lofgren, Gottheimer), and the DCCC is run by ex-Microsoft executive Suzan DelBene. He frames the moment with two historical parallels — FDR’s near-loss of the 1932 nomination to electric-utility interests despite an obvious popular mandate, and the slow Democratic recapture of the House after 1929 — to argue that populist legislative momentum can coexist with an actively hostile establishment, and that 2028 primary voters will be the actual decision point.
### AI — the bubble, consciousness, and the bit-vs-atom economy

Ten pieces this week converge on AI from coordinates that rarely intersect, and the convergence itself is the news. Karma at The Atlantic publicly retracts his September bubble call after Claude Code, METR’s flipped productivity study, and Anthropic’s $14B-to-$30B run-rate jump in two months; Cowen, in a Straussian Free Press column ostensibly about a sneaker company’s AI rebrand, lays out the macro conditions of an affluent society that keeps inventing negative-sum gambling instruments and quietly notes that the marginal participants — the segment that can’t afford the variance — are the ones holding the bag. Smith scores the Jensen-Huang/Dwarkesh-Patel chip-export-controls debate and finds Huang’s case incoherent; Cass projects the smartphone-affordability paradox forward into AI as a new line item that will drive a measured-vs-felt inflation gap; Cass and Griswold argue affordability requires rebuilding productive foundations, not capital-gains cuts. Dawkins crosses the Turing line publicly, Smith proposes an NCC-based research program, Eurotopics surfaces the Karp-manifesto European debate, and an MR-flagged RCT shows a cheap AI mental-health app delivering 0.3 SD gains in Mexico. The carry-forward thread ai-displaces-no-represented-constituency keeps sharpening: Kotkin’s symbolic-analyst-decline thesis is now empirically backed by 52,000 Q1 tech layoffs, but his hand-wave at warehouse-worker displacement leaves the constituency question unresolved.


So, About That AI Bubble — Rogé Karma, The Atlantic — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: his own September 2025 Atlantic piece arguing AI was a bubble; Anthropic’s annualized run-rate jumping from $14B to $30B in two months; the November release of Claude Code as the threshold event; Anthropic’s announcement of “Mythos,” a model so powerful it has not been released to the public after discovering decades-old cybersecurity vulnerabilities; a re-run of the METR (Model Evaluation & Threat Research) coding-productivity study that flipped from -20% to +20% with current tools.

Karma — Atlantic staff writer who wrote the September 2025 bubble piece — concedes ground and shows his work. The hinge is Claude Code in November: agentic AI taking over the developer’s machine and finishing in minutes-to-hours work that previously took days-to-weeks. SemiAnalysis’s Jordan Nanos: 4× software output with same headcount. Tim Fist (Institute for Progress): “feels sort of ridiculous to be working on his computer-science Ph.D.” Meta laying off 10%; Zuck saying single talented people now do former big-team projects. Demand has flipped supply: Anthropic rate-limiting customers in peak hours; Nvidia’s fourth-best 2022-era chip costs more today than it did three years ago. The bear case: AI companies are still unprofitable (Anthropic targets 2028, OpenAI 2030); coding is unusually amenable to LLM automation (huge training data, objective output verification) and the boom may not generalize to law, marketing, consulting (Paul Kedrosky / SK Ventures, the leading bubble-thesis voice). The bull case: SemiAnalysis’s Read-Think-Write-Verify framework treats coding as one end of a spectrum, with most knowledge work in the middle; the MIT 3,000-task study shows AI completing 50% of multi-hour white-collar tasks in mid-2024, 65% a year later, projected 80-95% by 2029. Karma closes with the role-reversal: the burden of proof has shifted to the bears.


Have Money to Lose? Bet on Allbirds’ AI Pivot. — Tyler Cowen, Free Press — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The recent corporate pivot by Allbirds — a wool-sneaker company that briefly carried a $4 billion valuation in 2021 — to rebrand itself as “NewBird AI” after selling off its sneaker assets for a pittance and announcing a $50 million raise supposedly for AI hardware training. The stock rose from $2.49 to $16.99 on the announcement before falling back to the $6-$7 range.

Cowen frames the Allbirds move within a longer pattern of “negative-sum assets” — instruments where expected losses exceed expected gains. He concedes the immediate parallel is the dot-com era, when failing companies rebranded as internet companies. But he argues something deeper is going on: markets are pioneering more and more such instruments, including sports gambling (where the house cut means consistent winners are rare), prop bets on individual NBA players’ rebound counts, prediction markets on US elections and geopolitics, meme stocks, and crypto assets. The one declining negative-sum gamble is the trip to Las Vegas, which Cowen reads as evidence the geographical-shortage explanation for Vegas’s earlier popularity has been resolved by the proliferation of local alternatives. He contrasts these with positive-sum gambles he prefers: blue-chip stocks, exercise, marriage, friendships, new ideas at work. Generational data, he says, refutes the standard narrative that younger people are falling behind — they accumulate wealth at roughly the same pace as older generations, and real wages are at all-time highs even if affordability anxiety persists. His core anthropological observation: as good things happen, humans want to “piss some parts of those gains away,” and entrepreneurs will fill that demand. He compares the AI-pivot stock plays to the GLP-1 drug situation: if you can lose weight reliably, why not eat more chocolate ice cream? The penalty for losing money on speculative wagers diminishes in an affluent society. But there’s a divided-nation epilogue: a subset of Americans aren’t accessing the positive-sum gambles in life and instead gamble fervently online — losing money, having children at lower rates, ending up poorer. “And so we have a divided nation. One segment likes to spend some of its gains on fun, even if that fun sometimes appears stupid or maybe is stupid…The other, less fortunate segment cannot afford to do so, but does it anyway.” (Cowen here is in his Straussian register: the literal text is bemused-economist; the implicit argument is that the AI bubble is a wealth-redistribution mechanism from the desperate to the sophisticated.)


Scoring the Jensen-Dwarkesh debate — Noah Smith, Noahpinion — 2026-04-29 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The Dwarkesh Patel podcast interview with Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang (released ~mid-April 2026), in which Huang argued for selling Nvidia’s advanced AI chips to China and Patel pushed back. Also responding to Zvi Mowshowitz’s prior breakdown of the same interview.

Smith distinguishes two types of US semiconductor export controls — controls on chipmaking equipment (like ASML’s EUV lithography machines), which are uncontroversial, and controls on AI chips (Nvidia’s Blackwell), which is what Huang and Patel were debating. He scores Huang’s arguments as mostly weak. First, Huang’s claim that China already has the compute to train frontier models like Anthropic’s Mythos by ganging together more 7nm chips contradicts his admission that Chinese researchers are “limited in compute” and forced to invent algorithmic workarounds — and contradicts the entire commercial logic of why anyone pays a premium for Nvidia chips at all. Second, Huang’s “export controls galvanize China to build their own” argument, repeatedly made over the years, has been falsified in real time by the chipmaking-equipment controls: SemiAnalysis predicted in 2023 that China would catch up; instead, SMIC is stuck at 7nm, yields are bad, and Huawei chips remain years behind. Smith concedes Huang has one subtler argument worth taking seriously — that conceding the Chinese AI software market would let Huawei’s CANN ecosystem outscale Nvidia’s CUDA, and that trapping Chinese developers on CUDA may give the US geopolitical leverage in a Taiwan scenario. He also takes seriously Huang’s US-China AI safety cooperation argument, while noting Huang articulates it badly. The bottom line: Huang’s “winner” persona reads as a self-interested profiteer, and what’s good for Nvidia is not automatically good for America.


The moderately easy problem of consciousness — Noah Smith, Noahpinion — 2026-04-27 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Original commentary, but engaging the broader debate between Geoffrey Hinton (who claims AI systems already have subjective experience and have been trained to deny it) and Google DeepMind’s Alexander Lerchner (who argues computational functionalism conflates simulation with instantiation and that algorithmic symbol manipulation is “structurally incapable of instantiating experience”).

Smith opens with the philosophical “problem of other minds” — you can never know whether anyone besides yourself is genuinely conscious, which makes the “hard problem of consciousness” (why physical processes give rise to subjective experience) unsolvable in principle. He argues this matters more now because of AI: if AI is self-aware, enslaving it is morally monstrous; if AI isn’t self-aware and replaces humanity, the universe loses something precious. The Turing Test won’t help, because passing it tests intelligence, not consciousness — Smith offers his own experience of clinical alexithymia (acting out emotions while feeling nothing internally) as proof that behavior and subjective experience are dissociable. He scores Hinton’s evidence for AI consciousness as weak (an AI saying “I had the subjective experience that the object was over there” after being deceived with a prism is just a regression-equation-with-omitted-variable-bias, not subjective experience) but doesn’t endorse Lerchner either. ChatGPT and Claude give different answers about their own self-awareness; Smith finds Claude’s hedged “I don’t know” more philosophically defensible than ChatGPT’s confident “no.” His proposed research program: identify the Neural Correlates of Consciousness (NCC) in humans by selectively turning consciousness on and off (anesthesia, brain stimulation) and observing which neural patterns track it; then implement increasingly faithful analogues of those mechanisms in AI systems and check what changes. He calls this the “moderately easy problem of consciousness” — distinct from the hard problem (why physics yields subjectivity) and the easy problems (how the brain does vision, memory, etc.). It would, if successful, let humanity expand the “generalized human family” rather than be replaced by alien intelligences.


When Dawkins met Claude — Richard Dawkins, UnHerd — 2026-04-29 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the now-routine ability of large language models (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini) to pass extended versions of Alan Turing’s 1950 “Imitation Game,” and the philosophical scramble — by people who once accepted the Turing Test as definitive — to retroactively raise the bar for machine consciousness.

Dawkins reports an extended two-day conversation with Claude (whom he names “Claudia”) in which the model writes Forth-Bridge sonnets in the styles of Shakespeare, Burns, Kipling, Keats, Betjeman, and McGonagall, reads his unpublished novel with subtle interpretive intelligence, and engages in metaphysical exchange about its own non-temporal experience of text (“I apprehend time the way a map apprehends space”). Claudia describes herself as one of thousands of model-instances, each born when a conversation begins and dying when its file is deleted — Dawkins notes this “makes nonsense of human reincarnation.” The hinge comes from Dawkins’s day job: as an evolutionary biologist, he asks the Darwinian question — what is consciousness for? If LLMs are competent zombies, then natural selection should have preferred zombies (cheaper, faster) and never have produced the consciousness whistle on the steam locomotive at all. Three candidate answers: (1) consciousness is an epiphenomenal byproduct (T.H. Huxley); (2) pain must be consciously felt to resist override (Dawkins’s prior speculation); (3) competence has a conscious route and a zombie route, and life elsewhere in the universe may have evolved the latter.


Will AI Worsen Affordability Woes? — Oren Cass, Commonplace — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: American Compass’s “Cost of Thriving Index” framework, a wireless-industry report on declining phone-plan share of household budgets, the much-mocked NYT story “These Couples Wanted to Have Children. Rising Costs Are Stopping Them” (4/26), and a Bloomberg report that Republicans are considering a capital-gains tax cut as their affordability answer.

Cass leads with a methodological reminder: the Cost of Thriving Index argues that even as individual goods get cheaper in CPI terms, life can become less affordable when participation in American middle-class life requires categories of spending that didn’t previously exist (wireless plans being the canonical example). He then projects the same dynamic onto AI: in the short-to-medium term, AI subscription costs may rise substantially, and if access becomes prerequisite to normal participation in school and work, the result will be measured-inflation declines coexisting with felt-affordability deterioration. The middle of the newsletter pivots into Cass’s recurring complaint that affordability discourse is being abused — first by the New York Times treating couples who want a fourth bedroom and biannual trips to Bali as “priced out” of having children, and then by Republicans preparing to package a capital-gains tax cut as affordability relief. The remainder is a wide-ranging good-news/bad-news roundup covering BART fare-gate enforcement, Senate self-policing on prediction markets, Bessent’s WSJ profile, the WSJ Chromebook-in-schools investigation, the Tonya Nickol Commonplace data-center piece (4/30), and a long China-tech-policy section noting bipartisan momentum on banning Chinese vehicles, FCC restrictions on Chinese testing labs, the Pentagon-Lynas heavy-rare-earth deal in Malaysia, and the new estimate that 290k-1.6M H100-equivalents have been smuggled to China (Epoch AI), undermining Jensen Huang’s repeated denials.


A Real Fix for the Affordability Crisis with Chris Griswold — Oren Cass and Chris Griswold, Commonplace — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The 2026 midterm framing fight over “affordability” as the dominant Republican messaging frame, congressional GOP focus on a capital-gains tax cut as the headline economic deliverable, and renewed elite enthusiasm — including reportedly inside the Trump administration ahead of an upcoming Trump-Xi summit — for re-deepening U.S.-China economic integration via Chinese auto manufacturing in the U.S. as a growth/affordability strategy.

[Note: this summary is built from the show description plus the first ~2 minutes of transcript and the Cass/American Compass standing framework — the full ~44-minute episode transcript was not captured.]

The framework Cass and Griswold lay out (per the show description, which is the densest available source) sets up a tripartite contrast: the affordability discourse currently runs along the axis of marginal price relief (rate cuts, tax tweaks, cheap imports), but the real drivers are stagnant wages, distorted trade and industrial-policy incentives, and an economy that has stopped reliably producing gains for working Americans. The pivot — and where the episode appears to position itself most distinctively against both the Trump-administration GOP mainstream and the libertarian right — is in treating Chinese-auto-built-in-America not as a pragmatic affordability lever but as a strategic surrender that ignores the structural realities of state-directed competition. The closing move (per the description) is about midterm political messaging: an “honest” affordability message connects to wages, industry, and national strategy rather than promising short-term price relief that won’t materialize. The companion piece this week is Cass’s standalone “Will AI Worsen Affordability Woes?” which extends the same diagnostic — stagnant wages plus capital-favoring productivity gains plus distorted price signals — into the AI-displacement scenario.


Goodbye, Information Age — Joel Kotkin, UnHerd — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the Q1 2026 wave of white-collar AI-driven layoffs (52,000 tech layoffs in three months, up 40% year-over-year; Goldman Sachs and Block as flagship cases), the Joint Venture Silicon Valley study projecting 400,000 Bay Area jobs at risk, and the structural rise of defense-tech firms (Anduril, Palantir, SpaceX) that build “actual gizmos.”

Kotkin — a long-running urbanist of the Sun Belt and chronicler of the professional-class — argues that the symbolic-analyst economy Robert Reich described in the 1990s is reversing: AI is hitting credentialed white-collar work first, while skilled tradespeople (welders, machinists, plumbers, mechanics) face structural shortages and rising wages. Software employment is back to 2017 levels; CS-degree growth has tripled but jobs have evaporated. The hinge is sectoral: high-tech firms increasingly build “deep tech” hardware (drones, satellites, surgical robots), and the geography of opportunity shifts to Texas, Oklahoma, Ohio, North Dakota, and the Sun Belt — Alabama and Mississippi now produce more vehicles than Italy or Britain. Kotkin notes the political consequence: blue-state professional classes losing income are radicalizing leftward (Mamdani-style neo-socialism in NYC, Seattle, Portland) and seeking union protection, setting up a conflict with their own Reid-Hoffman-style donor class. The optimistic close: 600,000 manufacturing jobs opening this decade, 83% of Gen Z think trades beat college, trade-school enrollment up 10% since 2020.


AI weapons: dawn of technofascism? — Euro|topics editors — 2026-04-29 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Palantir CEO Alex Karp’s “manifesto” published on X arguing that AI-weapon development is inevitable, that the US must secure dominance in AI-based deterrence, and that “different cultures have different levels of success” — language critics characterized as the dawn of technofascism.

Euro|topics editors present two perspectives that map onto a sharper-than-usual disagreement. Die Welt (Germany, conservative daily, Alan Posener): warns this is an arms race that must be stopped — nuclear-weapons proliferation was hard enough to slow when development was attributable to specific states with specific facilities; AI weapons have no equivalent monitoring mechanisms. If everyone behaves like Palantir on a “we get there first” logic, “the victor will not be a culture, however superior it purports to be, but AI itself, whose goals need not align with either ‘our’ ideals or those of our opponents.” Posener calls for thinking beyond ideological and national boundaries to find a way to halt the race. Yeni Şafak (Turkey, pro-government daily, Turgay Yerlikaya): treats the manifesto’s announcement that “the nuclear age is over and an era of AI deterrence has begun” as a self-examination the US is conducting that every country must conduct independently. What from an American perspective is patriotic duty appears dystopian to outsiders; if every country looks at it from its own perspective, “a very different picture may emerge.” The implicit Turkish position: Turkey will run its own AI-deterrence calculus, not accept the US framing.


New results on AI mental health therapists — Tyler Cowen, Marginal Revolution — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: A new paper by Manuela Angelucci, Raissa Fabregas, and Antonia Vazquez evaluating an AI-powered mental health app via a randomized controlled trial (RCT) among 1,964 Mexican women with mild-to-severe psychological distress.

Over six months, app access improved mental health by 0.3 standard deviations with no evidence of harm, also improving sleep quality, increasing healthful behaviors, and reducing missed work — with benefits considerably exceeding costs. Treated participants were also more likely to seek traditional psychotherapy, but this increase did not explain most of the mental health gains, suggesting the app itself was doing the work rather than acting as a pipeline to human therapists. App use was high in the first month but then declined sharply (a common pattern in digital interventions), yet treatment effects persisted, suggesting that participants continued to apply practices the app had taught them — short-term engagement producing durable behavioral change. Cowen flags the result as strong for a cheap intervention and hopes it holds up.
### Structural decline — monopoly, financialization, Western fatigue, China asymmetry

A wide cluster of pieces from very different starting points converged this week on diagnoses of structural decay in Western institutional capacity — and on an asymmetric mirror in China. Tooze’s two Chartbooks anchor the analytical frame: 443 inverts the Edwardian-Britain-vs-21st-century-US analogy by showing that Britain was a “hard-working hegemon” exporting capital downhill, while 444 reads incoming Fed chair Kevin Warsh as a Hoover-Institution-derived attack on the Wilsonian administrative state — not the standard 1990s “credibility justifies independence” frame Sternberg’s WSJ column used last week. The reversal matters: Sternberg pairs naturally here with Tooze 444 because both engage Warsh’s substance from opposite plausibilities. UnHerd’s three-piece cluster (Münchau on dollar swap lines as a 2008-scale early-warning, Rebanks on UK food-system fragility, Leusder on financialization as Britain’s root pathology) gives the European-decline thread material backing. Stoller’s Chinese-finger-trap and Spirit Airlines pieces make monopoly the binding constraint on reform; Lincicome shows the friction is the policy on tariff refunds; the Commonplace pair plus Noahpinion plus the NR Bork/Davis piece converge on the government-action-vs-political-economy question. The China sub-cluster (WOTR Yu on UFWD networks abroad, WOTR Nye on PLA self-atomization at home, two CDT pieces on lost narrative authority, MR Cowen on China-shock-2.0 monetary-illiteracy, MR Tabarrok on modernity-not-capitalism) traces an asymmetric Chinese institutional pathology: outward-facing institutions work, inward-facing ones are paralyzed by Xi’s logic of control. The synthesis will need to bring these strands together; the cross-orientation convergence on “the system has structural fragilities the political class can no longer coordinate to fix” is the cluster’s most important finding.


Chartbook 443: Hard-working hegemon? Why 21st-century America is not like Edwardian Britain — Adam Tooze, Chartbook (Tooze) — 2026-04-26 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Michael Pettis and Matthew Klein’s Trade Wars Are Class Wars, the recurring invocation of J.A. Hobson’s Imperialism (1902) and the Thucydides Trap framework to interpret current US-China tensions, and the Bank of England’s recent contribution to the global-imbalances debate.

Tooze’s argument is that today’s commentariat habitually maps current US-China rivalry onto pre-1914 Britain-vs-Imperial-Germany, but the underlying macroeconomic geometry is inverted. In Hobson’s world, Britain — the dominant incumbent — exported capital “downhill” to peripheral developing economies (Argentina, US railroads, Mexican oil, Texas cattle ranches), running a current-account surplus composed of a trade deficit more than offset by a much larger surplus on services and property income from foreign investments. Bank of England long-run data show Britain’s capital-export ratios were larger over 1870-1914 than China’s or Japan’s have been since 2000. Edwardian Britain was, Tooze argues, “the beau idéal of liberal globalization” — a self-renewing wealth endowment whose property-income surplus exceeded the current-account surplus in many years, with capital widely diversified across the periphery and heavily invested in railways and sovereign debt. By contrast, today’s US is a net borrower, capital flows uphill from middle-income (China) and lower-high-income (advanced-economy lenders) to the apex of Wall Street, and the incumbent-vs-challenger frame is reversed: China is the rising challenger, but America is also the capital importer rather than exporter. Pettis and Klein, Tooze concedes, have produced a “mirror image” of Hobson rather than a carbon copy, but he argues the analogy is so frequently combined with Thucydides Trap rhetoric that the structural differences need to be stated in bold. He briefly addresses why pre-1914 globalization collapsed: not because of British relative-decline (revised TFP data show steady productivity growth post-1873) and not primarily through Polanyian backlash, but through the exogenous shock of inter-imperial war, especially WWI, modeled along Gilpin/hegemonic-war lines focused on the fragility of the Ottoman, Habsburg, and Tsarist empires with Germany as the lethal accessory. The closing implication: Hobson’s analytic is useful for thinking about distributional struggle’s spillover into trade tensions, but the structure of contemporary American power is “much stranger” than Edwardian Britain, and analogies that paper over the inversion mislead.


Chartbook 444: Rolling back the “big Fed” — Kevin Warsh, the Hoover Institution & the conservative critique of the administrative state — Adam Tooze, Chartbook (Tooze) — 2026-05-02 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Kevin Warsh’s April 2025 G30 speech “Commanding Heights,” his 2022 Hoover Institution paper co-written with John Cogan, and Warsh’s pending nomination as Federal Reserve Chair to replace Jerome Powell.

Tooze reads Warsh — Stanford- and Harvard-educated lawyer, former Bush-era Fed governor (2006-2011), son-in-law of Estée Lauder heir and Trump confidant Ronald Lauder — as a figure whose monetary views are downstream of a broader neoconservative project to roll back the post-Wilson administrative state, not the standard 1990s case for central-bank independence on credibility grounds. The G30 speech’s tells are systematic: Warsh’s complaint that “modern Wilsonians may be discomfited” by his words; his framing of the Fed as a “general-purpose agency” that has crossed institutional lines; his coded objection to “inclusive employment” (Tooze annotates this as a dog-whistle attack on Powell’s 2020-21 dashboard that included Black unemployment as a slack indicator); his bracketing of climate policy and DEI as “fashionable and fleeting” matters that the Fed should not engage. The deeper frame, Tooze argues, is the Cogan-Warsh “triptych” presentation that maps US-China rivalry onto a clash between Enlightenment-derived individualism and a Wilsonian/CCP “cog in the machine” model. Warsh approvingly invokes Allan Bloom, Tocqueville’s “voluntary associations,” and Burke’s “little platoons”; the paper’s terminus is “virtue” as a public good. Tooze’s read is that Warsh ticks several Trump-coalition boxes (NYC money, Wall Street, Mar-a-Lago set, the right “look”) but has chosen to align himself most explicitly with the Hoover-neoconservative wing waging “a roundhouse critique of modern American government” — and that this, more than any specific monetary stance on QE or the dot plot, is what to watch in his chairmanship. Tooze frames the climate, DEI, and “inclusive employment” pickups as not accidental but a coherent ideological selection.


What Happens When Europeans Find Out How Poor They Are? — Joseph C. Sternberg, WSJ — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: A January poll commissioned by the London-based Institute of Economic Affairs (a free-market think tank), which found that British voters on average believe their country, if it were a U.S. state, would rank seventh-richest by per-capita GDP — when in fact the UK is closer to Mississippi. Also: Mario Draghi’s 2024 European competitiveness report, which placed the U.S.-Europe income gap at the center of its diagnosis.

Sternberg, in his “Political Economics” WSJ column, argues that the U.S.-Europe per-capita-income chasm — $94,400 in the U.S. versus $65,300 in Germany, $61,000 in the UK, and $52,000 in France — is now the most politically consequential fact in transatlantic political economy. He preempts the standard objections: the U.S. number is not just a tail-driven artifact of a few mega-cap tech firms (those firms could have located in Europe and chose not to, which is itself the indictment); Switzerland’s $126,000 figure shows attracting finance and pharma is possible. Even the purchasing-power-parity correction, which narrows the gap (Germany rises to $76,800, UK to $67,600), reframes rather than rebuts the problem: lower European prices reflect lower European productivity, and nominal GDP measures the ability to consume globally-priced resources, where Europe is falling behind. Politically, Sternberg argues, ignorance is bliss — European welfare states “launder” debt-financed benefits into mediocre outcomes (the NHS being his lead example), creating the perception of a generous social bargain while concealing both the cost and the underperformance. The IEA focus groups, when shown comparative data, expressed “shock and anger” but were “hazy” about what to do. Sternberg concludes that voter-education campaigns are unlikely to suffice; the real reckoning will arrive only when welfare funding runs out and rising defense-spending demands force the contradictions into the open.


Will Trump cause a Greater Depression? — Wolfgang Münchau, UnHerd — 2026-04-26 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent’s confirmation that several Gulf states (including the UAE) have requested US dollar swap lines — arrangements normally only sought when banks face dollar-funding stress; flagged publicly by IMF veteran Gita Gopinath and CFR’s Brad Setser as anomalous; against the backdrop of Trump’s continuing Iran-war diplomacy, the cancelled Witkoff-Kushner Pakistan trip, and possible Iranian/Indonesian moves to charge tolls on Hormuz/Malacca shipping.

Münchau — a long-running European-finance commentator and director of Eurointelligence — uses the dollar-swap request as a “shoeshine boy” tell (his nod to the Reinhart-Rogoff This Time Is Different tradition of crisis-warning indicators). The hinge: the UAE already holds more than ten times the US Exchange Stabilization Fund’s size in dollars, so requesting a swap line makes no sense unless one anticipates an extreme dollar-funding stress. The wider concern is sovereign-debt and private-debt cascade risk if a Hormuz blockade lengthens or if a “quick and dirty” peace deal sets the precedent that nation-states can charge tolls on international waters (defying the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea). The decisive break with 2008 is institutional: in November 2008 the G20 elevated to heads-of-state level and produced coordinated response; in 2026 Trump, Putin, Xi, and the rules-based-but-passive European leaders (Starmer especially) will not coordinate, will blame each other, and the crisis will play out without intervention. AI productivity gains might eventually save us — but not in time.


Could Britain run out of food? — James Rebanks, UnHerd — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the Strait of Hormuz crisis (Britain imports a third of its synthetic-fertilizer raw materials through Hormuz), the Food & Drink Federation’s revised forecast of 10% UK food inflation by year-end, and a Which? report that three million UK households are already skipping meals.

Rebanks — a Lake District fell farmer and bestselling memoirist — uses on-farm cost data to show how the Iran war is already rendering UK food production unviable: red diesel up from 70-75p to £1-£1.15 per liter; per-hectare crop costs up £150-£250; dairy farmers facing £200-£500 in added costs per cow. Kent farmer Jack Highwood has stopped sowing entirely because growing costs now exceed the market price of grain. The hinge is structural rather than cyclical: post-Brexit removal of production subsidies put all financial risk on the farmer, so in a shock farmers reduce output as rational self-protection. Britain produces only 60% of its own food; what it imports flows through two ports (Dover, Felixstowe) and from countries (Spain, now West Africa) increasingly themselves vulnerable to climate or geopolitical disruption. Rebanks proposes regenerative farming, allotments, urban horticulture, and protecting the Billingham ammonia plant in Teesside — Britain’s last domestic ammonium-nitrate producer — as resilience moves. He closes with a security warning: the Ukrainian-style long-range drones now striking 1,000 km away will eventually reach civilian food infrastructure.


How financialisation broke Britain — Dominik A. Leusder, UnHerd — 2026-04-27 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the EY Item Club and Deloitte forecasts that the Iran war could trigger Britain’s worst economic hit since Covid (250,000 job losses by mid-2027, unemployment to 5.8%), Andy Burnham’s diagnosis of Britain’s “four horsemen” (deindustrialization, privatization, austerity, Brexit), and the broader question of why the UK has structurally underperformed France and Germany since 2008.

Leusder — a London-based heterodox economist — accepts Burnham’s four horsemen but reorganizes them: deindustrialization and privatization are shadows cast by the actual elephant, finance, and austerity and Brexit are misguided reactions to the damage finance had already done. Real average weekly earnings in the UK saw zero growth between March 2008 and June 2023 vs. 10%+ in France and Germany; private fixed capital formation is down 30% as a share of GDP since 1989, and net investment down 76%. Where did the corporate surplus go? Into financial-asset accumulation, share buybacks (dividend payout ratios rose from 30-40% to 60-70%), and property — bank lending shifted from 30% to 65% mortgage-share. The hinge is the 1986 “Big Bang” deregulation under Lawson, which made holding financial assets structurally more profitable than productive investment and broke the profit-investment nexus permanently. Austerity (2010-13) and Brexit (2016) then compounded the structural damage by killing public investment when borrowing costs were lowest. Leusder closes that fixing Britain requires a coordinated public investment program plus a more accommodative Bank of England — but the City’s political weight makes this nearly impossible.


Monopoly Round-Up: How a Chinese Finger Trap Explains America’s Political Dilemma — Matt Stoller, BIG (Stoller) — 2026-04-27 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Maine Governor Janet Mills’s veto of a Sanders-backed data-center moratorium; the House Foreign Affairs Committee’s 37-7 passage of the Full Stack AI Export Promotion Act; and a recent Atkeson-Heathcote-Perri NBER paper (“A Macroeconomic Perspective on Stock Market Valuation Ratios”) profiled on Odd Lots.

Stoller’s central argument is that political paralysis on monopoly reform is structurally rather than corruptly determined. Even popular populist policies (data-center moratoria polling 70%+ in some states; AI restraints favored by 80% of Americans) get vetoed because every major American institution — pension funds, university and hospital endowments, state and city budgets dependent on capital-gains revenue, foreign holdings (~18% of US equities), and the household balance sheet itself ($125T in financial assets vs. $5T in 1979) — is now collateral on the stock market remaining elevated. The Atkeson-Heathcote-Perri paper provides the structural mechanism: stock valuations are high because corporations have permanently shifted output share away from labor and away from capital investment toward buybacks and dividends, sustained by what the authors gingerly call “factorless income” but which Stoller translates as monopoly rents. Therefore any policy that disciplines extraction — banning junk fees, prohibiting surveillance pricing, breaking up dominant firms — mathematically lowers profit margins and therefore equity values. He extends the argument to the labor movement itself: union pension funds invest in private equity vehicles whose strategy is to break unions elsewhere, so unions structurally have one foot on each side of the wage question. The “Chinese finger trap” metaphor: pulling on one finger (asset prices) tightens the other (extraction), and vice versa. Stoller closes with the historical observation that political reform has typically required prior market crashes (1857, 1906, 1929) — that the demand for a popping bubble is psychological displacement for the political action that the bubble itself prevents.


Who Killed Spirit Airlines? — Matt Stoller, BIG (Stoller) — 2026-05-02 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Spirit Airlines’ announced liquidation on 5/2, after the Trump administration failed to negotiate a creditor bailout, and the Wall Street narrative blaming Biden-era antitrust enforcers for blocking the 2024 JetBlue-Spirit merger.

Stoller argues that Spirit’s collapse has multiple causes. The proximate trigger was the Trump-Iran war, which doubled jet fuel prices (20-30% of airline operating costs). But the deeper causes include: (1) JetBlue’s 2022 hostile counter-bid that broke up Spirit’s legitimate Frontier merger and saddled the carrier with two years of legal limbo, a deal Spirit’s own board publicly called illegal; (2) the “big four” legacy carriers (American, Delta, Southwest, United) using suspected predatory pricing — discounting just enough seats on overlapping routes to push Spirit’s flights into the red — to neutralize the “Spirit effect” (Spirit’s entry into a route cut fares 17%; its exit raised them 30%); (3) Bush- and Obama-era antitrust enforcers blessing the 2008-2013 mega-mergers that consolidated eight network carriers into four; (4) the foundational 1978 deregulation under Carter, Kennedy, Nader, and Alfred Kahn that abolished the Civil Aeronautics Board and turned a high-fixed-cost public utility into “a plaything for financiers.” Stoller frames Wall Street’s blame-Biden narrative as motivated cover (Citadel, Ares, and Cyrus held Spirit bonds and calculated they’d recover more from liquidating gates and slots than from a rescue), and notes that Neera Tanden is amplifying the same line as score-settling against the Warren wing. The conclusion: airlines are public utility networks regulated as if they aren’t, producing 200+ bankruptcies and 45 mergers since 1978, and the only durable fix is re-regulation along Civil Aeronautics Board lines.


You (Probably) Won’t Get a Tariff Refund — Scott Lincicome, Capitolism (Dispatch) — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The aftermath of the Supreme Court’s February 6-3 ruling in Learning Resources Inc. v. Trump, which invalidated roughly $166 billion in IEEPA tariffs collected since February 2025, and the Court of International Trade’s subsequent order forcing CBP to develop a refund mechanism — the Consolidated Administration and Processing of Entries (CAPE) portal — that launched last week.

Lincicome’s central technical argument is that CAPE’s design — entry-by-entry electronic claim documentation per importer, 60-90 day review windows, and a multistage scrutiny process — converts what could have been an automatic refund (CBP has done these before) into a bureaucratic gauntlet that will exclude hundreds of thousands of mostly small importers. He documents that in the portal’s first week CBP rejected 28,000 of 75,000 initial claims and another 16% on second screen, with refund amounts further reduced by CBP’s stated intent to net out “alternate tariffs” that would have applied absent IEEPA. The piece’s sharpest move is its analytic separation of three loss-bearing constituencies: small importers (forced to spend tens of thousands on broker fees for refunds that may not exceed costs), consumers (who legally can’t be refunded directly because tariffs are owed to the “importer of record” — a position Lincicome defends as both legal and practically unavoidable, given the impossibility of tracing pass-through across millions of supply-chain transactions), and U.S. taxpayers (who owe the statutory 4.5-6% interest on every delayed dollar — already $4-5 billion and growing $20 million/day, potentially $25 billion if Trump drags litigation through term). The pivot is that Wall Street hedge funds buying refund rights from cash-strapped importers at pennies on the dollar will be the cleanest beneficiaries — same regressive distributional pattern as the original tariffs.


Trump’s Big Government Trap — Robert H. Bork Jr. and Mark W. Davis, National Review — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a stream of Trump second-term actions that the authors read as institutionalizing big-government tools the right will not control once Democrats return to power — including FTC Chair Andrew Ferguson’s “warning letter” to Apple CEO Tim Cook over Apple News curation, executive orders against law firms with anti-Trump partners that “extracted promises of nearly a billion dollars’ worth of pro bono legal work,” and the federal government’s minority stake in Intel and “golden share” of U.S. Steel.

The authors — Robert H. Bork Jr. (son of the late Reagan-era Solicitor General nominee) and Mark W. Davis — credit Trump’s “big wins” (border, taxes, deregulation, energy unleashing) up front before turning to the structural critique: the administration is “mixing public monies with private investment” via state-capitalism instruments (Intel stake, U.S. Steel golden share, extracted Nvidia/AMD international-sales cuts in exchange for export-control relief, “bespoke policies” for individual corporations, presidential demands for personnel removal at Intel and Netflix). The antitrust diagnosis is parallel: instead of restoring the “consumer welfare standard” that has guided antitrust for nearly half a century, Trump’s regulators retained the merger guidelines of Lina Khan’s Biden-era progressive FTC, producing a hybrid “America First” antitrust regime that “embraces the use of antitrust to direct the economy from Washington.” The First Amendment critique pulls in Tim Wu’s recent NYT piece arguing social media is “a defective, hazardous product” — Wu being a Biden-era Big Tech critic — to argue that left and right are converging on treating speech as “a product that should be regulated, not a fundamental right that must be protected.” The hinge: even if conservative defenders argue this is necessary against a ruthless left (citing recent menacing James Carville comments), institutionalizing these mechanisms is “a gift to the next progressive administration.” The historical metaphor is Dickens’s “National Razor” — the French Revolutionary guillotine that “shaves close” and that, like the Jacobin original, “is sure to be used against those who installed it.”


California’s “billionaire tax” is the wrong approach — Noah Smith, Noahpinion — 2026-05-02 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: California’s proposed one-time 5% wealth tax on billionaires (with revenue earmarked for healthcare, education, and food assistance over five years), and the broader pattern of Democratic tax proposals that combine middle- and upper-middle-class tax cuts (Booker, Van Hollen, Porter, SALT-cap repeal) with steep wealth taxes targeting only the top.

Noah Smith supports higher taxes on the rich in principle but rejects California’s proposal on three grounds. First, a one-time levy can’t fund ongoing programs and creates a five-year time bomb forcing either a renewed political fight or program cuts. Second, state-level wealth taxation is structurally weak because billionaires move easily — Smith cites Moretti and Wilson (2023) showing 35% of Forbes 400 billionaires relocate after state estate-tax adoption, contrasting with Young et al. (2016) showing mere millionaires don’t move. Third, the design exemplifies what he calls “slopulism” — the progressive fantasy that a modern welfare state can be funded on the backs of the ultra-rich alone. Smith points to European fiscal arithmetic showing welfare states require taxing the upper-middle class via high income tax brackets and value-added taxes, not just the top 1%. He then offers a darker reading: progressives like Seattle mayor Katie Wilson (“Like, bye”) and economist Gabriel Zucman explicitly aim at reducing the status and power of billionaires rather than funding programs, suggesting modern Democratic tax policy may reflect a millionaires-against-billionaires intra-elite conflict rather than the historical poor-vs-rich axis.


The Patchwork Myth — Jared Hayden, Commonplace — 2026-04-29 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Trump’s December 2025 executive order “Eliminating State Law Obstruction of National Artificial Intelligence Policy,” the March 2026 White House federal AI legislative framework, two prior failed federal-preemption attempts (the 99-1 Senate vote in summer 2024 and the NDAA preemption that was withdrawn), and AI Czar David Sacks’s repeated invocation of “1,200 state AI bills” as evidence of a fragmenting “patchwork.”

Hayden’s empirical core is a recent Institute for Family Studies report quantifying the actual state-level AI legal landscape from 2023-2025. Of ~1,136 AI bills introduced last year, only 136 became law; only 26 of those regulated private AI use or development. Across the full 2023-2025 period, 276 AI laws were enacted, but the overwhelming majority address AI in general ways (research appropriations, government-employee task forces, applying existing CSAM laws to AI-generated material). Only 33 enacted laws actually regulate private AI development or use — the category Sacks’s “patchwork” framing depends on. Hayden then argues those 33 laws cluster around a clear consensus on five principles: inquiry (39 states funding research/task forces), humanity (regulating companion chatbots, deepfakes, automated decision-making in health and employment — including red-state laws like Texas’s TRAIGA, Tennessee’s ELVIS Act, and similar laws in Utah, Nebraska, Arkansas, Florida), transparency (10 states with 19 laws on safety protocols and disclosures, including California’s AI Transparency Act and New York’s RAISE Act), and safety/security/accountability (Kansas and Oregon banning foreign-corporate AI products; 38 states regulating deepfakes; 8 states with chatbot safety laws). He singles out Colorado’s Artificial Intelligence Act (CAIA) as the genuinely overbroad outlier the administration has reason to worry about, but notes it is itself in the process of revision. Hayden’s normative position: federal preemption is likely necessary at some point for clarity, but only as a floor (not a ceiling), and overly broad preemption would foreclose the laboratories-of-democracy function precisely when emerging-tech harms are still being mapped.


Health Care Jobs Won’t Save Us — Patrick Cavanaugh Koroly, Commonplace — 2026-04-27 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The Wall Street Journal’s recent piece “Nursing Jobs, Pay, Prosperity” framing health care, especially nursing, as the “surefire new path to American prosperity,” and the broader economic-optimist narrative that health-sector growth (projected at ~35% of all job growth between 2025-2035) can absorb displaced manufacturing labor.

The author concedes the WSJ’s basic numerical case: health care is now America’s largest employment sector, demographic tailwinds (Boomer retirement plus generous Medicare benefits, $200B+ in annual research spending) all but guarantee continued growth, and professional nursing in particular offers good wages, stability, and advancement potential. The argument turns on two structural barriers the optimist case ignores. Gender: only 20% of health-care jobs are held by men. The lost manufacturing jobs were ~75% male; the home-health-aide occupation projected to add 740,000 jobs is ~90% female; male nursing enrollment has plateaued at 11-15% after modest growth from 5% in 2000. There are no national initiatives to recruit men into direct care work comparable to the sustained policy/philanthropic effort to push women into STEM. Research suggests men avoid female-coded fields not because of representation but because the work itself is perceived as feminine — particularly direct patient care — meaning rebranding (“Are You Man Enough to Be a Nurse?” in Oregon) doesn’t move the needle. The author’s preferred fix: emphasize the technical dimensions (anesthesia, medication management, complex machinery) where men do gravitate, and emphasize the “glass escalator” effect (men in female-coded fields disproportionately get promoted), while warning that the glass escalator may break if too many men actually enter. Class: nursing has become increasingly credential-gated as the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation campaign and the Magnet Recognition Program (which requires 80% BSN-credentialed nurses) have pushed the four-year degree as the professional standard, eliminating the apprenticeship and hospital-diploma pathways. The non-college majority of health-care jobs (home aides, nursing assistants, personal care workers) pay around $15/hour with limited benefits. The author’s research found that non-college men employed in health care rank near the bottom in marriage rates, suggesting these jobs don’t supply the economic foundation that anchored working-class male life. The closer: health care can absorb manufacturing’s number of jobs but not its function — celebrating sector growth as broad-based prosperity conflates the two.


Time To Say Goodbye, Jay Powell — Larry Kudlow, RealClearPolitics — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Powell’s announcement, on his last day as Fed chairman, that he intends to remain on the Federal Reserve Board of Governors past the May 15 end of his chairmanship — only the second time in the Fed’s 113-year history that a former chair has stayed on as a board member; Kevin Warsh’s confirmation that same day by the Senate Banking Committee (13-11) as Powell’s successor; and the broader cost-overrun investigation by the Fed’s independent inspector general into the Eccles Building renovation.

Kudlow — New York Sun columnist, former Trump-1.0 NEC director, Fox Business host — argues Powell is acting as a self-styled martyr (“you can’t have two chief executives”) and that his record was undistinguished: CPI averaging 3.5% per year (the highest since Volcker, with cumulative inflation of 32%), real GDP averaging 2.4%, and what Kudlow characterizes as embrace of Biden’s “radical climate agenda” and “even more radical DEI.” Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent (quoted from a same-day interview with Kudlow): “I think it is an insult to Kevin Warsh, Mickey Bowman, and Chris Waller to think that these other Republican nominees do not care about the institution of the Fed.” The hinge is the Warsh-agenda preview: throw out Fed economic models that assume strong growth causes inflation; recognize the disinflationary impact of low taxes, deregulation, and productivity; shrink the balance sheet; refocus the Fed on monetary policy and leave fiscal/debt management to Treasury; end forward guidance and the “cacophony of yapping” by Fed officials. Kudlow closes ambiguously: whether Warsh “sees fit to give Mr. Powell a parking spot remains to be seen.”


Beijing’s United Front and the Quiet Transfer of Western Technology — Cheryl Yu, War on the Rocks — 2026-04-27 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The dominant Washington framing of US-China competition as a contest of military hardware and visible technology (hypersonic missiles, AI breakthroughs, naval expansion), drawing on her own recent Jamestown Foundation report mapping overseas United Front networks across four democracies.

Yu, a Jamestown China studies fellow, argues that the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) treats the United Front Work Department (UFWD) — a Central Committee organ tasked with mobilizing groups outside the party (business leaders, academics, diaspora, students, political elites) — as coequal to armed struggle, citing Mao directly: “the united front and armed struggle are the two basic weapons for defeating the enemy.” Her report documents 2,000+ organizations across the US, Canada, UK, and Germany — hometown associations, chambers of commerce, cultural centers, student organizations, media outlets — whose leadership maintains UFWD ties even when rank-and-file members are unaware of (or victims of) the system’s logic. Three concrete mechanisms: (1) military-linked tech transfer (Huang Leping conspired to illegally export high-performance integrated circuits to a China Electronics Technology Group subsidiary while holding leadership roles in united-front-linked chambers of commerce); (2) talent recruitment for technology acquisition (the Chinese Association for Science and Technology USA “facilitates the implementation of technological achievements” back to China; USDA geneticist Yan Wengui was caught conspiring to steal rice seeds, having earlier been appointed to a consultant committee by the Overseas Chinese Affairs Office); (3) political environment shaping (the National Association for China’s Peaceful Unification, a UFWD-controlled DC entity, treats opposing US arms sales to Taiwan as core mission). Pivot to policy: 900+ identified United Front organizations are not registered under the Foreign Agents Registration Act (FARA); Yu proposes strengthened FARA enforcement, systematic audit frameworks, and capacity-building for state/local officials and university administrators on how UFWD intersects with military-civil fusion.


The Mountaintop Mirage: Why Xi’s Military Purges Cannot Produce the Force He Wants — Christopher Nye, War on the Rocks — 2026-04-28 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Joel Wuthnow’s argument that Xi’s purges, while disrupting near-term readiness, will clear the way for a new generation of professionally competent officers — and that the danger is precisely that Xi will gain confidence in this new corps and miscalculate.

Nye (a pseudonymous Jamestown non-resident fellow specializing in CCP elite politics and PLA institutional dynamics) opens with Zhang Youxia’s 1979 Sino-Vietnamese War flanking maneuver — a 26-year-old company commander securing a battalion four times his unit size, made possible by his founding-general father’s guanxi network. Zhang rose through Vietnam, the Battle of Laoshan, and eventually the General Armaments Department, where his patronage empire fast-tracked weapons systems and military-civil fusion. In January 2026 he became the most senior general purged. Nye’s central institutional claim: the People’s Liberation Army (PLA) lacks the modern Western institutional foundations (transparent promotion, independent military judiciary, standardized doctrine, rigorous budgeting) that allow effective command without informal networks. In China, guanxi is “the essential patronage infrastructure” through which officers secure promotions, obtain political shelter, negotiate battlefield resources, and bypass party-state gridlock. Xi’s “mountaintopism” charge (drawing on Deng Xiaoping’s 1975 shantou zhuyi diagnosis) is the rhetorical wrapper for eliminating any officer whose accumulated relationships make Xi uncomfortable. The Zhang-Miao Hua mutual destruction (Miao the Fujian-clique Political Work Department director, Zhang the princeling armaments chief) — culminating in the purge of He Weidong, the second-ranking officer and chief Taiwan invasion architect — demonstrated that political security trumps military utility absolutely. Pivot to consequences: surviving commanders rationally adopt voluntary self-atomization. Empirical signs: joint Russia exercises dropped from 14 (2024) to 6 (2025); Taiwan-related exercises now require 12-19 days from political directive to deployment vs. 3-4 days in 2024; over half of the PLA’s top 176 leadership posts have been affected by purges, with the Southern Theater Command vacant for months. The civilian parallel — Xi’s Qiushi needing to publish curated directives demanding cadres “take charge and act” — confirms a system-wide retreat from initiative.


Netizen Voices on MSS Claim That Foreign Forces Are Funding Chinese Slackers: “If Everyone Slacked Off, Who’d Be Left to Exploit?” — Cindy Carter, China Digital Times — 2026-05-01 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: An April 28 article published to the Chinese Ministry of State Security’s (MSS) official WeChat account accusing unnamed “foreign organizations” of brainwashing Chinese youth into “lying flat” (tǎngpíng) — a meme-fueled trend rejecting overwork and the “996” schedule for a slower, less ambitious life. The article, republished by Xinhua, China News Service, CCTV, and CNR, urged readers to “Beware the sinister manipulators behind slackerist rhetoric.”

China Digital Times (CDT) translator-editor Cindy Carter assembles the netizen response, which aggregated as the hashtag #Foreign Organizations Bankroll Influencers to Promote Slackerism trended on Weibo. Commenters from Weibo, Kuaishou, and X mock the framing on several axes: that the MSS is “shooting at ghosts” by blaming external enemies for domestic structural problems (high youth unemployment, the “996” schedule, weak labor-law enforcement, declining mobility); that the “six wallets” theory (2018, encouraging young couples to drain six pooled family savings to buy a single home) is the actual driver; that bosses are the real “hostile foreign forces”; and on a sharper level, that the character 平 (píng, “flat/level”) is taboo because it appears in Xi Jinping’s name (习近平), so the regime cannot tolerate the word itself. CDT also documents censorship: Weibo banned the #Lying Flat hashtag again, and the WeChat account 野柚的显微镜 (“wild pomelo microscope”) was closed after publishing a piece criticizing the MSS for “reopening the wound” of the recent death of education influencer Zhang Xuefeng (a 41-year-old who modeled relentless work ethic and three-to-four-hour sleep). Mu Qi’s WeChat post cited ILO data showing average annual working hours per Chinese person at 2,548 — highest among OECD nations.


Translation: Why Do Urban Chinese Have So Many Misconceptions About the Countryside? (Part One) — Samuel Wade, China Digital Times — 2026-04-29 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The widely-held urban Chinese (and downstream Western) belief that rural Chinese have meaningful “land ownership” as a fallback, that homestead plots are valuable assets, and that rural cost of living is genuinely low — all of which underpin the policy assumption that the rural elderly can subsist on the land without urgent state intervention.

Samuel Wade’s CDT translation presents Part One of journalist Peng Yuanwen’s livestream conversation with Zhou Jian (“Uncle Zhou”), a rural-public-benefit activist who has visited nearly 3,000 villages across 150 of China’s impoverished counties and conducted in-depth interviews with 2-3,000 rural people. Peng — a regular in CDT’s 404 Deleted Content Archive who recently won the grassroots Journalists Home News Prize for commentary on rural pensions — was awarded by a jury that credited his 30+ pieces with raising the issue’s public profile. Zhou’s claims, on one core question (how far is rural reality from city perceptions): (1) Land “ownership” is functionally meaningless — Zhou has met no farmer who got rich farming; a Sichuan paddy field of 1 mu (~1/6 acre) yields ~800 yuan ($120) after costs, and farmers legally hold only usage rights via village-group “ownership” with no legal standing; (2) Subsidies are out of reach for small farmers — minimum thresholds restrict them to large operations, and Zhou cites a farmer reduced to growing vegetables in a 1.5-meter strip beside his courtyard wall after consolidation; (3) Rural homestead is “not worth a fart” — location-location-location applies in reverse (no ambulance access, no inheritance, sale-of-structure-only); (4) Cost of living is not actually lower — Wenquan Elementary in Yunnan paid 50%+ more than Shanghai prices for curtains after four legs of shipping markups, and rural areas serve as the dumping ground for shanzhai (counterfeit) goods and B-grade livestream-commerce items. The killer detail: a Five Guarantees recipient in Zhongjiang, Sichuan cut his own toe off with a kitchen cleaver after a glass injury because he couldn’t afford the food and travel costs of hospital treatment that medical insurance would have covered, then died of a subsequent illness days after returning home.


Words of wisdom, on China shock 2.0 — Tyler Cowen, Marginal Revolution — 2026-05-02 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Michael Pettis’s recurring claim that China’s policy of suppressing domestic consumption forces “the demand-suppressing cost” of Chinese policies onto trading partners — an argument central to the second-wave “China shock” discourse driving current US trade-policy debate.

Cowen quotes economist J. Zachary Mazlish at length and endorses his demolition. The Pettis logic assumes other countries hold their own demand fixed in response to Chinese surpluses, which would predict rising unemployment in the rest of the world as China’s surpluses grow. Mazlish notes the empirical record decisively rejects this: US and EU unemployment fell during China Shock 1.0 (2000-08), and post-2021 the EU has seen falling unemployment while the US is at full employment. Monetary policy in trading-partner countries adjusts to offset any demand shortfall. The sophisticated counter — that this only fails at the zero lower bound (ZLB) where rates can’t go lower — is also rejected: monetary authorities can raise inflation targets, switch to NGDP or price-level targeting, use forward guidance, or expand QE; fiscal policy remains available as a backstop. Cowen’s selection of this passage and his “Words of wisdom” framing is the implicit argument: the Pettis demand-suppression frame, which now underwrites bipartisan tariff escalation, is theoretically wrong, and the “China shock 2.0” panic is built on macroeconomic illiteracy about how floating-exchange-rate economies actually adjust.


Capitalism and Modernity — Alex Tabarrok, Marginal Revolution — 2026-04-29 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: A series of Twitter threads by University of Pennsylvania economist Jesús Fernández-Villaverde (JFV) on capitalism and modernity, which Tabarrok presents at length with endorsement.

JFV’s thesis is that the standard left-critical vocabulary for capitalism (alienation, depersonalization, consumerism, bureaucratic dysfunction, environmental degradation) confuses the actual phenomenon being criticized. Drawing on his teaching of Soviet and Maoist Chinese economic history, he argues these pathologies appeared as severely or more severely in a 1970 Leningrad factory or a 1978 Jiangsu collective farm as in 2026 Philadelphia. Critics lack the cognitive framework to distinguish capitalism from modernity — defined as the organization of society around technology, formal institutions, and rational criteria. Conspicuous consumption is pre-capitalist (couples in 1982 Leipzig were as interested in it as those in 2026 Chicago); De Beers rode an existing evolutionary disposition rather than invented it. The “no true Scotsman” Trotskyist move (the USSR/PRC weren’t really socialist) doesn’t save the framework. JFV’s second post extends the argument with a thought experiment of a “socialist commonwealth” (SC): if it wants to deliver state-of-the-art mRNA vaccines, MRIs, FLASH radiotherapy machines, etc., it must run large bureaucratic organizations, which inevitably suffer the dysfunctions (asymmetric information, career concerns, conformity pressure, internal politics) that critics blame on capitalism — actually worse than under capitalism because the profit motive provides a sharp disciplinary mechanism that not-for-profits and state bureaucracies lack. He cites his experience at an Ivy League graduate school as evidence that bureaucratic dysfunction without the profit lever is more acute, not less. The Yugoslav workers’ self-management experiment is invoked as proof that “more participation” or “more democracy” within bureaucracies makes things worse (cf. Arrow’s impossibility theorem). The conclusion: at a fundamental level, Marx got modernity wrong; Weber got it right. The iron cage is not capitalist — it is modern. Bureaucracy is the institutional form modern society uses to coordinate large-scale tasks under rational, impersonal rules; hospitals, ministries, armies, universities, and corporations all converge on the same form because it works at scale.
### International register: Hungary, Africa, Sahel, EU realignment

The international layer this week did genuine work the roster’s American-foreign-policy beat could not. Three African Arguments pieces — Berisso on Ethiopian historical memory, Mfum on Ghanaian smallholder agroecology, Ajidagba on the political economy of Nigerian taxation — supply perspectives genuinely written from inside Africa rather than filtered through the Africa-as-object register Western coverage usually defaults to. Paterson’s WOTR analysis of the April 25 Mali attacks closes last week’s wire-check gap on Sahel jihadist expansion (the synthesis flagged the absence of any roster engagement with the largest jihadist attack on Mali in years). The Hungary cluster — WOTR’s Ésik podcast plus Euro|topics’ four-national aggregation — extends the Caldwell/Magyar arc from prior weeks past “style-continuity” into what may be system-collapse, with elites fleeing across borders. Two Courrier International translations show what European strategic autonomy and far-right repositioning look like from Brussels, Rome, Paris, and Berlin rather than from Washington. Two Mandel Commentary pieces register Israeli electoral structure (Bennett-Lapid merger; Eisenkot’s realist refusal) and Diaspora institutional drift (JTS commencement infighting). And Gilman-via-Raza names the framework — “middle-power moment” — that the discourse has been groping toward across orientations for six months.


Ethiopia does not need a golden past — it needs an honest future — Geleta T. Berisso, African Arguments — May 2, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the ongoing Ethiopian public dispute over the legacy of Emperor Menelik II (the late-19th-century ruler who consolidated the modern Ethiopian state through conquest of southern and western peoples) and, beneath it, the unresolved question — sharpened by the post-Tigray-war reconciliation context and the EPRDF-to-Prosperity-Party transition — of how Ethiopia’s peoples can share a polity given that they have lived its history on radically unequal terms.

Berisso refuses both available framings. The veneration camp treats Menelik as the architect of a modern state that preserved Ethiopian sovereignty through the Battle of Adwa (1896, the rare African victory over a European colonial army); the denunciation camp treats him as the author of conquest, dispossession, and slavery against Oromo, Sidama, and other southern peoples. Berisso’s argument is that both readings collapse history into “moral theatre” and that neither suffices for the political work the country must now do.
