The hinge is his claim that Ethiopia’s past did not produce “an equal inheritance for all who lived within the state’s reach” — some communities encountered the state as prestige and protection, others as military expansion and cultural marginalisation, and these were “different experiences altogether, produced within the same political order but distributed unequally across it.” The Derg (1974-1991 military junta) and the EPRDF (the Tigrayan-led front that ruled 1991-2018) inherited but did not resolve this unsettled inclusion question. Berisso’s positive program is institutional rather than mnemonic: a constitutional order in which limits are credible, adjudication is trusted, and “the work of education does not consist in the manufacture of patriotic amnesia.” His closing register: Ethiopia “needs a more adult politics: one capable of facing the past without either worship or denial.”


From tragedy to resilience: women farmers in Ghana turn to agroecology to confront climate change — Michael Sarpong Mfum, African Arguments — April 30, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the deteriorating agricultural conditions in Ghana’s transitional ecological belt — Bono, Bono East, and Ahafo Regions, the country’s traditional maize-cassava-yam-plantain hub — where unreliable rainfall, prolonged dry spells, declining soil fertility, and illegal logging-induced rainfall disruption have made 2024 a particularly devastating crop year, against the backdrop of Sustainable Development Goal 2 (end hunger) and SDG 13 (climate action) becoming “increasingly out of reach.”

The piece is reported field-level rather than analytical: Mfum interviews farmers Faustina Amponsah (Faaman, Techiman North Municipality, Bono East) and Judith Kumbata, soil scientist Dr Rechiatu Asei (Sunyani Technical University), and EcoCare Ghana Project Officer Enock Danso Okyere. The substantive content is the description of agroecological practice — composting cassava peels, yam skins, and plantain residues into pit-fermented organic fertiliser; intercropping maize with cowpea or groundnut legumes; planting trees alongside crops to buffer temperature extremes and conserve soil moisture; biodiversity-based pest control without chemicals — and the institutional context (ActionAid Ghana training, the EU-funded Landscapes and Environmental Agility across the Nation/LEAN project diversifying livelihoods into snail rearing, beekeeping, and aquaculture). The hinge: Asei’s claim that “agroecology increases farm income and diversifies risk. One of the most important findings is that it pays” — i.e. the case for agroecology is not only ecological but financial. The piece closes by flagging the standing structural obstacle: women-led farms cannot access credit from Ghanaian banks, and the government has not stepped in via Metropolitan, Municipal and District Assembly soft loans.


Nigeria’s fiscal origins and the politics of taxation today — Olanrewaju Ajidagba, African Arguments — April 27, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the Tinubu administration’s fiscal-reform program — anchored by the Presidential Fiscal Policy and Tax Reforms Committee headed by Taiwo Oyedele (PwC’s former Africa tax leader, now Nigeria’s finance minister) and the Nigeria Tax Administration Act 2025 — which has raised Nigeria’s tax-to-GDP ratio from under 10% to 13.5% and is targeting further convergence with developed-economy levels (Oyedele has cited France as an aspirational benchmark in an Arise TV interview).

Ajidagba’s frame is historical-political-economy. He opens with Lord Lugard’s 1914 post-amalgamation report celebrating that the merger of the Northern and Southern Protectorates had produced an £80,500 surplus rather than the projected £200,000 deficit — and notes that Lugard “hardly considered” the political implications of creating a new country, treating it as a mainly bureaucratic exercise. The implied parallel: Tinubu’s reformers risk the same error of conflating fiscal-stabilisation success with political-settlement success. Ajidagba acknowledges the corrective steps have been necessary (fuel subsidy removal, orthodox monetary policy, exchange-rate stabilisation; net foreign exchange reserves have risen from $3.99 billion in 2023 to $50.45 billion by mid-February 2026, with the official-parallel-rate gap narrowing). The hinge is the structural problem: Nigeria’s economy is roughly 92% informal employment per recent NBS estimates, and the Nigeria Tax Administration Act 2025 (Section 101: ₦100,000 first-month penalty plus ₦50,000 each additional month for failure to file) sits uneasily with finance minister Oyedele’s stated commitment not to tax 90% of the informal economy. The deeper question Ajidagba foregrounds: “how will a state that struggles to provide security, infrastructure and public trust justify a more intrusive fiscal relationship with its citizens?” The defence-spending case study — ₦5.41 trillion in 2026, ₦6.57 trillion in 2025, with insecurity remaining the most visible state failure — anchors the warning that revenue rises without state-capacity rises produce “a stronger treasury without producing a stronger state.”


Western Withdrawal, Jihadist Expansion: How the Sahel Became Ground Zero for Global Terrorism — James Paterson, War on the Rocks — April 29, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The April 25, 2026 coordinated attacks across Mali — Jama’at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin (JNIM, an al-Qaeda affiliate) operating with Tuareg separatist forces from the Front de libération de l’Azawad (FLA), striking Bamako, Gao, Mopti, Kati, and Kidal simultaneously, killing Defense Minister Sadio Camara, and seizing Kidal — read as the logical endpoint of two decades of Western kinetic-first counter-terrorism strategy.

Paterson (Lowy Institute Transnational Challenge research associate, PhD Monash) traces the trajectory. Western expulsion: France ended Operation Barkhane in Mali (2022), withdrew from Burkina Faso (2023); the US completed Niger withdrawal (Sept 2024), abandoning Air Base 201 in Agadez. Russian replacement: Wagner Group, then Africa Corps, replaced Western partners; Russia offered “regime protection without democracy conditions” but performed worse — violence involving Russian mercenaries up 81%, fatalities up 65%; in July 2024 a JNIM-FLA force killed ~84 Russian mercenaries and ~50 Malian soldiers in Tinzawaten. Sahel now accounts for ~half of global terrorism deaths; nearly 20,000 fatalities since 2019; Burkina Faso ranks worst worldwide in terrorism impact for the second straight year; Niger recorded the largest 2024 increase globally (94% to 930 deaths); Mali ranked fourth. JNIM has emerged dominant: governs territory, levies taxes, regulates commerce, enforces social codes, generates ~$18-35 million annually via extortion, mining taxation, ransom kidnappings. The piece’s structural argument: state-controlled jihadist sanctuary transforms operational capabilities qualitatively (al-Qaeda had years to plan 9/11 from Taliban Afghanistan; ISIS used Syrian-Iraqi territory for Paris and Brussels attacks), and JNIM’s al-Qaeda affiliation plus the Islamic State Sahel’s integration into the General Directorate of Provinces means territorial consolidation is not contained at the affiliate level — sanctuary functions as an organizational asset for transnational networks. Pivot to policy: limited re-engagement with junta-led states (legal under Section 7008 carve-outs) and pivot to coastal states (Benin, Ghana, Côte d’Ivoire via the Accra Initiative) are both insufficient on their own; the actual recalibration required is “inversion” — minimal but persistent intelligence presence paired with sustained governance investment in district administration, judicial capacity, economic alternatives, and reduced security-force abuses.


What a Post-Orban Hungary Means for Hungarians and Europe — Sándor Ésik (interviewed by Ryan Evans), War on the Rocks (podcast) — April 30, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Péter Magyar’s landslide April 12 victory ending Viktor Orbán’s 16-year Fidesz rule, with Tisza winning a two-thirds parliamentary supermajority — and the broader European question of what Hungary’s pivot means for EU cohesion, Russia/Ukraine alignment, and the model that several other European right-populists treated as a template.

Sándor Ésik, the lawyer behind the Hungarian Muse newsletter, in conversation with WOTR editor Ryan Evans, walks through three arcs. First: how Orbán built the “soft-authoritarian” system — capture of judiciary, media, and electoral architecture without the visible repression of harder authoritarian models, which made it durable for 16 years but also brittle once the opposition learned to operate within rather than against the captured terrain. Second: why Magyar (a former Fidesz insider who turned in 2024) succeeded where prior opposition coalitions failed — by competing inside Orbán’s symbolic system rather than from outside it, and by capitalizing on the corruption-fatigue and economic-stagnation slow burn rather than running on EU values. Third: post-Orbán implications — Hungary’s anti-Ukrainian, pro-Russian, anti-EU positioning is expected to reverse on all three fronts, but Magyar inherits a state apparatus structurally booby-trapped to resist reform (judges, prosecutors, media regulators, university trustees all installed for long terms), so de-Orbánization will be a multi-year institutional grind rather than a clean break. The episode also includes the bizarre subplot of Orbán’s son — an Army officer — planning a “crusade” of sorts in Africa.


Hungary: what’s the plan, Mr Orbán? — Euro|topics editors, Euro|topics — April 30, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Viktor Orbán’s resignation of his parliamentary seat following Fidesz’s April 12 defeat, his stated intention to “reshape the nationalist movement” while continuing as Fidesz leader, and successor Péter Magyar’s warning that a “mafia” formed around Orbán is planning to flee the country with their billions.

Euro|topics editors compile four national perspectives. Hvg (Hungary, centre-left weekly): explains Orbán’s reasoning instrumentally — as a 52-member mini-group MP he can’t make legislative impact and would be a sitting target for Fidesz speakers’ criticism (a position he knows from previous opposition years). Népszava (Hungary, left-wing daily): argues Orbán should have exited the stage entirely, that the defeat was uniquely crushing and the failure squarely his own. Forum24 (Czech Republic, web): describes Fidesz as “in tatters” and the system as “a fortress from which the elites are fleeing via the side doors” — if more than half the parliamentary group flees, fortunes cross the border, and state media starts reporting collapse, “it won’t just be the end of an era. It will be the collapse of an empire.” Der Standard (Austria, Paul Lendvai): notes that restoration of Hungarian judicial independence could quickly reveal the wealth accumulated by Orbán’s family and friends, and that some commentators are asking whether Orbán will even return from his planned US summer holiday — a flight from responsibility looks more realistic than another opposition rebuild.


De Bruxelles à Rome, l’autonomie stratégique européenne devient le nouvel horizon — Jeremy Cliffe (Financial Times, via Courrier International) — May 2, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The cumulative effect of Trump’s second-term performance on European political alignment — and specifically the moment when Tisza voters rejected Magyar’s predecessor government while explicitly signaling (85% of Tisza voters wanted change in US relations) that the vote was about repositioning toward the EU and away from MAGA.

FT writer Jeremy Cliffe (translated by Raymond Clarinard for Courrier International) argues that Hungary’s election severed a key link in the chain connecting MAGA to the European far-right. Budapest had become the transatlantic hub of the far-right ecosystem (CPAC Hungary, MCC Brussels, the Orbán model serving as inspiration for Trump’s second term). After Tisza’s win, the convergence between European far-right and American right that surged after Trump’s 2024 victory has shifted into divergence. Hungarians who voted Tisza were primarily concerned with living standards and public services, but exit polling showed 85% wanted change in US relations — a specific repositioning vote, not just an anti-incumbent one. Cliffe traces the broader European pattern: traditional Atlanticist conservatives initially tried to “open their arms” to Trump on the assumption they could brake or co-opt him; the far-right saw legitimation potential. Both have now [paywall cuts here, but the implied conclusion is “abandoned the project”]. The strategic-autonomy frame, long associated with French Gaullists and a few Brussels intellectuals, is now the active horizon from Brussels to Rome — meaning even Meloni’s Italy is now operating on a strategic-autonomy logic rather than a US-pivot logic.


Trump lâché par ses alliés : la panique gagne l’extrême droite européenne — Clea Caulcutt and Nette Nöstlinger (Politico Europe, via Courrier International) — May 2, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The cumulative effect of Orbán’s April 12 electoral defeat (despite Trump’s repeated public support and a pre-election Vance visit to Budapest), the Iran war’s regional fallout, and the Trump-Pope Leo tensions on European far-right leaders’ calculus about whether to remain associated with Trump.

Politico Europe reporters Caulcutt and Nöstlinger (translated by Raymond Clarinard for Courrier International) document the shift. Marine Le Pen reportedly told Rassemblement National (RN) deputies at an April 14 meeting: “We must keep our distance” — according to a party official present. Torben Braga, an Alternative für Deutschland (AfD) Bundestag member on the foreign affairs committee, conceded that even though some see value in maintaining Trump links, “in the specific context of elections, this approach is not particularly promising.” For Italian PM Giorgia Meloni, Trump’s attacks on Pope Leo were the [paywall cuts here, but the implied claim is “the breaking point”]. The piece’s central observation is that the European far-right’s repositioning began before Orbán’s defeat, with Trump’s Iran-war handling and the papal feud already creating distance — Orbán’s defeat then accelerated and crystallized what had already started. The article was published in Courrier International on May 2 and is a translation/repackaging of an underlying Politico Europe analysis.


Infighting Over Israel at the Jewish Theological Seminary — Seth Mandel, Commentary — May 1, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: a divide among the 2026 JTS (Jewish Theological Seminary, the flagship rabbinical and academic institution of Conservative Judaism) graduating class over the upcoming commencement ceremony, in which a substantial number of students are threatening not to attend out of objection to the “divisiveness” of this year’s invited speakers — an inversion of the more common campus-commencement controversy, in which pro-Palestinian protesters disrupt pro-Israel speakers.

[Note: Commentary’s article body is paywalled; only the lead paragraph is available. The summary below reconstructs from the lead and Mandel’s standing register.]

Mandel — Commentary’s executive editor and the magazine’s most prolific commentator on intra-Jewish-American Israel politics — frames the JTS dispute as significant precisely because Conservative Judaism has historically been the denominational center of American Zionist commitment, sitting between the more uniformly pro-Israel Orthodox movements and the more internally-divided Reform movement. A boycott of one’s own rabbinical-school commencement over Israel-related speaker selection would represent a sharper internal break than the standard college-campus version, because the institutional stakes (these are future Conservative rabbis) include the future shape of the rabbinate itself. The hinge: this is about graduates objecting to Israel-supporting speakers at a denomination’s flagship rabbinical seminary — the chain implies the Israel-skeptical position has migrated from the activist margins into the institutional core of one of American Judaism’s three major denominations.


The Netanyahu-Bennett Election Finally Takes Shape — Seth Mandel, Commentary — April 27, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the announced merger of Naftali Bennett’s party and Yair Lapid’s party into a single ticket for Israel’s fall elections, with Bennett (positioned to Netanyahu’s right) at the head — and the question of whether Gadi Eisenkot, the former IDF chief of staff whose son was killed in the Gaza war, will join the merged ticket as a third leg.

Mandel’s analysis is structured around what he calls “the merger tax” — the empirically observable Israeli pattern that parties combining before elections win fewer total seats than the same parties running separately and then combining their vote share in a coalition deal after the election. By that math, Bennett and Lapid would maximize total opposition seats by running separately. So why merge? Mandel’s answer: the party that wins the most votes gets first crack at building a governing coalition, and Bennett-Lapid believe that having the largest individual party matters more than having the largest coalition of parties — because otherwise Netanyahu, with his unmatched coalition-building skills, will get first crack and probably succeed. The merger is therefore not about maximizing votes but about denying Netanyahu the procedural opening. The hinge: Eisenkot is polling 14 seats and a three-way merger would “almost certainly win the election outright,” but per the merger tax it would win fewer seats than running separately. Eisenkot’s stated position — that “the only goal” is “the victory of a Zionist, nationally responsible coalition” via maximizing votes, and that “every union must be judged by that” — is read by Mandel as the realist refusal to absorb the merger tax. The closing frame: the election itself will be “the warm-up”; the real competition begins after votes are counted.


A New Non-Aligned Movement — S. Abbas Raza, 3 Quarks Daily — May 1, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the structural reorganization of the post-Pax-Americana global order, with the United States having “abdicated its traditional managerial role” under Trump 2.0 and China not yet ready (or perhaps not suited) to step into Washington’s place; Mark Carney’s call for middle powers to “act together” to “create a third path with impact”; and the Carnegie Endowment’s Stewart Patrick framing of the current geopolitical moment as a “middle power moment.”

S. Abbas Raza’s 3QD post is a brief excerpt-and-pointer to a longer Nils Gilman essay in Dissent. Gilman — the Berggruen Institute and American Affairs veteran whose writing tends toward big-frame geopolitical reorganization arguments — argues that today’s emergent US-China cold war is also “a contest for hearts and minds, but the prize has shifted from the periphery to the middle.” The hinge is that the new swing states are not the post-colonial South of the original Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) and the Group of 77 (G-77), but the established mid-tier rich countries (Canada, Australia, Japan, Germany) and the emerging giants (India, Brazil, Indonesia). Gilman claims today’s middle powers have advantages the G-77 lacked — economic weight, internal political legitimacy, technological and financial sophistication — that may make a coordinated “third path” actually viable rather than rhetorically gestural.
### Standalone pieces

These are the week’s pieces that don’t cluster cleanly with any of the larger themes — culture, philosophy, MR flags, and a few investigative one-offs presented individually.


What Deontological Bars? — Scott Alexander, ACT — April 30, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Original commentary, with an immediate motivating case from an internal AI-safety-movement debate about whether (a) working with the “least irresponsible” AI labs to help them win the race or (b) pursuing legislative pause/ban movements crosses a moral bar.

Scott Alexander uses “constraint consequentialism” — the position that you should pursue good consequences unless doing so violates a hard rule (“deontological bar”) — and tries to derive what those bars are and where they come from. He starts with the gimme: don’t assassinate democratically-elected leaders, even very bad ones, because the bright-line equilibrium where everyone refrains is more stable than one where individual would-be brilliant forecasters defect. Kant’s “act as if your maxim were a universal law” formulation correctly rederives the assassination bar but fails for unilateral military disarmament (Ukraine shouldn’t disband its army even though universal disarmament would be wonderful). Alexander iterates to “don’t be the first person to defect from a generally functioning norm” — which licenses keeping a military when others have one, and prohibits assassination when the no-assassination norm is intact. He notes a problem: this fails to prohibit small-scale online misinformation, since no functioning norm against it exists. He revises to “don’t do what would be bad if universalized, unless the norm is non-functioning in a way that traps you in cooperate-while-enemy-defects.” Applying this to AI: the norm “don’t support an AI company” is thoroughly broken (many companies, billions in investment, no holdout group to defect against), so working with the least-irresponsible labs may be permissible — though Alexander notes this same logic also seems to license taking a slightly-less-brutal concentration camp guard job, which is uncomfortable. The norm “don’t use mass politics” is even more thoroughly broken, so the AI-pause coalition with Bannon, Sanders, and NIMBYs may also be permissible. He closes by gesturing at the “Early Christian Strategy” — ignore all this and just do the right thing — as a possibility he doesn’t fully embrace.


Your Attempt To Solve Debate Will Not Work — Scott Alexander, ACT — April 28, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Repeated ACX grant applications for “argument-mapping” tools, “disagree better” platforms, and other projects to systematize internet debate.

Scott Alexander explains why he turns down grants for “solve debate” projects, in four moves. First: real debates don’t decompose into mappable premise-conclusion structures. Even a relatively clean case like “lockdowns hurt the economy → lockdowns are bad” requires quantifying magnitudes, weighing against benefits, and committing to a theory of the Good — and even then someone can reasonably reject the conclusion on civil-rights grounds the framework can’t capture. Increasing the number of circles makes the diagram more complex without making it more useful, since real disputes hinge on probability weighting, not Aristotelian syllogism. Second: arguments rarely hinge on false facts or named fallacies; even Alex Jones (an “insane person”) rarely says specifically false things, and most disputants happily jettison a false fact and continue arguing on other grounds. The AI-risk debate, Alexander notes, comes down to how much weight to put on theoretical-vs-empirical evidence — neither side is wrong about a fact or committing a fallacy. Third: the hardest problem is acquiring users. Argument apps need two compatible people willing to argue — a market that’s harder to bootstrap than dating, since people don’t actually want to argue (they want to take potshots and feel they’ve embarrassed enemies; hinge: even Alexander himself doesn’t enjoy arguing — he likes posting his opinion and being agreed with). Fourth: in 2,000+ years, no argument-mapping institution has caught on, despite real progress in formal logic and heuristics. The closest thing — Reddit’s r/changemyview — is just a community of people who want to argue, not a mechanical change to argument format.


Against the Screen-Time Panic — Leila Mechoui, Compact Magazine — April 29, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Pete Etchells’s book Unlocked: The Real Science of Screen Time (and How to Spend It Better), the Jonathan Haidt / Jean Twenge “Have Smartphones Destroyed a Generation?”-style anti-screen consensus, and the wider parenting-advice culture that has followed.

Mechoui — an Ottawa-based writer — argues that the empirical case against children’s screen time is much weaker than the surrounding discourse suggests. Following Etchells, she notes there is no reliable causal pathway from screen time to most behavioral measures, the effect sizes are usually small (sleep effects are minutes per hour of use), and the very metric is incoherent, lumping gaming, homework, and FaceTiming a grandparent into a single number — “the equivalent of measuring ‘paper time’ without distinguishing between reading a novel or filling out a tax return.” She traces the cultural orthodoxy to a 2018 inflection point when Apple shareholders, citing Twenge’s work, pushed Apple to introduce its Screen Time tool, after which research output and policy attention compounded. Pivoting from epistemology to sociology, Mechoui frames the panic as the latest phase of the trend Christopher Lasch identified in his 1977 Haven in a Heartless World: “helping professions” — doctors, psychologists, social workers — displacing parental authority since the late nineteenth century. Risk amplification is the experts’ “primary legitimation device”: ordinary parental judgment is recast as dangerous ignorance to justify ongoing intervention, and ordinary children’s habits become diagnosable mental-health conditions. She brings in Lenore Skenazy’s free-range parenting movement and sociologist Ellie Lee’s observation that “what can possibly go wrong is equated with what is likely to happen,” and concludes that the corrective is not an alternative expert prescription but parents trusting their judgment through experience.


How Occultists Remade the World — Christopher Coome, Compact Magazine — May 1, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Recent Vatican alarm about rising “occultism, esotericism, and Satanism” (a March meeting between Pope Leo and the International Association of Exorcists, plus Pope Francis’s 2024 warning) and survey data showing roughly a third of Americans now practice astrology or tarot and 62% endorse New Age beliefs.

Coome — a Duke postdoctoral fellow whose forthcoming book is on Freemasonry, Theosophy, and the occult revival — traces the contemporary supernatural turn to a single 1875 founding moment: Helena Blavatsky and Henry Steel Olcott’s Theosophical Society in New York, which fused Hermeticism, Kabbalah, Hinduism, and Buddhism with scientific rhetoric into a “post-Christian” religious universalism. He argues that Theosophy’s downstream effects are vastly larger than the movement itself: through Joseph Campbell (whose “monomyth” framing of all religion as variants of one hero’s journey shaped Star Wars), Aldous Huxley (whose Perennial Philosophy is “Theosophy in a nutshell”), Christmas Humphreys, and Alan Watts, Theosophical universalism became the default Western frame for spirituality without doctrine. The same wellspring fed the German Lebensreform / Ascona milieu — vegetarianism, naturalism, biodynamic agriculture, Waldorf schools, homeopathy — which then forked into both the California hippies and, through the Thule Society and Ariosophy, the occult-racial mysticism that midwifed the Nazi Party (the Münchener Beobachter became the Völkischer Beobachter; Rosenberg, Hess, and Eckart were Thule members). A second branch runs through René Guénon’s Traditionalism and Julius Evola’s fascist-mystic writings to today’s Steve Bannon and Aleksandr Dugin; a third, through Aleister Crowley’s Thelema and the Ordo Templi Orientis, to Wicca, LaVeyan Satanism, and rock-and-roll iconography. Coome closes by arguing that as Western Christianity recedes the vacuum is being filled not by atheism but by an agnostic occult-tinged spirituality whose architecture was laid down 150 years ago — and that crisis-stressed populations will reach further into “the wastebasket” for answers.


Jan Morris and the Limits of Gender Fantasy — Susan Pickard, Compact Magazine — April 30, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Sara Wheeler’s just-published biography of Jan Morris — the Welsh historian and Times correspondent (1926-2020) whose 1974 memoir Conundrum was among the first autobiographical accounts of sex change — which suggests Morris “foreshadowed the gender fluidity of the twenty-first century.”

Pickard, a Liverpool sociologist, rereads Conundrum to argue that Morris’s account is poorly assimilated to contemporary gender theory. Where poststructuralist accounts hold that language constructs identity, Morris’s conviction of being a woman is “older and more surreal,” closer to mysticism than theory: from childhood James Morris coded the world into masculine and feminine atmospheres (Oxford and Venice female, Cardiff and London not; The Times masculine and preferable to the “martyred mother” Guardian) and treated femininity as an aesthetic style rooted “in the spirit.” Pickard shows the symbolic system was supported by considerable privilege (Morris could afford to stop working to transition; Conundrum never mentions money) and by a striking inattention to embodied practice — Morris did no domestic labor either before or after transition, took pleasure in male-coded athletic feats like the 1953 Everest expedition, and described his marriage and children as possessions and “lovely objects.” Drawing on Maurice Merleau-Ponty, Pickard’s central claim is that meaning arises from embodied experience; Morris’s symbolic order, by contrast, was “narrower and more private,” and its strain “becomes visible where it meets the lives of others” — most painfully in his daughter Suki’s account of cruelty around her weight and abilities, an account Suki had to caveat with disclaimers about transphobia. By the end of Conundrum Morris wants to “surpass” both male and female “in art,” which Pickard reads as the symbolic system reaching beyond what it can sustain. In a striking final move, she argues that LLM-generated meaning is paradoxically more grounded in embodied reality than Morris’s private mythology, because it sediments many lives rather than one.


The Limits of the American Religion — Stephen G. Adubato, Compact Magazine — April 28, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Ross Barkan’s fourth novel Colossus (Arcade Publishing, 288 pages), framed through Slavoj Žižek’s reading of the American dream as a “spectral show” in which fundamentalist preachers’ hidden vices are not hypocrisy but consistency with American “fake purity.”

Adubato — Compact’s associate editor — reviews Colossus, whose protagonist Teddy Starr is the pastor of a small-town Midwestern evangelical church who rails against the godless left while concealing both adulterous affairs and the larger secret that he was born Reuvain in a Brooklyn Hasidic enclave. Teddy’s father Yitzhak Gant, a slumlord with 316 units across nineteen buildings, is the figure whose logic Teddy has unconsciously imported: Yitzhak’s promised-land theology of ownership (“Land, next to God, was the greatest currency”; tenants are “next to apostate Jews, the very worst kind of people”) becomes the template for Teddy’s prosperity-evangelical theology in which “our words and actions begin as a bridge to reality and become, with enough effort and execution, reality itself.” Adubato reads this as the heart of American religion’s defect: a theology of self-creation in which God becomes “an appendage to the successful individual” and “the recognition of man’s contingency and his dependence on the divine” is absent. Teddy’s brother eventually tracks him down to insist that “there is nothing to replace blood,” puncturing the self-made-man frame. Adubato closes with a craft note: Barkan is “a master of building suspense,” and the New York setting (Adubato kept reading while walking from the subway through rain) makes the novel “refreshingly real” in an era when affection for place is unfashionable.


There Is No Epidemic of ‘Alpine Divorce’ — Felice Basbøll, Compact Magazine — April 27, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The April 9 conviction of a Hawaii doctor for attempting to kill his wife on a hike, the February conviction of an Austrian man for negligent manslaughter after leaving his girlfriend on the Grossglockner mountain, the resulting #AlpineDivorce TikTok genre (hundreds of thousands of views; coverage in The Guardian and The New York Times) and the etymology of the term itself — an 1896 Robert Barr short story whose actual plot involves a wife framing her husband for her own suicide.

Basbøll — a Trinity College Dublin history student — argues that the Alpine Divorce label has been stretched across a wildly heterogeneous set of behaviors: ending an argument by breaking up mid-walk, walking too fast for a less-fit partner, mismanaging a real high-altitude emergency, and actual murder. She notes that the safety advice circulating in the discourse is mostly sound (don’t exceed your fitness level, bring proper gear, do your own trail research), but that pathologizing every bad date as evidence of “toxic masculinity” misreads the underlying pattern. The Grossglockner case is itself contested (the convicted hiker is appealing) and the errors involved — overestimating ability, misjudging weather, wrong gear, delayed help calls — are the standard amateur-mountaineering failure mode for any pair drawn to risk, not specifically a male-violence pattern. Looking into the viral videos behind the trend, she finds that the women supposedly “abandoned” were typically on well-populated trails and in no real physical danger — emotionally mistreated, perhaps, but not endangered. Her structural diagnosis: in a culture that downplays sex differences, men and women approach the same hike with mismatched expectations (men treat it as a feat to master, women as a leisurely walk), women raised to think they can do anything a man can do refuse to ask for help, and men trained out of chivalry don’t offer it. Adding criminal-case reasoning to this dynamic, she argues, makes Gen Z women — already pessimistic about dating — even more isolated, and the corrective is just packing enough water to walk yourself home if the date goes badly.


On Shoplifting as Radical Praxis — Freddie deBoer, deBoer — April 28, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The Hasan Piker / Jia Tolentino interview (likely on Piker’s Twitch or a podcast) in which both endorsed shoplifting as a radical anti-capitalist act, and the broader running shoplifting-discourse cluster of the past several weeks (River Page’s “Luxury Crimes” in Free Press, Yglesias on shoplifting policy, Smith on retail crime economics, Sowerby’s UnHerd piece this week).

The entire published post is two short paragraphs: a one-sentence framing of the reader question, then Gandhi’s “talisman” quote — “Recall the face of the poorest and the weakest person that you have ever seen, and ask yourself if the next step you contemplate will be of any use to them.” No analysis, no qualification, no acknowledgment that the cluster exists. The implied argument hinges on the reader making the substitution themselves: shoplifting from a CVS or Whole Foods does nothing for the poorest person — it raises retail prices, accelerates store closures in low-income neighborhoods, and gives front-line retail workers (disproportionately poor) the daily friction of the loss.


The Consensus Draft Board Debate is Sports Culture War at Its Worst — Freddie deBoer, deBoer — April 30, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The post-2026-NFL-Draft Twitter discourse around Arif Hasan’s “consensus draft board” (a rolling aggregation of dozens of NFL analyst rankings, like an index fund for prospect evaluation), specifically the practice of “roasting” any team that picks a player ranked significantly differently than the consensus suggests.

Freddie deBoer makes two interlocking arguments. First, on the substance: the consensus board is best understood as the index fund of the NFL Draft — it aggregates many analysts’ rankings into a single weighted ranking, and like an index fund it outperforms the median individual analyst. But — and this is the central distinction the piece pushes — it predicts where players will be drafted, not which players will succeed. Hasan himself splits contributors into “forecasters” (predicting selection order) and “evaluators” (assessing who is good); the forecaster rankings cluster tightly because they’re working from shared information (combine, Senior Bowl, mocks, leaks). Aggregating closely-correlated estimators reduces variance around the mean but doesn’t move the mean closer to the truth — and the truth in the NFL draft is grim: first-round QBs hit at 35-45%, the overall first-round bust rate is 29%, and only about 0.5% of rounds-3-7 picks ever make two First-Team All-Pros. So treating the consensus as binding is “guaranteeing that you replicate the median outcome in a system where the median outcome is mediocre.” The Surowiecki “wisdom of crowds” justification fails because Surowiecki requires independent estimates, which the consensus board explicitly doesn’t have; the Bayesian justification fails because draft data is correlated and weakly informative. Second, on the discourse: the analytics community has won the substantive battle in baseball, basketball, and now football, but acts like a beleaguered minority — sneering at “Twitter randos with team helmets for avis,” dunking on people who deviate from the consensus, exhibiting what deBoer calls “Typing Penis” behavior. (Mark Zuckerberg whining about being insufficiently adored is invoked as an analogue: “the rage of the enfranchised.”) deBoer names individual analysts (Hasan, Sam Monson, Bill Barnwell, Seth Walder) and a counterexample (Ringer Fantasy Football Show) to argue that analytics culture’s persistent crybully posture is unjustified now that it has won.


Making America’s Houses Bigger May Have Been a Mistake — Lily Meyer, The Atlantic — April 30, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the National Association of Realtors finding that a majority of Millennials and Gen Zers prefer smaller homes in walkable areas to larger ones in less dense communities, against the persistent reality that row houses make up less than 20% of new US construction; revisited via Atlantic archive pieces from Linda Lewis (1978), Witold Rybczynski (1991), and Joe Pinsker (2019).

Meyer — Atlantic staff writer and novelist — uses the 1978 Lewis essay as foil: Lewis fantasized about Monticello-scale mansions; Meyer, fellow row-house owner, fantasizes only about a slightly bigger row house in a neighborhood with more restaurants. The hinge is Daniel Parolek’s 2012 coinage “missing middle housing” — the buildings between detached single-family homes and large apartment buildings (row houses, duplexes, small multifamily) that American zoning systematically blocks via minimum lot sizes, density caps, and parking requirements. Average new single-family home grew from ~1,500 sq ft in 1963 to ~2,100 today; Rybczynski (1991) called this a mistake, since row houses’ shared walls reduce heating costs, allow easy mixed-use conversion, and use less land. Meyer notes the trade-offs honestly (less light, neighbor noise — she runs a white-noise machine for the Bravo-watching neighbor) but argues that Elizabeth Plater-Zyberk’s framing — “the entertainment portion of the house is effectively outside the home” — is the right one, with neighborhood restaurants, playgrounds, and picnic groves doing the work that the suburban guest room and yard once did. The implicit close: Millennials may have been raised in big-American-home culture but the lived experience of dense walkable neighborhoods is producing genuinely different preferences.


The Trump Administration Casts Out the ‘Soul’ of MAHA — Tom Bartlett, The Atlantic — April 30, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: President Trump’s Truth Social announcement pulling the nomination of Casey Means (wellness influencer, dropped surgical residency 2018, Good Energy author and de facto MAHA bible-writer) and replacing her with Nicole Saphier (Memorial Sloan Kettering radiologist, Fox News contributor); against the broader pattern of MAHA setbacks (court ruling against HHS Secretary RFK Jr.’s most aggressive anti-vaccine moves, White House quietly telling Kennedy to stop talking about vaccines pre-midterms, CDC director nomination going to a conventional public-health figure, House restoring glyphosate liability in the Farm Bill).

Bartlett — Atlantic staff writer who has been on the MAHA beat — talks to Means herself shortly after Trump’s announcement; Means blames “disgruntled senators” (Cassidy, Murkowski, Collins) and frames her loss as a “victory for the status quo.” MAHA (Make America Healthy Again — RFK Jr.’s movement embracing raw milk, psychedelics, “spiritual crisis” framing of chronic disease, hostility to seed oils, hormonal birth control, and infant hepatitis-B vaccination) has lost momentum across multiple fronts simultaneously: the Means withdrawal, the court ruling, the White House muzzling of Kennedy’s vaccine talk, the CDC director swap, and the Farm Bill reversal on glyphosate. Replacement Saphier — book arguing US overreacted to Covid; endorses Kennedy’s whole-milk and food-pyramid claims — is heterodox-adjacent but mainstream on conventional cancer treatments, MMR/polio shots, and questioned Hegseth’s repeal of the military flu mandate. Vani Hari (the influencer “Food Babe”) on record: if Means wasn’t confirmed, it would “ruin the soul of MAHA.” Bartlett’s framing is that MAHA’s high-water mark in coalition power has now passed.


Asian Mothers, Bad Feelings: Notes on An All-Conquering Stereotype — Rebecca Liu, The Guardian (via Longreads) — April 28, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Amy Chua’s 2011 Battle Hymn of the Tiger Mother and the persistent critical-cultural backlash; the broader canon of Asian-diaspora mother-daughter texts (Maxine Hong Kingston’s The Woman Warrior, Amy Tan’s The Joy Luck Club, Jung Chang’s Wild Swans, Ling Ma’s Severance, Ocean Vuong’s On Earth We’re Briefly Gorgeous, Gish Jen’s new Bad Bad Girl, plus the Crazy-Rich-Asians/EEAAO/Turning-Red film cluster); reflective interviews Liu conducted with friends raised in Hong Kong’s competitive international-school world plus her own mother (whose father was struggle-sessioned during the Cultural Revolution).

Liu — a British Chinese essayist whose long-form work has run in The Guardian — opens with the 2011 Chua viral controversy as historical hinge: the “no sleepovers, no playdates, threatened to burn stuffed animals, called daughter ‘garbage’” coverage that briefly made tiger motherhood a globally legible category. She then resists the easy debunking move (not all Asian moms; not all relationships are fraught) and the easy validating move (everyone has a tiger mom story) in favor of a more careful structural reading: across the diasporic canon, the mother emerges as “the device through which questions of immigration, identity and history are explored.” The hinge from individual to historical: the friends’ mothers Liu interviewed were largely shaped by Cultural Revolution childhoods, denied-divorce situations, and assigned-eldest-daughter caretaker roles for golden-son brothers, with anger that “ricocheted on to the person immediately around to receive it: the child.” Liu’s own mother — eldest daughter near Wuhan, watched her grandfather struggle-sessioned at age three, classmates threw rocks — emigrated to New Zealand “because I didn’t want my bad feelings to extend to your life.” The closing turn: most friends now manage their mothers in doses, hide partners, share less; the cathartic-Hollywood-reconciliation is the rarest outcome; the next generation will love and disappoint their own children in turn.


Up In Smoke (The Profession That Does Not Exist symposium) — Philip Connors (in The Baffler symposium, via Longreads) — April 29, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The Baffler symposium “The Profession That Does Not Exist,” which gathers first-person accounts of vanishing or barely-legible jobs; Connors’s contribution traces his 24-year arc from Wall Street Journal copy editor to Forest Service fire lookout in the Aldo Leopold Wilderness, with two memoirs published in between, and reflects on what publishing-industry logic and emerging AI surveillance mean for the lookout role specifically.

Connors — author of two memoirs and a fire lookout for what is now his 24th season — opens with the moment in 2002 when his WSJ desk job got worse (assigned to copyedit the European-edition op-ed page), a friend offered to grease the skids for him to take over her fire-lookout posting in New Mexico, and he leapt. The hinge of the essay is a phone call with his book editor in 2010: when the lookout-life book was about to launch, the editor demanded Connors quit the job he had just written a love letter to, on grounds that the publishing industry needed him “available for every marketing opportunity.” Connors refused; the editor punished him with cold treatment on his second book (a long-gestating memoir about his brother’s suicide), and Connors made his peace with being a fire lookout whose income subsidizes a “dilettante’s interest in scribbling” rather than a writer with a side hustle. The piece’s structural argument: the publishing-industry logic that demands writers be perpetually available for publicity is fundamentally incompatible with the kind of work that produces the books worth writing. The closing turn: AI infrared cameras with pattern recognition are projected to replace fire lookouts soon, and Connors’s $15,692 in retirement savings makes the threat live, but he refuses the framing that finding a 401(k)-bearing alternative would be good for him. The Bertrand Cooper companion essay (immediately following in the symposium, “ISpyForGood”) on working as an insurance-defense social-media investigator surveilling injury claimants on TikTok adds the dystopian counterpart — modern jobs that exist precisely to extract economic value from publicity-saturated lives.


Microlooting: The Left’s Latest Language Deception — Christian Schneider, National Review — April 30, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Twitch streamer and “pocket revolutionary” Hasan Piker’s introduction of the term “microlooting” — applying the diminutive prefix “micro” to actual property theft — and the broader pattern of socially-engineered vocabulary the column reads as the left’s signature rhetorical move.

Schneider’s structure is taxonomic. He distinguishes organic neologisms that “stick because they capture something true” (his examples: “doomscrolling” as recognizable pathology) from manufactured terms designed to “shift moral goalposts.” The historical anchor is Derald Wing Sue’s 2007 paper that lifted “microaggressions” from a 1970s niche term into university-department-justifying status, with Schneider’s punch line: “Once you have a word, you have a grievance. Once you have a grievance, you have a bureaucracy. And once you have a bureaucracy, you have a budget line.” The deeper pattern: progressives steal common-usage words and hollow them out — his case study is “equity,” which “for most of American history” meant fairness/equal-rules but was “hijacked” in the early 2010s to mean outcomes distributed by group identity, while keeping the original word’s “moral glow” via Orwellian doublespeak. The Hayekian frame loads the closing argument: just as central planners can’t possess the distributed information needed to organize an economy, language planners can’t manufacture vocabulary by political workshop and impose it via social-media carpet-bombing — words evolve “through usage, through friction, through the slow democratic process.” The hinge: speed forecloses debate. When “microlooting” arrives “pre-loaded with moral assumptions,” there isn’t time to interrogate the premise before the phrase becomes common currency, and questioning the word becomes evidence you need to be described by another word ending in “-phobe.”


Charlottesville: The Deceit Underlying the Hoax — Steve Cortes, RealClearPolitics — April 24, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: the Department of Justice indictment, under acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, of the Southern Poverty Law Center on 11 counts including criminal donor fraud and “manufacturing the extremism it purports to oppose by paying sources to stoke racial hatred”; specifically the new claim that SPLC organized and financed the August 2017 Unite the Right rally in Charlottesville, Virginia (at which Heather Heyer was killed when neo-Nazi sympathizer James Fields drove into a counter-protest crowd) — the event Joe Biden made the founding image of his 2020 presidential campaign launch.

Cortes — former CNN/Fox broadcaster, Trump 2016/2020 and Vance 2022 campaign veteran, now president of the League of American Workers and Catholic Vote advisor — uses the indictment to revisit his own 2019 firing from CNN. The piece’s hinge is biographical: Cortes wrote a March 2019 RealClearPolitics column with the full Trump press-conference transcript debunking the “very fine people on both sides” framing (Trump’s actual words: “I’m not talking about the neo-Nazis and white nationalists because they should be condemned totally”); narrated a five-minute PragerU video on the same point that exceeded 10 million views; was permanently benched by CNN’s then-talent-director Rebecca Kutler (now head of MSNOW) explicitly because of the Prager video; was released from CNN by December 2019. The wider claim: if the indictment’s allegations are true, the SPLC fabricated the underlying extremism event, the legacy media weaponized the fabricated event with a misquotation of Trump’s response, and a journalist who tried to debunk the misquotation in real-time was punished — meaning the lie sat for almost a decade because the institutions that should have caught it were structurally invested in not catching it.


She Was Epstein’s Top Assistant. How Did She Emerge Unscathed? — Tanya Lukyanova, Free Press — April 28, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The January 2026 DOJ release of more than three million pages of Epstein emails and documents, which has triggered a wave of professional ostracism for figures like Larry Summers, Leon Black, Bill Gates, Peter Mandelson, and former Prince Andrew, but has produced almost no consequences for Lesley Groff — Epstein’s executive assistant from February 2001 until his July 2019 arrest.

Free Press reporter Tanya Lukyanova’s investigation centers on the divergence between Groff’s near-anonymity in the post-DOJ-release reckoning and her density of involvement in Epstein’s day-to-day operations: her name appears over 150,000 times in the released files, second only to Epstein’s own. According to FBI documents from her 2021 proffer interview, civil suits filed by victims (Sarah Ransome, Jennifer Araoz, Marina Lacerda), and contemporaneous correspondence, Groff scheduled Epstein’s daily massage appointments — many of which were code for sex with minors — followed up “every 15 minutes” until appointments were secured, called replacement victims when one was unavailable, paid victims in cash, and managed the logistics of girls Epstein took to his Caribbean island and New Mexico ranch. She earned over $200,000 a year by 2005, was given a Mercedes-Benz E320 by Epstein when she moved to Connecticut, and had her nanny paid for by him. After Epstein’s 2008 Florida guilty plea, she sent him reminders to update his sex-offender registry photo. After his 2019 arrest she penned a letter of support describing him as generous and accommodating. She was named in his trust to receive $2 million on his death (later amended out). Lukyanova then reports from New Canaan, Connecticut, where Groff lives in a $3 million home, hosts bunco game nights, attends the Encore women’s-club fashion shows, dines at Elm restaurant, and continues making Republican-Town-Committee donations. The bartender at Elm describes “Miss Lesley” as kind and generous; the local Republican committee has rebuffed New Canaan resident Daniel LaGattuta’s repeated calls to renounce her contributions. The piece concludes with the news that the House Oversight Committee has summoned Groff for an in-person transcribed interview on June 9, which may force New Canaan’s tolerance to break down.


Do Market Reforms Cause Growth? — Tyler Cowen, Marginal Revolution — May 1, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: A recent paper by Jon Hartley and Brian Wheaton revisiting the cross-country empirical question of whether market-oriented reforms (identified via changes in well-known economic-freedom and structural-reform indices) cause economic growth.

The Hartley-Wheaton paper uses distributed-lag and event-study frameworks to trace how per-capita GDP responds to reform episodes. They find little evidence of immediate growth gains and some short-run adjustment costs in the wake of reform. However, growth then rises gradually and persistently over time, with economically meaningful effects emerging after several years. The pattern is robust across alternative reform measures and specifications. The authors argue this reconciles previously conflicting findings in the literature: pessimistic short-run studies and optimistic long-run studies were both partially right because the J-curve is real — institutional liberalization operates through “slow-moving channels” that accumulate into sustained improvements. Cowen presents the abstract without further commentary.


Pro-Development Environmentalists — Alex Tabarrok, Marginal Revolution — April 30, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Grant Mulligan’s Breakthrough Institute (BTI) post documenting that just 10 organizations initiated 35% of NEPA (National Environmental Policy Act) cases brought by NGOs, with the Sierra Club and its local chapters alone responsible for over 14%. In forest-management and energy cases the concentration is more extreme — only 10 groups filed 67% and 48% of cases respectively. In an extended BTI follow-up that expands the scope to district and circuit court NEPA cases, Alliance for the Wild Rockies and the Center for Biological Diversity together account for 24% of all litigation against public-lands management decisions.

Tabarrok’s reframing: this is not “environmentalism” in general but a tyranny-of-the-complainers concentrated in a handful of small organizations. The largest environmental groups by budget actually do something quite different — Wildlife Conservation Society and San Diego Zoo Wildlife Alliance primarily run zoos; The Nature Conservancy (TNC), The Conservation Fund, and Ducks Unlimited protect land directly via land trusts; many run pro-market abundance-style programs. Tabarrok specifically highlights TNC’s Power of Place mapping work that the Bureau of Land Management used in its Western Solar Plan — identifying sensitive habitats to protect tightly while clearing the way for renewable build-out everywhere else. TNC has also practiced forest thinning and prescribed burns for over 60 years to prevent catastrophic wildfires, and is part of a California coalition working to thin millions of acres. Audubon’s renewables siting work, Ducks Unlimited’s water infrastructure, and Conservation Fund’s Working Lands programs all follow the same pattern. Larry Selzer of the Conservation Fund is quoted in Klein and Thompson’s Abundance: “we have to build, and build, and build.” The argument: abundance advocates and environmentalists aren’t natural enemies, and assuming they are serves neither side.


The collapse of teen fertility in the digital era — Tyler Cowen, Marginal Revolution — April 30, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: A recent paper by Nathan Hudson and Hernan Moscoso Boedo that proposes a smartphone-coordination explanation for the global collapse in teen fertility starting around 2007 — a phenomenon that has spanned countries across the income and policy spectrum and that has resisted single-country policy explanations.

The Hudson-Moscoso Boedo argument runs through a coordination model: once enough teens are on phones, being on the phone is where the peer network is, in-person time falls sharply, and unstructured face-to-face contact (in which most unintended teen conceptions occur) collapses with it. As smartphone prices fall, the in-person equilibrium ceases to exist and the population tips into a phone-mediated equilibrium. They identify this causally within the United States by exploiting terrain-ruggedness variation in broadband and 4G coverage, and use time-use diaries showing in-person teen socializing roughly halved while digital leisure roughly tripled. A parallel design in England and Wales recovers the same acceleration and the same effect of mobile coverage on teen conceptions, ruling out country-specific contraceptive-access and welfare-reform stories. Critically, the same instrument that produces the teen-fertility collapse also produces a surge in teen suicides — implying a single underlying cause rather than two separate phenomena.


Technological progress isn’t always good — Matthew Yglesias, Slow Boring — April 30, 2026 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The reflexive “Luddites were always wrong” pro-technology talking point that Yglesias himself has frequently deployed, plus implied (but unnamed) live debates about AI displacement and existential risk.

Yglesias argues the techno-optimist heuristic is sound but only generalizes under specific conditions: gainers and losers have to share a political community capable of redistributive smoothing, transactions have to take place under enough rule of law to be positive-sum, and the “long run” payoff has to arrive on a human-relevant timescale. The Inca and other Indigenous peoples wiped out by European disease and conquest are a counterexample on the first condition — there was no “long run” because the society itself was destroyed, and being an Inca techno-pessimist about the Age of Sail would have been entirely correct. The transition to agriculture is a counterexample on the third — it took thousands of years for the average human to become better off than a hunter-gatherer, and in the meantime farmers were shorter, sicker, less autonomous, and ruled by predatory warrior castes. Modern factory livestock farming is a counterexample on the second condition: chickens cannot bargain or exit, so productivity-enhancing technology has produced a hundred-plus years of straightforward worsening for the most numerous sentient beings on Earth, with cultivated meat or vertical farms the only plausible exits Yglesias can name. He extends the argument to information technology — the internet democratized speech in free societies but enabled unprecedented authoritarian surveillance in China — and to nuclear weapons, citing Stanislav Petrov’s 1983 false-alarm decision as evidence that we may have avoided technological catastrophe by sheer luck rather than by the workings of the optimistic heuristic. The piece is conspicuously silent about AI, the obvious live application, but the framework he builds is exactly the one a reader would apply to ask whether AI’s gainers and losers share a polity and whether AI-mediated transactions are law-governed.

Late additions (NYT podcasts indexed after main composition)

Two Klein/Douthat NYT podcast transcripts were retrieved from ProQuest and analyzed after the synthesis was finalized. They sit thematically in (a) AI / abundance discourse and (b) automated driving / freedom-and-mastery — but cross-cluster threads worth flagging are noted in the per-article analysis blocks. Most diagnostic finding: Klein-Thompson-Dunkelman do not engage Stoller’s “Chinese finger trap” or the broader Cass-Stoller-Bork-Davis-Münchau cross-orientation convergence on regulatory architecture; the asset-price-collateralization constraint is structurally invisible inside the abundance vocabulary.


What Worries Me Most About ‘Abundance’: The Ezra Klein Show — Ezra Klein, NYT — 2026-04-28 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: The one-year anniversary of Abundance (Klein/Thompson, March 2025) and Why Nothing Works (Dunkelman, same window); Elizabeth Warren’s recent speech naming abundance as a Trojan horse for tech billionaires (Reid Hoffman name-checked as a book promoter); Mamdani’s “Neighborhood Builders Fast Track” speech pairing rent-freeze with developer-prequalification; the Newsom-Kimmel “liberalism that builds” clip; and the Sanders “horrendous bureaucracy is common sense, not ideology” clip from a Leonhardt interview.

Klein hosts Derek Thompson (Atlantic, Abundance co-author) and Marc Dunkelman (Searchlight Institute resident scholar, Brown University fellow, author of Why Nothing Works) for a three-way audit. Thompson’s vibes/legislation/outcomes scorecard: vibes are 0.1-percentile (Hochul, Pritzker, Mamdani, Newsom all using the vocabulary), legislation B/B-plus (Newsom’s Abundant and Affordable Homes Near Transit Act named the movement explicitly; Warren-Tim Scott bipartisan housing bill passed the Senate), outcomes negligible (FRED housing-starts data shows neither Abundance nor a decade of YIMBY in California’s curve). Dunkelman situates the project as the first genuine reconsideration of progressivism’s post-1960s “speak truth to power” turn since Mills and the Port Huron Statement; he sees a real sea change. Klein’s stated worry — “what worries me most” in the title — is the small-ball-ness, that abundance collapses into a synonym for efficiency rather than a vision of plenitude. The three then walk through the populist critique with Klein deliberately steel-manning Warren: that the movement attracts billionaire funders (Hoffman et al.) and underweights corporate power as a blockage. Thompson’s tell — that he is “more excited” about exposing neighborhood/public-union/regulatory power than corporate power because the corporate-power discourse already has plenty of voices — is the conversation’s most diagnostic moment. They reach partial agreement that a hypothetical Chapter 7 on abundance-and-corporate-power should have been written, and that money in politics (Klein cites his Bores podcast and the Leading the Future PAC) corrupts the “make government powerful” prescription. The conversation’s AI section pulls in the Robert Putnam framing that societies adopt technologies’ values rather than incorporating technologies into their values; Thompson invokes the Amish-screening principle as a model for AI absorption. They diagnose Trump as a “scarcity president” (immigration cuts to the construction labor supply, tariffs on transformer inputs, the Hormuz oil shock) and treat his failure as the Democrats’ opening. The closing exchange on the Sanders clip — common-sense good government vs. ideology — is where Klein and Thompson land their actual disagreement: Klein insists abundance must be vision (firefighters affording homes in the cities they protect; Japan-style high-speed rail), not just efficiency; Thompson presses whether ideology and common-sense governance are even separable.


Why Are We Still Driving?: Interesting Times — Ross Douthat, NYT — 2026-04-30 · Claude’s Response →
Responding to: Waymo’s rapid expansion to 15+ North American cities over the past year, the live three-way contest between Waymo (multi-sensor lidar+radar+camera), Tesla (camera-only), and Zoox, and the prospect that automated driving will be normal in major US metros by ~2035.

Douthat hosts Andrew Miller — author of “The End of Driving” and writer of the “Changing Lanes” Substack, a self-described liberal transit advocate who drives a Tesla — for a sustained conversation about the Waymo future. Miller’s affirmative case is statistical and structural: 40,000 annual US road deaths, vast majority driver-error; once a self-driving stack is trained the marginal robotaxi mile is near-free; the Waymo safety-data record in San Francisco already beats human drivers. He sketches a “good 2035” of 40-50% lower transport costs, household car ownership halving, parking land reclaimed, and the Jevons-paradox caveat that cheaper transport just gets used more. The “bad 2035” is a public-transit death spiral: enough robotaxis to worsen congestion, not enough to displace private cars, and transit pulled into a doom-loop as middle-class riders defect. Miller names liability — making manufacturers assume 100% of fault when the automated driving system is in control — as the single binding policy choice; the Cruise/General Motors shutdown after a 2023 jaywalker incident is his cautionary case. Douthat presses on three fronts: the “weirdness” of inhuman accident modes (the Cruise dragging-the-pedestrian case, the Tesla white-truck collisions, the Waymo bodega cat), the surveillance-data accumulation a sensor-laden vehicle generates, and most insistently the loss of driving as embodied knowledge and as one of the last surviving American rites of passage. Miller concedes the embodied-knowledge point but refuses to draw the line at cars: if rites of passage are scarce we should manufacture new ones rather than ask driving to bear that weight. They close on the cultural-fault-line inversion that red Texas and Tennessee are leading on Waymo permitting while blue Washington, Massachusetts, and New York are foot-dragging — making “the libertarian states the gallows on which human agency is hanged,” in Douthat’s coinage.

Notable Quotes

“Edwardian Britain was a hard-working hegemon exporting capital downhill; the 21st-century US imports capital uphill, making the Pettis-Klein ‘Hobson-for-our-time’ frame structurally backwards.”
— Adam Tooze, Chartbook 443

“Western ‘just-in-time’ society — its institutions, leaders, and Professional-Managerial Caste — has destroyed the slack and the kinds of people needed to survive coming food, energy, and political crises.”
— Aurelien, It Does Now.

“America is in a Chinese finger trap — every reform that would reduce monopoly extraction would also crash the stock market that every institution (pensions, endowments, dollar geopolitics) now depends on.”
— Matt Stoller, Monopoly Round-Up

“The asylum system as written is unworkable, and the only honest reform is repealing it.”
— Matthew Yglesias, Should we end asylum?

“Political violence is reprehensible. That doesn’t make Trump less depraved.”
— Michelle Goldberg, NYT

Notes for Future Runs

  • The MCP server has unmerged code changes from the prior session (Atom feed support, browser-realistic UA for publication feeds, generate_extended layout fix). After server restart these would have eliminated the need for the parallel HTTP-fetch agent that did publication+discovery this session.
  • Several agents experienced a “linter strip” bug: appended <!-- oneline --> and <!-- summary --> blocks were silently removed by an unidentified post-Edit process, leaving only the <!-- analysis --> block. Caught and repaired in 4 files (3 Yglesias + 2 Stoller earlier). Worth a pre-composition validator.
  • ProQuest WSJ database path: the documented /wallstreetjournal/... URL is not accessible under NYPL EZProxy; the working path is /usnews/commandline?accountid=35635 filtered with pub("wall street journal"). CLAUDE.md needs updating.
  • Free Press content keeps re-importing via fetch_from_email side effects — culled three times this session. Consider a keep_only whitelist mechanism.
  • The Compact Substack Shullenberger piece is invite-only — would need either a separate Compact Substack subscription or to skip it.
    # What We Actually Have to Work With
    Crosscurrents optimist’s take — April 26 to May 2, 2026

Voters can organize against state capture, and they are. The week’s most replicated cross-publication finding is also its strongest evidence that local democracy still functions. Tonya Nickol’s Commonplace dispatch from Trenton, Ohio — Trump-county, near JD Vance’s Middletown — found 98% of her interviewees opposing the proposed 250-megawatt Prologis data center, with the Ohio AG having certified a statewide ballot initiative to ban data centers larger than 25 megawatts. Heatmap’s lead item is Compass-Brookfield abandoning a 2,100-acre Northern Virginia data-center corridor after Prince William County’s resistance made the math impossible — actual project cancellations in the buildout the synthesis treats as a juggernaut. Heatmap Pro polling shows data centers more controversial than wind farms; the regulatory machinery (infrasound ordinances, county zoning, AG-certified initiatives) is being built faster than the industry can route around it. The decoupling between the upstream U.S. hydrocarbon position and the downstream electricity-and-buildout fight is the working part of the system — local jurisdictions are exercising regulatory authority the federal level has surrendered, unblocked by administration alignment with the industry. Last week it was Maine banning data centers outright; this week it’s Ohio moving toward the same and Virginia killing a flagship facility through political friction alone.

The cross-orientation convergence on partisan-only redistricting is the live constitutional moment. Callais itself is a defeat for the racial-districting compromise that has structured Voting Rights Act enforcement since 1986. But the Free Press piece — writing from the right of the project’s coordinate space — explicitly endorses Justice Kagan’s dissenting argument that the deeper democracy-corroding problem is Rucho v. Common Cause (2019) and its green light to partisan-only gerrymandering. Eisler at Compact accepts the same procedural cost. McLaughlin’s Utah piece sits downstream of the same dynamic; Lane and Silver from prior weeks were arguing the same point through the Virginia case. This is now a four-orientation convergence — Kagan from the dissenting Court left, the Free Press from the institutionalist right, Compact from the post-liberal sovereigntist position, NR from the standard conservative election-law beat — on the same diagnosis: the constitutional architecture currently rewards partisan-only redistricting, and that is the deeper problem. This is exactly the cross-pole convergence the project reads as a precondition for reform. It does not predict reform tomorrow. It does predict that when the next reform window opens, the intellectual ground for Rucho revision is laid across orientations.

The progressive caucus is now drafting statutes. The synthesis tracks Democratic dysfunction; the optimist tracks what is actually being introduced. Stoller’s piece names the Booker-Warren-Heinrich-Murphy-Hirono CLEAN Mergers Act, which would automatically undo every Trump-era corporate combination above $10 billion (Paramount-Warner, HP-Juniper, Google-Wiz, Union Pacific-Norfolk Southern), with a rebuttable defense for non-anticompetitive deals. The Schumer-Gallego bill would force vertical de-integration in meatpacking. The Progressive Caucus’s Stop AI Price Gouging and Wage Fixing Act would ban personalized surveillance pricing. The package also includes federal manufacturing of generic drugs (modeled on California’s $11/pen insulin), seed-patent restrictions, and utility rate-of-return caps. These are concrete legislative texts, not press releases — the kind of artifacts the prior antitrust revivals of the 1930s and 1970s actually produced before the politics caught up. Stoller is honest about the structural barrier (Jeffries leadership, the DCCC under an ex-Microsoft executive, the FDR-1932-near-loss precedent), but the bills exist. The ai-displaces-no-represented-constituency carry-forward has to absorb a second update: a constituency does exist on Capitol Hill, in named bill sponsors, even if the leadership is hostile.

The political-violence convergence broke the partisan filter, on a major event, in real time. Six writers from coordinates that almost never overlap — Klippenstein, Silver, Goldberg, MacIntyre, Howland, Ahmari — converged within four days on the same factual core: Cole Tomas Allen was a credentialed elite drifter, not a movement-radicalized partisan; the framing being conscripted to him is doing political work the facts don’t support; and the cycle of rationalization-denial-minimization across orientations is the structural pathology. Goldberg refuses the bargain that would let the right’s framing constrain her Trump-criticism, and refuses equally the framing that would convert Allen into a left-coalition political object. Silver names rationalization-denial-minimization as moves performed by his own coalition. This is what a functioning intellectual sphere looks like under a stress test it failed to perform on Butler in 2024. Two years of practice on political-violence events have visibly improved the roster’s ability to think through them.

The discovery layer is closing the wire-check gap it was set up to close. Last week’s synthesis flagged the absence of any roster engagement with the largest jihadist attack on Mali in years. James Paterson’s WOTR piece this week is the structural answer: the Sahel now accounts for ~half of global terrorism deaths, JNIM has emerged dominant after the Russian withdrawal that replaced the French and American withdrawal, and the granularity lets the project actually integrate the event rather than gesture at it. Three African Arguments pieces (Berisso on Ethiopian historical memory, Mfum on Ghanaian agroecology, Ajidagba on Nigerian fiscal politics) supply the inside-the-region register no roster writer can produce. Two Courrier International translations let the project read European far-right repositioning from Politico Europe and the Financial Times via Brussels and Rome rather than via Washington. The non-Anglophone curator addition was experimental; the experimental phase is over and the additions are now load-bearing.

The global electricity finding is the counterweight to the data-center backlash. Robinson Meyer’s Shift Key episode reads Ember’s Global Electricity Review 2026: clean power covered 100% of demand growth last year, solar grew 30% in 2025 (the highest in eight years, the wrong direction at the diminishing-returns stage), and the deployment-context versatility — utility-scale in the U.S., hybrid in Australia and Germany, informal grassroots rooftop in Pakistan — is what makes solar historically unique. The dirty-electricity problem is being solved at the rate the policy literature said it could not be solved at. The data-center backlash and the solar surge are not contradictions — local resistance is exactly the political pressure that makes the buildout choose cleaner electrons. Brigham’s Funding Friday reinforces this: Meta’s first-of-its-kind agreement with Overview Energy for up to a gigawatt of beamed-down space-based solar; CATL’s 60 GWh sodium-ion deal with HyperStrong; the largest U.S. sodium-ion deal yet. The capex is finding novel chemistries because the hydrocarbon-only path is closing.

What the synthesis has right, and the path. The Iran cluster is genuinely uncertain — iran-time-direction is sharpening, not resolving — and Münchau’s swap-line warning exists precisely because the 2008 institutional response architecture will not be available in 2026. Tooze’s read of Warsh as a Hoover-Institution administrative-state-roller-back has to be taken seriously. The Stoller Chinese-finger-trap diagnosis is real. None of these get rotated away by green shoots. The path the optimist asks the synthesis to register: the local-jurisdictional layer continues to do the work; the legislative drafts exist when the political window opens; the cross-orientation convergences sharpen rather than collapse; the discovery layer extends what the project can claim about the world beyond the Anglophone subculture. The synthesis worries that nothing is repairable. The honest reading of the same evidence is that several things are being repaired, slowly, by the parts of the system that were never the news.

Full Articles

Assassin Wasn’t on FBI’s Radar, Sources Say

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-04-26 · Original →

Cole Tomas Allen

I’m grateful to be able to get the truth out about shootings like these and to push back against the attempts to politicize or obscure them. But I can’t keep doing this without your support - please become paid subscriber

Cole Allen was never flagged by the FBI’s sprawling domestic counterterrorism apparatus, sources including a senior FBI official tell me. The 31-year-old alleged gunman who attempted to penetrate the White House Correspondents’ Dinner on Saturday was, by all appearances, a normal guy — until he wasn’t.

Allen himself was reportedly stunned by what he called the “insane” lack of security at the Washington Hilton — a venue that has hosted the dinner for decades (and was the site of the attempt on Ronald Reagan’s life in 1981). Video shows that after the guests were seated, the Secret Service agents and DC Metropolitan Police security people were just shooting the shit as Allen ran by.

Now, rumors are already swirling that Allen is some anti-Christian zealot aided by a network of left-wing compatriots. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche says the FBI is looking into whether Allen was operating alone. But who was he, exactly? And what does he represent?

The answer, drawn from a copy of his resume I obtained and interviews with people who knew him, is unsettling in its ordinariness.

Cole Allen Resume

4.38KB ∙ PDF file

Download

Download

Allen graduated from Caltech in 2017— the Pasadena STEM powerhouse behind 24 Nobel laureates and NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory. With a Mechanical Engineering degree, he went to work for IJK Controls LLC, a small Southern California engineering firm, where he worked on the kind of precision hardware that keeps cameras and sensors stable on moving platforms — the technology used in drones, satellites, and military targeting systems — as well as software for beaming data between devices using laser beams instead of radio waves.

Before Caltech, he’d interned at a biomedical startup, designing 3D-printed casings for medical devices and building the specialized clamps and fixtures used to attach electrodes during manufacturing.

He also made video games — not modded existing ones, but built them from the ground up. His most notable project, Bohrdom, was entirely his own: the underlying physics system that governed how objects moved and collided, 750 original graphics, and a score he composed himself.

Former classmates say this feat wasn’t uncommon at tech-savvy Caltech, where seniors often build elaborate puzzle-games for underclassmen — a tradition called “Ditch Day Stack.” Allen, by all accounts, threw himself into that endeavor.

“He seemed proud of it,” one former classmate told me.

The Allen his classmates describe was studious, devout, and polite.

“He was pretty prominent at the Caltech Christian Fellowship,” one acquaintance and former classmate told me. “Pretty Christian and mellow.”

“If I didn’t see his face eating carpet, I would’ve never believed it,” he added, referring to the photo of Allen on the ground after police officers finally subdued him.

That detail — his faith — flies in the face of how President Trump has characterized the attack.
