Because so many federal workers live in the suburbs, anyone trying to understand the impact of DOGE layoffs on the local economy should probably look at the labor market impacts on the metro area rather than the district alone.

There are 56 metro areas in the United States with a population of over one million, and out of them Washington ranks 27th with a totally unremarkable 4.4 percent. We’re in a three-way tie with San Francisco (the white hot center of the artificial intelligence boom) and Phoenix. The fact that the economic trends in these three metro areas don’t seem to have very much in common is probably a hint that this metric alone is not an especially informative indicator.

One issue here is that the unemployment rate is a ratio, so the denominator matters a lot. The lowest unemployment metro in America is Honolulu, which is not because the Honolulu economy is so insanely good so much as it is that nobody can afford to live in Hawaii if they don’t have a job.

People with money to spend go to visit Hawaii, of course. But if you want to live there, you need work. If you can’t find a job, you need to go elsewhere. Conversely, I don’t think there’s anything particularly wrong with the economic situation in metros like Houston or Orlando or Tampa that all have higher unemployment than DC. These are places that a lot of people are moving to, maybe speculatively in hopes of finding work or just because they’ve decided they want to go somewhere warm and relatively affordable, and it may take some time to find a job. Who knows?

At any rate, the DC area did in fact suffer catastrophic job loss over the course of 2025.

What’s kept a lid on unemployment is outmigration from DC and the suburban counties. In the District itself, we experienced a population crash during Covid and though there’s been partial recovery, there are still fewer people living in the city today than there were in March of 2020.

Labor market problems manifest at the low end

Layoffs of highly educated senior public sector workers are likely to be covered by highbrow media, but that’s not really where economic problems arise.

I know some people are eagerly peering at the unemployment rates for young college graduates, trying to figure out whether AI is hurting entry-level white collar hiring. But even if it does hurt entry-level white collar hiring, I wouldn’t expect that to show up specifically as unemployment for recent college grads. If you’re looking to hire someone for a retail job, a person with the intelligence and conscientiousness to get a bachelor’s degree is going to be an above-average candidate. It’s true that if you used to be a such-and-such at some agency, you might feel the jobs that are now available are beneath you. But if you really need work, you’ll be able to find it.

The problem is that this leaves the people who’d be getting the retail jobs in a boom locked out of the job market.

The District publishes monthly labor market data, including the unemployment rate by ward. And it’s clear that unemployment has risen the most in the poorest wards.

Some of this is the aforementioned outmigration. Upscale professionals who moved to DC for work are more likely to leave in response to a layoff than a working class lifelong resident.

But a lot of it is that we’re seeing a large overall contraction in the district’s economy. Most of the laid-off federal employees lived in the suburbs and don’t show up in the DC unemployment rate cited by The Guardian. But many of them worked in the district and bought meals here. They spent discretionary entertainment dollars here that are no longer being spent in tough times.

And the entire flow of funds through the service economy has slowed down — this shows up in things like the high number of area restaurants that closed last year.

If fewer people are commuting into work then fewer people are buying lunch downtown, meaning fewer people who used to work at the downtown lunch spots are going to the barber shop nearby. The people who end up suffering the most are the ones “last in line” in the broader economic hierarchy. They just don’t have affinity groups of laid-off feds to shop their stories to the media.

What was this for?

DC is like any other city. Most people are working class, and most of them make a living providing locally-facing services. They’re security guards or retail or food service workers or they cut hair or clean houses or answer the phone at the dentist’s office.

Obviously, the federal government should not spend wastefully on public sector employees just to prop up the larger economy of one metro area. But you have to contextualize the harms of DOGE against the lack of benefits. Aggregate federal spending has not fallen (or even slowed), because personnel is a tiny share of the budget that’s easily offset by things like expensive wars and higher interest payments.

Hamilton Project graphic using Treasury data

Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act is adding trillions of dollars in new debt, including $800 billion in higher interest rate spending. By contrast, DOGE itself “had no noticeable effect on the trajectory of spending” according to analysis from the Cato Institute.

Again, personnel costs are just not a major part of the federal budget.

And despite “efficiency” being in the name, nothing DOGE did was intended to improve the cost-effectiveness of government. They didn’t do anything to make public sector hiring smoother, or to make promotions and pay raises more merit-based, or to improve the evaluation of the work people were doing.

In fact, they cut a bunch of grants that were dedicated to program evaluation and weakened the actual day-to-day oversight of contracts and federal spending. Kneecapping the IRS is costing the government hundreds of billions of dollars in tax revenue, leading to more borrowing and more interest rate spending. All the major claims about Social Security fraud were fake. Real fraud was identified in Minneapolis through normal channels by the career prosecutor who’d been investigating it since the Biden administration until he and several of his colleagues quit because the Justice Department was pressuring them to engage in unprofessional behavior related to the killing of protesters by immigration enforcement personnel.

Basically Trump started with two reasonable ideas:

  1. We should reduce the deficit
  2. We should make federal spending more efficient

He has not done either of those things. But he has kneecapped the economy of a mid-sized American city.

Claude’s Response

This is one of the better Yglesias columns of the week and a good example of his analytical strengths. The statistical disaggregation is correct and important — the Guardian’s “DC has higher unemployment than any state” framing really is misleading in exactly the ways he says, and the move to look at the metro area and at intra-District ward variation is the right unit-of-analysis correction. The downstream-services mechanism is also right and worth naming clearly: when professional-class workers lose income, the people who feel it first are the working-class workers who serve them. The class-incidence story is what the piece adds beyond the existing DOGE-was-bad consensus.

The case against DOGE on its own terms is also well-made. The point that personnel is a small share of the federal budget and is easily offset by other spending growth is correct, and the Cato citation does the work of preempting the obvious counterargument that this is just a partisan complaint. The IRS-enforcement bleed is the real fiscal scandal of the DOGE era and Yglesias has been consistent on this across multiple columns.

What’s missing — and would have made this a stronger piece — is any engagement with whether the right policy response would have been a smarter version of the same goal. Yglesias acknowledges that civil-service hiring is genuinely “stilted, inflexible, and difficult” (he made this point in the previous day’s mailbag) and says DOGE didn’t address that. But he doesn’t say what would have. A version of this column that ended with “here’s what real federal-workforce reform would look like” would be more useful than one that ends at “Trump kneecapped a city.” The implied counterfactual is “do nothing about civil-service rigidity,” which probably isn’t his actual view.

The piece is also a narrowly DC-focused take in a week when DOGE’s structural effects on federal capacity (IRS, program evaluation, contract oversight) are arguably bigger national stories than the local-economy hit. Yglesias gestures at this in the closing paragraphs but the piece’s center of gravity stays on DC. Whether that’s a deliberate framing choice or a missed opportunity depends on what he’s planning to write next; in the context of the broader Slow Boring run this week, it reads as the local-incidence companion to the deficit-and-fiscal-reform piece from 4/28. The two pieces work better together than apart.

The most interesting unstated implication: if working-class service workers in DC’s poorest wards are bearing the cost of cutting federal employment, then the political constituency for federal-workforce reform is structurally weak in exactly the place where it would be politically most defensible (a Democratic city where the people hurt are not white-collar professionals). That’s a popularism point Yglesias should make explicitly somewhere — that the intuitive “blue-state, federal-worker class” reading of who DOGE hurt is wrong in instructive ways — but he leaves it as subtext.

Back to summary

Should we end asylum?

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-04-30 · Original →

Should we end asylum?

Is ending asylum the way to stop chaos at the border?


I’m not sure asylum has a future.

Donald Trump has overreached and gone too far on immigration across multiple dimensions, and I’m allergic to his fundamentally zero-sum approach to the issue. But the specific issue of asylum — a fundamentally reactive, procedural, and legalistic mechanism that takes control over who gets to enter the U.S. (or any other country) and in what quantity out of the hands of politicians and into the hands of lawyers and judges — created problems for Barack Obama, for Joe Biden, and for many European governments. On a new episode of The Argument, I make the case that the post-WWII asylum framework is both politically untenable and practically unworkable. To save openness to immigration, we need to basically ditch this idea.

Jerusalem, naturally, disagrees.

She concedes that asylum is a huge political and policy problem for liberals. Anyone who takes seriously democratic legitimacy, economic growth, and basic human rights can’t pretend the backlash to Trump will fix the politics of immigration.

But she argues that giving up on asylum actually undersells the difficulty of the problem. People want to come to the U.S., and especially during hot labor markets Jerusalem thinks that is bound to cause politically and substantively problematic chaos unless there is a legal mechanism for them to do so.

My bottom line is that “immigration policy should be designed to maximize the national interest,” and a framework that focuses on the legal rights of would-be migrants is poorly suited to accomplish that.

Jerusalem thinks I’m oversimplifying the real issues and reaching for a cheap political solution.

Watch or listen wherever you get your podcasts.

The transcript will be after the paywall in this post for paying subscribers.

WATCH THE EPISODE HERE

New episodes post every Thursday.

For an ad-free version and full transcript, subscribe at TheArgumentMag.com.

Corrections:

  • At 0:17:13, Jerusalem says 2024 U.S. asylum grant rates for “Afghans, Syrians, Cameroonians, Eritreans, and Venezuelans” were at or above 75%. TRAC data shows this was true for Afghans (88.4%) and Eritreans (85.3%), but slightly higher than the rates for Cameroonians (74.4%), Syrians (68.9%), and Venezuelans (64.5%).
  • At 0:17:59, Matt says “I’m sure people who want to move there [the Dominican Republic] from here [the U.S.] have good reasons for it. He means people who want to move from the Dominican Republic to the U.S.
  • At 0:43:00, Jerusalem said the hiring rate in March 2022 was 7.4%, which was actually the job opening rate. The hiring rate was 4.4%.
  • At 0:45:00, Matt says Trump has expanded “H-1A visa programs,” when he means H-2A.
  • At 0:50:06, Jerusalem says that “I think a series of extremely specific and narrow programs is actually incentivizing this gaming the system rather than incentivizing it.” She means those programs incentivize gaming the system rather than addressing it.

Show notes:

  • Border Act of 2024, sponsored by Sens. Chris Murphy, James Lankford, and Kyrsten Sinema, which would have set aside funding for immigration judges and asylum officers in an effort to get processing down to 90 days: Congressional Record, Sen. Murphy page, GovTrack bill text

  • Manhattan Institute study arguing that around $1 billion a year for new judges and asylum officers would solve the asylum crisis: Manhattan Institute report

  • ICE budget document showing an $11.3 billion budget in 2026 (not counting extra funding from the One Big Beautiful Bill): DHS file (PDF)

  • One Billion Americans: The Case for Thinking Bigger, book by Matthew Yglesias making the case for rapidly increasing the U.S. population through immigration, in an effort to reframe the conversation in a more patriotic way: Goodreads page, Amazon page

  • Report showing 84% of Americans support resettling Afghans who helped troops during the war to resettle in the U.S.: With Honor PAC report

  • Polling showing 78% of Americans supported admitting up to 100,000 Ukrainian refugees from the war in 2022: Gallup report

  • Polling showing 66% of Americans supported admitting Kosovar refugees in 1999: Gallup report

  • “Europeans’ support for refugees of varying background is stable over time,” study by Kirk Bansak, Jens Hainmueller, and Dominik Hangartner showing general support for admitting asylum seekers in 15 European countries: Nature article

  • “Why America is so much better than Europe at immigration,” article co-written by Alexander Kustov and Kelsey Piper about the differences between U.S. and European approaches to immigration: The Argument article

  • In Our Interest: How Democracies Can Make Immigration Popular, book by Alexander Kustov examining immigration issues through a strict framework of self-interest: Columbia University Press page, Amazon page

  • Report showing that U.S. asylum seekers from five countries had grant rates at less than 20%: Dominican Republic at 11%, Mexico at 16.6%, Colombia at 19.3%, Ecuador at 19.7%, and Brazil at 19.7%: TRAC report

  • Coverage of Bush-era emphasis on border security: Bush White House archived page, CBS News article

  • “Democrats can’t hide from immigration forever,” 2025 article by Matt Yglesias that described the CHNV immigration parole program as having reduced irregular migration and border chaos: Slow Boring article

  • Article cited by Matt about Nigerian migrants in the U.K. being coached on how to appear gay in order to get asylum: BBC article

  • Coverage of Venezuela hitting 10,000,000% inflation: CNBC article

  • Coverage showing over 70% of Venezuelans are still in their country: UNHCR page, N-IUSSP article

  • Coverage showing around 70% of Syrians stayed in the country even through the war: MSF release

  • Coverage showing over 90% of Sudan’s population remains in the country despite famine, civil war, and internal displacement: International Organization for Migration article, Al Jazeera article

  • “How Deterrence Policies Create Border Chaos,” 2023 article by Jerusalem Demsas arguing that deterrence policies in immigration don’t work: The Atlantic article

  • Chart showing nearly half as many U.S. job openings in 2026 as in 2022: FRED data

  • Chart showing U.S. hiring rate at 3.1% in February 2026 and 4.4% in March 2022: FRED data

  • Quinnipiac poll showing Biden hitting 33% approval in January 2022: The Hill article

  • Coverage of Trump expanding the H-2A visa program for guest agricultural workers: Newsweek article, Time article

  • Coverage of Biden-era labor regulations, which made it more difficult to get an au pair, among other things: The Washington Post article, The Wall Street Journal article, Cato Institute commentary

  • Coverage of Australia having a higher share of foreign-born residents than the U.S. does: McCrindle Research article

  • Coverage of Australia letting in more immigrants, relative to the existing population, than the U.S. does: NPR article, Cato Institute article

  • Coverage of the creation of the CBP One app, which made it possible to set up an appointment with an asylum officer ahead of time: Los Angeles Times article, CBS News article

  • Peer Review: “Germs in the Family: The Short- and Long-Term Consequences of Intra-Household Disease Spread,” by N. Meltem Daysal, Hui Ding, Maya Rossin-Slater, and Hannes Schwandt, showing younger siblings have much higher rates of hospitalization due to respiratory conditions contracted from older siblings: NBER working paper (PDF)

  • Study showing that households with at least two children have an illness at least 50% of the time: Clinical Infectious Diseases article

  • Studies showing that, on average, younger siblings…

    • Have higher delinquency rates: NBER working paper
    • Have less educational attainment: Intelligence study
    • Have lower lifetime earnings: PNAS Nexus study, The Times article

  • Studies showing that, on average, winter birth dates are…

    • Slightly worse for life outcomes (higher risk of schizophrenia, asthma, or cardiovascular disease): Current Biology article, The Lancet article, JAMIA article
    • Slightly better for life outcomes (longer lifespan): PNAS article, The Science of Nature article, PLoS One article, Demographic Research article

  • Next week’s reading: The Feminine Mystique by Betty Friedan: Goodreads page, Amazon page

(Illustration by The Argument, image by David Dee Delgado/Stringer via Getty)

Transcript

Matthew Yglesias

What do we do? How do we get up off the floor of Trumpism? Because I think we saw this experiment with Biden of being like, “The witch is dead. You know, we’re free again.” And it was a fucking catastrophe. Like, right away.

Jerusalem Demsas

Hi, I’m Jerusalem Demsas.

Matthew Yglesias

I’m Matthew Yglesias.

Jerusalem Demsas

And welcome to The Argument. This is a show where two friends argue about politics, policy, and whatever else we’re interested in that week. This week, Matt has a take.

Matthew Yglesias

I do. I think that asylum is not really something that we can or should bring back from Donald Trump and the Trump era.

Jerusalem Demsas

It’s a depressing take, Matt. Depressing take. Why?

Matthew Yglesias

Because, I mean, I think that the concept of a legal right to asylum, as it was enshrined in the post-World War II era, worked well enough for a long time. But it was very small. Very few people were actually taking advantage of the right to asylum. And then, over time — to put a little bit of a slightly arbitrary number on it — but starting in 2014, in the United States and then propelling forward, it became more and more of a loophole, essentially, right? A thing where people had learned — immigration lawyers in the United States, people smugglers, would-be immigrants — this was a way to gain entry.

Jerusalem Demsas

Let’s just — from first principles here. What is the point of asylum?

Matthew Yglesias

Well, I mean, there’s the point of asylum…

Jerusalem Demsas

I just mean people may not know what the loophole is.

Matthew Yglesias

Sure, OK. Because I think there’s two things here, right? The purpose of asylum is to help people in need. The functioning of asylum…

Jerusalem Demsas

But specifically, a political asylum was created for people who had a well-founded fear of persecution. And this is largely in the context of political persecution from the state. So, whether it’s people fleeing communism or the idea that you’d be fleeing, the fact that you were going to be genocided by your own government.

That’s the idea of political asylum, not just helping random people around the world.

Matthew Yglesias

Sure, yes. But I also want to draw a distinction because there’s the concept of a refugee, right? Which is: This is a person who we are going to allow to come live in our country because they’re facing some kind of problems in their homeland, and we’re helping them out. It’s humanitarian immigration.

But refugee resettlement exists in a numerical framework, right? A country says, “We are willing to resettle X number of refugees, and, you know, we do it.” And that’s like many different things that governments do to try to be helpful actors in the world. The idea of asylum is that it’s a procedural legal right.

There’s an immigration officer and they’re like, “You’re not here legally. We’re going to deport you now.” But then you can say, “No, I need asylum here,” right? And it’s supposed to be like any kind of judicial legal process, just judged on the basis of the facts, right? It’s not a discretionary government decision — how many people are going to get asylum? It’s, if the asylum claims are valid on the facts and the law, you get it, right?

So, what started to happen is that when very few people are making asylum claims, the asylum claims are adjudicated very expeditiously, and maybe not that many people are being granted asylum. And it just goes on and on for years and years and years as a low-level aspect of the American immigration system.

But when I — I graduated college in 2003, I covered the 2007 immigration reform debate. I covered the 2013 immigration reform debate. I covered a lot of arguments that were happening between then and now.

We were talking, almost exclusively during those periods, we were talking about what to do with the stock of illegal immigrants living in the country and what should we do about, you know, ongoing flows of illegal immigrants.

And then we started talking about legal immigration, like visa allocation. But shortly after the 2013 compromise fell apart, we started getting more and more debate about asylum claims. At first, it’s because people were sending, sort of, teenagers alone to come to the United States, because there were legal restrictions on the government’s ability to, you know, incarcerate children essentially.

Right? So if you came and you were caught and you made an asylum claim, then while that was being processed through the system, you could be released to a relative or a family friend or something like that. Which, again, if you’re thinking about one unaccompanied minor who shows up at the southern border, that totally seems like the correct way to handle the situation.

Right? It’s like, that’s what I would do. I think it’s what anybody would do. But it became a flood of people because it became clear that…

Jerusalem Demsas

I mean, it wasn’t that many people in the grand scheme of things. We’re talking about in [the] tens of thousands, we’re not talking about anywhere near…

Matthew Yglesias

But it was too many people for the system to handle, right? Because the system was built on the presumption…

Jerusalem Demsas

But I think this is actually a really good place because the fact is that there are many people who are in bad situations that would not qualify as political asylum, right? They’re not like, “my government is not trying to genocide me. They’re not trying to destroy me,” but are in a bad situation that anyone would be like, “Yeah, it doesn’t seem good.”

Maybe you’re in a domestic violence situation. Maybe you’re in a situation where there’s a bunch of crime in your country and it’s really unsafe and it’s bad, but you’re not specifically being targeted, or you’re just, like, really, really poor, and you’re looking for a better job.

People can understand that that’s not a good thing. And you might want to go search for a better life here. And they notice that the asylum system might be a place for them to say, “OK, well, I am following, you know, maybe there’s a smuggler or there’s some sort of an informal economy that’s been set up to help me get into the United States. And they tell me to go across the U.S. southern border, go up to an American official and say, ‘Hey, I’m here for asylum,’ and then someone will help me figure it out. In the meantime, I’ll probably be able to get a work permit and be able to stay here for a few years.”

And the fact that that happens, I think is like, not really contested at this point.

What I think is strange is the turn from “there’s clearly a problem here” to “the whole system doesn’t work.” Because, to me the number, like the amount of money it would take to actually make it possible to run the backlog through is negligible. It’s a drop in the pond of the U.S. federal budget.

So, the Lankford, Murphy, Sinema border act was talking about $1 billion a year for asylum officers, another $1 billion a year for immigration court judges. They thought they could get that down to — like, processing those claims — under 90 days. In that case, I think that’s a little optimistic.

But even if you tripled that, even if it was like $3 billion and not $1 billion each, that’s like nothing in the federal budget. And the Manhattan Institute, not a liberal outlet, themselves also had very similar estimates, about $1 billion a year. It would take a little bit longer when there’s a backlog built up, because you can’t just hire thousands of officers.

But I don’t get how you go from “There’s a problem with the system” to “Just throw it out.”

Matthew Yglesias

Well, I mean, I think the question is: What are we trying to do with our billion dollars? Because say it’s not $1 billion. Say it’s $3 billion, right? Same difference; it’s not that much money, right? Three billion dollars on addressing a significant problem is money well spent.

Jerusalem Demsas

And we spend like, $11 billion on ICE right now, right? So it’s not like we’re not spending money on immigration.

Matthew Yglesias

So the question is: What is the problem? What are we trying to do, right? And what I remember well during Trump’s first term — I was doing The Weeds then. We had a number of immigration episodes over the years. Dara Lind, who was one of my cohosts for most of those episodes, she was a real immigration specialist. And I think [it’s] also clear that she and the people in the immigration advocacy community, broadly speaking, they would concede that things were going kind of off the rails, right?

And we should do something to fix the system. But what they really wanted was to make sure that a decently large number of people would get these claims positively adjudicated. And we had a lot of expansion, actually, of the eligible categories, right? The whole deal…

Jerusalem Demsas

What we’re talking about here is how to set up — I agree with you that a lot of people who care about immigration, including myself, are sort of like, “Alright, more people come in: great.” Like, that seems good to me because I have broader views about whether or not it’s good for immigration to come in or not.

But on the merits itself, I think that — and what I hear you getting at is that — OK, maybe we can fix this problem with a few billion a year and then people don’t think there’s a loophole anymore, and then there are fewer people trying to come in through asylum.

But like, what are we — why do we even have asylum to begin with? I think asylum on the merits is a good thing that many Americans actually do think should exist.

Matthew Yglesias

Well, there’s the question, right? Because again, when it was small, you know, things that are small by definition are not that big of a deal, right? And so if you get a future where like 400 people are making asylum claims per year and six of them are getting in, I’m not gonna…

Jerusalem Demsas

Four hundred’s a little bit…

Matthew Yglesias

Yeah. But I’m just saying the process that I started having during this time, right, is on the one hand, I was writing various articles that are like, “These anti-immigration people are being kind of crazy. They are wildly exaggerating the downsides of immigration, and they are wildly underestimating the upsides of immigration.”

And, you know, eventually I get the idea of writing a book that is going to pull together a bunch of different things that — I’m interested in housing and transportation, but also immigration, right? It’s called One Billion Americans. And it was like, an effort to, you know…

Jerusalem Demsas

The original Abundance.

Matthew Yglesias

Reframe a lot of these ideas in sort of patriotic national greatness terms. And what happens when you write a book is you need to — you know, the whole book is mostly just stuff I already thought. But you need to put your ideas together in a more systematic, more holistic way.

Jerusalem Demsas

You actually don’t have to. You can just publish a bad book.

Matthew Yglesias

Well, fair enough. You know, and decide what you actually think, right? And the more I dug into, like, “No, morons, immigration is good. Immigration is good for America,” the more I found myself pivoting on the other side of it to like, immigration policy should be designed to maximize the national interest, and the sort of proceduralism and reactiveness of asylum does not fit into that very well.

Jerusalem Demsas

I think this is a bit weird. I mean, do you think this is true for all criminal justice? Do you think that the criminal justice system should not have procedural rights?

Matthew Yglesias

It’s not that the criminal justice system shouldn’t have procedural rights. But, you know, as I always say: The criminals get rights to protect the innocent.

Jerusalem Demsas

And because people have rights even if they’ve committed crimes.

Matthew Yglesias

Well, yes, but when you’re convicted, you’re punished in ways that you wouldn’t punish an innocent for sure.

Jerusalem Demsas

Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias

So just trying to do first principles and think about not, you know, me being less reactive as a writer, right? Instead of me saying, “Well, you know, if we look at the Hmong refugees in Minneapolis, you get such and such outcome and it’s actually broadly good.” If you were like, “How are we going to maximize economic growth in the Twin Cities over the next 50 years?” It’s like…

Jerusalem Demsas

But this is such a narrow view of national interest.

Matthew Yglesias Yeah.

Jerusalem Demsas

Well, no, but I’m saying if you ask individual people — this is why I think that the immigration public opinion debate has gotten way too black and white here.

Matthew Yglesias

This is why I’m trying to stay away from public opinion.

Jerusalem Demsas

But my point is, when you think about national interest, it’s also what people believe to be a good outcome for their country. It’s not just like we just look at GDP, which if we did, maybe we wouldn’t have an asylum seeker system per se, but we would have a lot more immigrants, and many of the same people would probably come in through a different system.

But it’s also like, hey, I want to live in a country that actually reflects my values. And I want to be clear, this is not just Jerusalem doing her lib bullshit — which is like, I’m also doing that — but when you look at systematic efforts to try to figure out when and why people support different kinds of groups coming in asylum, you largely see that there’s actually a clear pattern of deserving-ness.

And people will probably be surprised by this. But when you look at Afghan translators, right, you had like, 84% support for resettling Afghan asylum seekers because people thought there’s a deserving-ness aspect. They’d helped the United States. This isn’t new. Ukrainian refugees: Seventy-eight percent of Americans approved admitting up to 100,000 Ukrainian refugees.

It’s not 400, Matt. It’s like 100,000 people. And this is old — even older, when we had a much more anti-immigration country — 66% of Americans approved admitting ethnic Albanian refugees from Kosovo. And again, this is not just the United States. Even in far more anti-immigration countries:

There’s a great study by Bansak, Hainmueller, and Hangartner, which I’m probably saying that was all wrong. They look at 15 European countries. And they, again, see this over and over again that people are willing to be in favor of this. And so when I think about shifting the asylum system, the problem to me is that we’ve conceived of it as “There are all of these people in the world; the masses of the world are coming to your doorstep, and they’re in need.”

And it’s like a charity, like you have to let them in and you have to let them in no matter what it does. And from that aspect, I totally agree with you.

I think that entire framing is is wrong; I think harmed by the fact that we’re often not making clear discrete cases for specific groups of people that are either in the national interest, like Afghan translators, where it’s clearly, like, we want people like this to be able to help the American military or the American government when there are foreign entanglements that we’re getting involved in for bad reasons, we still want people translating for us. Or for other reasons, like there’s not a huge reason to care about Albanian refugees, other than the fact that we thought that it was bad what was happening to them.

Matthew Yglesias

I don’t want to overly interrogate the specifics of the polling on this kind of stuff. But I want to draw a distinction, right? When we were talking about doing this episode, one framing I toyed with was that humanitarian immigration is, like, got to go, right? Because I’ve been reading — Well, I was actually surprised when The Argument published an Alexander Kustov piece because I think his views…

Jerusalem Demsas

I publish a lot of people.

Matthew Yglesias

…on immigration. No, no, no, I know. So, I mean, he has got a book about the politics of immigration where he is very exclusively tethered to self-interest in a very narrow kind of conception. I wanted to pivot my claim to the asylum concept, right? Because, again, the nature of the asylum concept is that it is legal and procedural and it is rights-based.

I think the Afghan translator situation is a thing where the case one would make for it — I mean, not just to anybody, right? Common sense is that there is an obligation incurred by the United States of America as a polity when other people work with us in a military effort. And so we should help those people out.

It’s a way of paying faith forward. If the United States had never invaded Afghanistan or — forgetting the United States — you just imagine some other country, right? Like, there’s a war between, you know, Kazakhstan and Tajikistan or something, and there’s a bunch of Tajik translators who are working with the Kazakh army.

And then the Kazakhs leave, and then the Tajik translators are getting in trouble back inTajikistan. The case for the United States to take those people in is much weaker than the case for the Afghan translators, because they were working with our military, right? So this is like:We can have immigration for lots of different kinds of reasons, but it’s still purposeful, intentional, selected, right?

Jerusalem Demsas

But I think this actually presumes that the existing asylum system isn’t actually good at determining when asylum cases are or are not legitimate. So, you look at the 2024 data about asylum grant rates, and you have 11% for Dominican Republic nationals, 16.6% for Mexicans, 19.3% for Colombians, 19.7% for Ecuadorians.

And then on the other end, for Afghans, Syrians, Cameroonians, Eritreans, and Venezuelans, It’s like, 75-plus percent. And that tracks [with] my basic understanding of which of these countries has a lot of people who are going to be fitting under the specific narrow definition of political asylum. And so, our existing system is actually quite good.

And I think that you’re underestimating how difficult it would be for the government toselect those people rather than ask them to petition themselves to us.

Matthew Yglesias

But I don’t read those numbers the same way that you do. Because, go back, like the Dominican and Mexican numbers.

Jerusalem Demsas

Yeah. It’s like 11% for Dominican Republic.

Matthew Yglesias

Right. So, like, the Dominican Republic — a country with problems — I’m sure people who want to move there from here have good reasons for it. But the Dominican Republic is a democracy.

Jerusalem Demsas

I mean, I don’t know what’s going on there, but I think we’d have to know the facts a bit more to make claims that they don’t actually fit under political asylum.

Matthew Yglesias

But this is what I’m saying, though. They are clearly fitting under political asylum in the existing legal structure of what political asylum looks like. And I think it does not look like a reasonable system — that is, that some fraction of people from Latin American, middle-income, imperfect Latin American democracies, are successfully making asylum petitions is itself, like, that is the broken system that is not selective immigration in the national interest.

Jerusalem Demsas

I guess this is my thing: I understand that you, Matt Yglesias, believe this, but I’m saying I don’t think this is actually the viewpoint of Americans.

Matthew Yglesias

I mean, I don’t know what is, I mean…

Jerusalem Demsas

I’m just saying, like, you’re just…

Matthew Yglesias

So I’m trying to not have a discussion just about politics because I think it’s clear, if you look at the politics of the United States and every other country and basically every election for the past generation, that the real public support for immigration is lower than whatever is in these polls and these academic studies. Like, this relentless growth of far-right political parties, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah.

But I’m trying to stick to the merits of this, right? The case for the Afghan translators is that I don’t need to know anything about the specific details. We all remember that that war happened, and we all remember that American forces departed. And we all know that the Taliban is bad.

So for any given asylum applicant, there’s a factual question like “Was this person, in fact, a translator who cooperated with the American military?” But “What is the normative case?” It’s completely transparent, right?

Similarly, if you wanted to say, “OK, we want to say, categorically, if you are a defector from the People’s Republic of China, that we want you in.”

And you would have to write out some specifications of, like, what does that mean? How are we going to make sure you’re not a double agent or a spy or something like that? But that would be a policy, right? The Dominican Republic is like — you could just go there on vacation.

We have a free trade agreement with the Dominican Republic. They’re not like a formal treaty ally, but this is a friendly country. It is not a state that the United States of America regards as a despotic adversary, where we are trying to, like, cultivate a Dominican opposition.

Jerusalem Demsas

I understand. My thing is just — I think that, to be clear, I think that the argument that you are laying out now is very… we can call it ending asylum. But many people call this reforming the asylum system.

Matthew Yglesias

Yeah, but that would be a boring podcast.

Jerusalem Demsas

OK.

Matthew Yglesias

This is why I consider it ending rather than reform: With most immigration, there is a centrally directed, top-down quantitative limit on what is happening, right? It is not a proceduralist, rights-based claim, the way that asylum is, right? The way that asylum works — anybody who is physically present in the United States and is being faced with deportation can say, “No, I deserve asylum.”

Jerusalem Demsas

The only reason that matters at all is because the system is incapable of keeping up with those claims. If someone was like, “Oh yeah, give me asylum right now.” And then it took, you know, we put you in detention for three months and then we’re like, “No, this is completely fake,” and then we deported you. And because now you’ve been deported, rather than leaving on your own, it’s more difficult to come back to the United States. Like, this would just end the problem we’re talking about. But the question then becomes to me, what is the purpose of… Because I understand what you’re saying.

I’m saying many of the problems that you’re outlining here, to me, just seem very clearly like problems with the implementation of how the system is working right now. And I think this is not just the thing that we’ve already talked about with fixing all the judges or whatever, which I know you agree with, but it’s also just like, if you expanded the legal pathways in our national interest, like you made it possible for businesses to hire workers that they needed when labor markets were incredibly tight like they were during the Biden years, etc., many of these people would just not be coming through this pathway. They would just take the legal pathways that were available to them. That’s the literal thing that you would expect to happen, because that’s what happened before.

People were using the system. They were using the other sorts of legal pathways that were available. And then they transferred over to asylum when that was no longer available.

Matthew Yglesias

I mean, what would help? Part of my interpretation of the increase in asylum claims starting in the late aughts is that there was this big push for border security starting in the later years of the Bush administration.

I think we essentially succeeded in making it harder to sneak across the border to work illegally. And then we had the Great Recession. So there was a decline in demand, and we continued investing in the border security rollout during a period of years when there was very little demand to emigrate. And it worked.

And as is often the case with these things, it sort of backfired because the fundamental dynamic of, “people want to move to the United States to work here when the unemployment rate is low and businesses like to hire people,” remained in place. So it’s like, the water starts trying to flow around the dam.

And what they found was this asylum thing. I definitely favor trying to create both more generous visa programs for skilled workers, other things like that, but also more, I guess we’re calling it rotational labor rather than guest workers, but particularly during a labor market boom, there should be a system by which companies can recruit temporary workers to come live here and do jobs.

And, hopefully, if that is in place and that is working well, it reduces people’s inclination to want to try to find backdoors and loopholes in the system.

Jerusalem Demsas

Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias

We’re both agreeing and I think the Biden administration tried a, like, chewing gum and masking tape version of this with a lot of parole programs and, like, quasi-legal immigration to try to tamp down on the chaos element. And it sort of worked and sort of didn’t.

And if we had, like, a real Congress that did things, we could put together a better version of those kinds of systems. All the more, that’s leaving me with not really seeing a big role.

Jerusalem Demsas

Well, because there’s a bunch of things that are unknowns that you don’t want to just leave to Congress to have to legislate on a dime about in order to make sure that you can actually meet your national interest needs in the moment. The Trump administration right now is destroying … as many ways as possible for there to be legal pathways that the president can quickly spin up.

The parole programs — which you praised in a Slow Boring article many years ago as having actually reduced irregular migration when we had them at the CHNV program that the Biden administration stood up to actually process people and make sure they were eligible in these specific four countries to come to the United States — that program has not only been canceled by the Trump administration that’s been rubber stamped by a Supreme Court at this point, which means the process that you’re talking about, where you could just have the president go “OK, well, now we know we want to help this specific country because of this national interest,” that has been derailed.

And now what we’re left with is the asylum system and Congress. And so, like, in Matt’s world, there’s a crisis happening somewhere, and your entire faith is on the fact that Congress will pass something in time in order to do our national interest well. And, to be clear, when Congress fails to do this, at some point, the chaos that results is what actually creates the anti-immigration policy.

Matthew Yglesias

Well, that’s not… Yes, clearly Congress sucks. Everything sucks. You know, the next president is going to have to deal with a chaotic and difficult situation under whatever the circumstances are. We can’t really know what they’re saying.

What I’m saying is that, if we got to open up the box and have Congress change immigration laws, I would be there in the committee, wherever it is, I would be saying, “We need more visas for skilled workers. We need to completely uncap certain categories of programs. We need to create some legal guest worker programs, things like that. We need to preserve some level of presidential discretion around different kinds of things.”

But if restrictionists are coming to me and they’re like, “but we gotta scrap these asylum courts, you know, we gotta tell people if you’re transiting anywhere,” that’s where I’m like, “Yeah, you know, that makes sense.” Especially in the context of “We’re making these reforms to change, to create more selective, more organized pathways.

I just don’t see a strong argument for the proposition that we should be letting random immigration judges look at cases that they feel sympathetic about and saying, like, “Yeah, you know, 16% of Mexicans are getting their their claims approved.” Because, again, Mexico, it’s one of these countries where, like, obviously there are lots of profound problems in Mexico. You know, there’s a very serious interplay between organized crime and state governments in Mexico. It’s also just much poorer than the United States.

I get why people want to come here. And if I was empowered, right, if a specific Mexican individual was sitting in front of me and I was empowered to give that person the right to live and work in the United States, I would feel incredibly tempted to just give it to them, right?

Because I’m a humane person, I care about the world. I don’t want to send someone back to be deported, to deal with cartel shit and all kinds of other terrible stuff. But, again, Mexico is a democracy. Mexico is a friendly country. We have a free trade agreement with Mexico. We have a formal diplomatic process with Mexico.

I don’t think it makes sense to say that there are political refugees from Mexico or from the Dominican Republic, or things like that. If we want to create as part of our foreign policy apparatus, like, selective, humanitarian immigration pathways, that makes sense. But it has to be integrated with the whole rest of our foreign policy outlook.

Jerusalem Demsas

So I just asked Claude why Mexican asylum cases are coming through.

Matthew Yglesias

Are we just gonna have AI resolve these questions?

Jerusalem Demsas

Well, we don’t have time to do — I didn’t look into this. I didn’t think it would be relevant.

Matthew Yglesias

I’m just going to pop in and say, “is asylum good?”

Jerusalem Demsas

Claude’s gonna say yes.

Matthew Yglesias

You let me know what Claude says about Mexico.

Jerusalem Demsas

Apparently it’s a few cases. One, it’s journalists covering cartels or corruption. So they’re journalists who can document specific published stories about cartels and government corruption and specific threats tied to those stories, the Mexican government’s inability or unwillingness to protect them, and often the murder of colleagues or family members. Those are the ones who’re getting grants.

The second are LGBTQ asylum seekers. So it is folks who have shown, particularly from states with actual patterns of violence against these people. So it’s not, like, anywhere in Mexico, because obviously, some places it’s not it’s not as much of a problem.

And third, it’s family members of cartel targets, which is very, very conditional on being able to prove this. And, to be clear, 84% of these cases, of Mexican asylum claims, are not succeeding. I just think it’s very weird to look at that and go, “these judges are, like, real soft-hearted libs who can’t get over the fact this person is begging not to be sent back.”

And again, I don’t want to make this — I agree it’s not about public opinion here. But when we’re conceiving of what is in the national interest it is in the national interest to have a country with reasonable values. I think that people don’t want their countries to be completely without any level of care for issues that Americans care about, like freedom of speech and journalists being able to cover a literal cartel that’s causing spillover problems in multiple American states.

I mean, that’s a category where I think it’s actually really just like — I think that if the American president got up and was like, “Hey, it’s really important that people cover this horrible shit that cartels are doing. It’s creating problems in Arizona. It’s creating problems in New Mexico, creating problems in Texas. We think these are bad people. Look at how they’re murdering the families of journalists, the Mexican government. We’re leaning on them to protect these journalists, but they can’t do it, or they won’t do it for whatever reason. And if they come to this country, we think we should give them asylum claims.”

I agree that someone should do that rather than let it just happen without any kind of explanation to the public. But I don’t think that actually means getting rid of the system or expecting Congress to legislate around these very specific categories.

Matthew Yglesias

OK. But I feel like this is maybe more convergent than you’re seeing.

Jerusalem Demsas

What should happen? Let’s say in your world, there’s no asylum. Congress has not made some specific carve out for a journalist in Mexico and a journalist for Mexico who’s credibly done great work or whatever comes to the United States and says, “Help me, they’re about to murder me.” You think what should happen?

Jerusalem Demsas

I just don’t think that people think that that person should just be sent back to be murdered.

Matthew Yglesias

I agree with that, but this is my point, though. These are sympathetic individual cases, right? And in the case of Mexicans, I think you have the strongest claim, because the United States is obviously adjacent to Mexico, right?

I mean, no country enjoys it when a bunch of refugees cross the border because it’s a civil war in the adjacent country. But in almost every case, the view is that you can’t just put the guards at the border and be like, “No, you have to come…” That’s like, immediate crisis, something, something. You know, I mean, I was very struck. Again, this was during…

Jerusalem Demsas

That’s asylum, Matt. What you just said is asylum.

Matthew Yglesias

To an extent. I’ve been to San Antonio and seen people who had come from Congo into South America, over land, through Mexico, to the southern border, apprehended there. CBP has limited language capacities. And so they bused those people to San Antonio, which is the big city that’s closest to the border, where they have more translators.

You got the Congolese translators there, and what the people are asking to do is be sent to Maine, where there’s a Congolese community and they have relatives, things like that.

Some of those people, I think, have invalid claims. Some of them have claims that, like, [there are] probably just a lot of problems in Congo. You know what I mean?

People who are saying they’re being persecuted, stuff like that, that’s not BS, right? But it’s also not imminent emergency, right?

When you’re like, going from continent to continent, across oceans, across multiple countries, right? It’s different from the kind of classic refugees at the border. Again, to me, this is just an example of a system where what was written down in policy in the years following World War II was meant for a set of circumstances that has stopped applying in the modern world, and it requires changes that are tantamount to…

I mean, look, if for some reason it goes down better with libs to call this reforms to asylum, I’m all for that. But, like, in your Mexican case, you know, the United States of America, whether through discretion granted to the executive branch or explicit congressional action, has policy toward Mexico, right? A country that is nearby. The cartel activity in Mexico is a significant public policy issue in both the United States and Mexico.

Different administrations on both sides of the border have had different approaches to that. Saying, as part of Mexico cartel policy, “we want to establish a process whereby people whose lives are at risk from the cartels can get refuge in the United States,” that could make some sense. And you would have to think about what are the rules of that program.

What’s its exact purpose? Are we trying to get people to cooperate with us in legal cases? Are we trying to make the Mexican government like us more? What are we trying to do here? Right? Versus a system where it’s just how objectively threatened you are, is the criteria. Because I just don’t think that that fundamentally makes sense.

If you talk about one person, like, I’m a humane person, I get it. But when you start getting to, “oh, this is overwhelmed, oh, we need more judges, blah blah blah,” like, yes, to an extent. But also, we need a policy. We need to say, like, how many people are we taking in? Why are we taking them in? What are the categories of eligibility?

I don’t know, did you read the story that the BBC did recently about Nigerians going to the U.K. and being coached into how to pretend that they’re gay?

Jerusalem Demsas

OK. Again, I think that the places of overlap between our positions are largely that we think it’s really important for a case to be specifically made about the people that we’re letting in, and that the system — the mass majority of immigrants should not be coming in in some sort of system where they’ve never been justified on the grounds that coming to the United States, I think that is completely reasonable.

I think we live in a democracy. There should be, clearly, some sort of public reasoning about what we’re doing because it’s an obligation we have to citizens and residents of this country to be clear on what’s going on and why, and what the process actually looks like and how decisions are being made.

That’s just a fundamental right — or a fundamental way that liberal democracy is supposed to work. But I think there are a few things I want to draw here, because I think that what’s different about our positions is a few things. One, I think discretion with really high-impact cases is actually really important.

I mean, most of my work is about places that the government should have way less discretion because, like, what the fuck are you doing? Every single house does not need to go through a historical design review process in order to decide whether or not you should be allowed to build it or not.

If it’s a triplex or a quadplex, like, who gives a shit? Let’s keep going because there’s no point in — like the error rate, right? If you err on the side of building, you know, if you accidentally build a house that was slightly outside the design purview or like, the cost, they’re not that great relative to the cost of running a system with high discretion.

But that’s different with immigration, Matt. With immigration, the stakes are incredibly, incredibly high and the cost of running the system is actually not that high. So now, we have a situation where instead of like the housing situation or other kinds of areas where we want to reduce discretion, we have a situation where the stakes for getting it wrong are that people die in ways that no one would want to see happen, or American interests are undermined.

And let’s be clear here, it’s not just like, “Oh, it’s boo hoo. It’s really sad that someone gets murdered somewhere else.” If America deports someone to be murdered by a horrible regime, it’s actually a problem for our national interest. It’s not a good outcome for our ability to do diplomatic relations, to do whatever.

There’s lots of things that create issues. If that’s a news story that goes viral — and again, those things actually do make Americans, I think, set Americans apart, in really not wanting to see their country do that.

But I think that, implicit to a lot of what you’re saying, and I think I hear this explicitly from other people, is there’s this expectation that if you allow this to happen, like, OK, it’s $1 billion now to make the system work. It’s $2 billion. But soon every single one in the world is going to come to our doorstep. And we’re not going to be able to process that because that will be the entire GDP of America. I think this is fundamentally founded on an incorrect premise about how many people actually want to migrate.

Matthew Yglesias

So how many people want to migrate?

Jerusalem Demsas

I mean, you can look at, like the really hard case, for instance, Venezuela, right, where you have, like, 10,000,000% inflation. It’s really bad. Whatever. I think it’s like, 70-plus percent of the country don’t even leave. 70% of people don’t leave.

Also in like, Sudan or in Syria this happens. And I think less than 5% leave the immediate region even in the worst situation imaginable, like, a Syria-level state breakdown where like, actively, people are going to be murdered if they stay, 70 plus percent stay, and then all of the people who leave, like, like, single digit percentages leave the immediate area.

Matthew Yglesias

Plenty of Syrians, enough Syrians left the immediate area to, like, destabilize all of European politics for multiple years.

Jerusalem Demsas

It’s a question of where you want to put the problem. To me, and this is my fundamental view of the majority of the immigration backlash, is that it is fundamentally founded in chaos, not in specific numbers. And you can see this because in Australia, which is like every immigration mod’s favorite country.

Matthew Yglesias

Love Australia. I’ve never been, but…

Jerusalem Demsas

Australia’s system is, from America’s perspective, quite draconian. They can do this for a couple of reasons: One is that they’re obviously an island, but secondly, that most of the people who are trying to come to Australia are trying to come on boats from usually, like, Indonesia, and Indonesia has mostly been willing to take those people back if Australians just, like, return them on boats back.

Notably, when Indonesia decides not to do this, it creates a massive political backlash within Australia. So even in Australia right now, you see far right populist backlash happening in a country where we’re talking about extremely small numbers of irregular migration causing chaos.

And so, to me, it’s like unless you can generate a system which no one can — in which you can eliminate all ability for anyone to come at any point without expectation — then you need to have a system that can process unexpected numbers of people coming in.

And the judges that can dictate “Hey, should you come in or should you not,” that’s the whole point of having a discretionary system.

Matthew Yglesias

But this is why we’re talking about this at all now, because, you know, when Biden was president, right, there was all this hemming and hawing, and he said we needed new legislation, and blah, blah, blah.

I think you wrote a piece about how, like, deterrence won’t work, you know? etc., etc. Like, Trump has become president, and whatever problems are happening in the United States of America now — there are many — we are not grappling with large quantities of people making asylum claims.

Jerusalem Demsas

Okay, sorry, I agree if you make the United States of America so unappealing for people to come to, there will be a small break in people attempting to come here until they figure out other pathways of entry.

And you know how I know this? Because in Trump 1, they literally started separating families from one another. And you saw increases of families coming to the border under Donald Trump. Why? Because the American economy bounced back in 2019, and the labor market pulled a bunch of people in.

Matthew Yglesias

To an extent, but…

Jerusalem Demsas

Not to an extent. Despite all of the draconian measures we remember from Trump 1, despite all of those things happening, the highest number of irregular arrivals at the Southwest border of all measurable time happened in 2019 under Donald Trump.

Matthew Yglesias

But right now, the unemployment rate is very low.

Jerusalem Demsas

Well, the hiring rate isn’t.

Matthew Yglesias

And we’re seeing, like, I think we can argue which direction this is going. But there’s negative migration, basically.

Jerusalem Demsas

I wrote this piece which Matt called out, which is about deterrence not working. When we say deterrence doesn’t work, we’re not saying there’s no way to pause the number of people coming in. But what we also observe from places, for instance, like the Mediterranean, where in Europe, you know, way more draconian than what Trump’s doing. They’re, like, literally trying to capsize people’s boats coming over.

People figure out other pathways, and they try to figure out where the system has stabilized in order to figure out what next to do. The pulls and pushes are still going to exist even under these regimes. And notably, I mean, I’m just putting this out here and, you know, job openings are like, half what they were in 2022, and the hiring rate is 3.1, whereas the hiring rate in March 2022 was like, 7.4%.

Matthew Yglesias

I am not, like, an anti-immigration maximalist.

Jerusalem Demsas

I agree.

Matthew Yglesias

I want to get more immigration to the United States. But I think the baseline is important to understand, right? Because we are now not having the Biden debate. We’re not having the Trump 1 debate. We’re not having the Obama debate. We’re having a debate about, you know, say…

Jerusalem Demsas

Would you recommend that a Democratic president have like 33% approval rating?

Matthew Yglesias

Absolutely not. I’m saying somebody new is taking over and it’s like — or maybe it’s not even somebody new, right? Donald Trump is just phoning me up. He’s like, “Matt, what should I do? I gotta, I gotta improve my numbers.” And I would say, just like, chill on the interior enforcement. Just let it slide a little bit, you know?

Like, who cares, right? And then I would say to him, “Your proposed H-1B reforms, some of those ideas make sense, but some of them are counterproductive. And if you can make H-1B better, you ought to expand the program.”

Jerusalem Demsas

But the presumption here — I’m not even talking about, like, yes, you should tell Trump good policy ideas if he calls you up. In that case, I’m not objecting to you doing that. But what I’m saying is that the politics of immigration are such that it actually does not serve anti-immigration forces to fix these procedural problems anytime there’s a procedural issue.

Matthew Yglesias

I agree, but this is why I’m trying to come up with good advice. I’m just like, “How do you improve from this baseline where people have been quite chilled from coming to the United States?”

Something Trump has done that I think is good and that Joe Biden should have done is that he has expanded H-1A agricultural worker visas, and he has also weakened the Department of Labor regulation of those people. The Biden administration, because it was so screwy, like, the only restrictionist things it did were, like, labor regulations, right?

They made it harder to get an au pair, stuff like that. And you should drop all that stuff. Take all the well-organized channels and try to broaden them. Because, like, chaos is bad, etc., etc., etc. The thing about asylum, right? Like, I don’t want to make this sound excessively narrow, but I really am harping on the proceduralism of it because I think that you are inviting chaos, right?

The difference between saying we are going to allow 700 LGBT Mexicans from the following three states to immigrate, because somebody told us that this is a bad situation and people feel sympathetic, blah, blah, blah, fine — And like, which states, which are the right number? I don’t know, you can negotiate it — but like, if there’s a number and there’s a region and it is set out in advance, that is fine.

People can then apply, you can do the thing, you can have it. If you, say, if you show up illegally and then argue ex post, “No, I should have protection,” and you and your lawyer are allowed to make a reason…

Jerusalem Demsas

It’s not illegal to request asylum. A lot of asylum seekers come here on legal visas and then request asylum. I just want to put…

Matthew Yglesias

Sure. There’s that too.

I tried to ask you about this before, but like, the story about the U.K. and the, sort of, Nigerian gay status fraud question, right? Where it’s like, the incentive…

Jerusalem Demsas

There will always be an incentive to figure out a way to get into a country where you can like — without asylum — to 20x your life’s potential outcome. That will always exist.

Without asylum or with asylum, it will exist.

Matthew Yglesias

It’s important, I think, to try to create immigration pathways that do not become invitations for rulebreak.

Jerusalem Demsas

Anything you could possibly imagine — The O-1 visa is gamed. The H-1B visa is gamed — like, there’s not the system that you’re talking about where you have a very specific set of metrics, and then someone has too…

Matthew Yglesias

But people have to apply from abroad, right? You’re avoiding the chaos. Like, I think that you are big chaos theorist in terms of immigration, right? It’s about the chaos. It’s about the disorder. It’s not about the numbers. But then, I think you are shying away from the implications of that.

Go back to the Australian case that you talked about, right? You talked about how draconian the Australian system is on asylum seekers. Australia also has a significantly higher foreign-born population share than the United States. Right? Like, Australia is a big pro-immigration success story on a sustainable basis.

I think they put into effect, like, Jerusalem-ism, right? It is fanatical about maintaining order. And you are correct that even very quantitatively small breakdowns in the orderliness of the Australian system create big shockwaves to the politics.

And I think you had something similar in Canada, which is not literally an island, but has some of the same kind of geographic separation, and for a long time, Canada had a very, very high foreign-born population share without a lot of political dissent around it.

And the Trudeau government started flipping some switches and pulling some levers and changing things up. And the situation got more chaotic in Canada, and there was backlash to that.

And so I think you were correct to underscore the relative importance of orderliness versus like, the sheer numbers that are involved, but that you’re then shying away from what it takes to maintain orderliness. And I think that the functioning of a kind of ex post asylum system is an invitation to chaos.

Jerusalem Demsas

I think restraining myself from interrupting you when you said that Australia was Jerusalem-ism, I think I deserved some sort of kudos for that because that was beyond the pale, Matthew.

So, I think two things: I mean, I think the first thing I want to say is that this question of gaming the system, I think is really important because I think you and I both agree that many Americans really don’t enjoy the idea that people are not coming here, existing immigrants, they get very upset at the idea that they’re people who are going to jump the line, who don’t get to follow, who don’t have to “follow the rules.”

I think a lot of people often have mistaken understandings of how easy it is to follow the rules to come in, but putting that aside, they really don’t like that idea.

So then the question becomes, what is the way to reduce people from actually gaming the system? And I think a series of extremely specific and narrow programs is actually incentivizing this gaming the system rather than [addressing] it.

Like, yes, this Nigerian situation that you mentioned earlier about how people are being coached on how to, you know, say that they’re gay and at risk of LGBTQ violence, this is true in many other cases. There are people who are Ethiopians who are being coached to sound like they’re from Eritrea.

The entire purpose of having this asylum system is you actually have subject matter experts on the ground who are like, “I know things about Ethiopia and Eritrea, and I ask you questions about Eritrea.”

And, like, you say, you’ve been coached, but … you have gotten coached by a random person you paid $1,000 to for like a week. And I am someone who’s an American consulate official or whatever it is, or asylum officer who is literally trained and has been to your country, has been to this country you’re faking being from, or whatever, for ages. And so, faking that you are LGBTQ? Do you have evidence?

They don’t just say, like, “Hey, yeah, I’m gay.” You have to be like, show us any kind of evidence that you’ve ever been gay. Have you told anyone that you’re gay? Have you been prosecuted? Persecuted in any way?

I think that the whole point of these kinds of judicial system[s] is to be able to say “OK, let’s say [in] Matt’s perfect world, there is some allotment from, like, as you mentioned, these Mexican LGBTQ persecuted individuals, there still is a discretionary system that people will try to game.

Matthew Yglesias

The difference here is between, you know, you’re saying people game O-2, you know, blah blah blah, but there’s a difference between any kind of thing, which is like, apply for a visa at your consulate, and a thing that’s you are here already in the country and you are asking…

Jerusalem Demsas

But that’s Australia. Most of Australia’s foreign born population are visas. I don’t know if it’s most, but very many of these are people who are on visas who then are applying, going like, “Can I have asylum now,” or like “Can I have can I stay here,” that’s what we’re talking about.

Matthew Yglesias

Sure, sure, sure, sure. But I’m saying in the invitation to chaos, right, that we were seeing in the United States under Obama, that I think they’re seeing now in the U.K., right? It’s from the fact that you can apply, you can come in an irregular manner and then make the asylum claim. In Australia, they categorically forbid that.

Jerusalem Demsas

And there’s still irregular migration to Australia.

Matthew Yglesias

There is some, but there is much…

Jerusalem Demsas

Because we live in an irregular world, Matt.

Matthew Ygleisas

There is some, and there is much less.

Jerusalem Demsas

My point is this: Like, what are we trying to solve for?

Matthew Yglesias

This is why I say that Australia is Jerusalem-ism. Because, like, what are we trying to solve for? We want to have — I want to have a world in which we have a good amount…

Jerusalem Demsas

One billion Americans.

Matthew Yglesias

Well, I mean, no, but I mean, broadly in the world, right? Like, international labor mobility has a lot of benefits. Many, many countries are very poorly governed. Lots of people are suffering from illiberal… I mean, LGBT persecution, for example, it’s bad, right? Like, I don’t know, it’s not good. And the United States benefits from a growing labor force, you know? etc.,, etc., etc.

Matthew YglesiasI think it’s a little bit perverse that I feel like — not you, not accusing you — but think that the kind of like, lib-NGO Borg in the United States of America views the pre-Trump American system as better than the Australian system because they regard the American system as less inhumane. But they’re letting in more immigrants in Australia.

Jerusalem Demsas

Well, not more.

Matthew Yglesias

Well, relative to the population. I mean, there’s like seven people in Australia. But I think if you take seriously the view that it’s not really the numbers that matter, it’s the chaos and the orderliness, that points to…

Jerusalem Demsas

But I think I’m actually surprised by this view of yours, actually. I mean, I’m not surprised that you, you know, want to mod out on immigration, but I think I’m surprised by this at like, a principled level that you have because exactly what you said about free movement of people and of understanding there’s a lot of random cases that are still very positive, some that are in the national in the United States exist that are very, very difficult to categorize, and I agree. We want as many applications happening in — not at the Southwest. I don’t think anyone thinks it’s good that these things happen in the Southwest border.

That’s why, you know…

Matthew Yglesias

I think these immigration lawyers do.

Jerusalem Demsas

A lot of these NGOs were in favor of the processing centers being set up through Latin America of the CHNV parole programs, which processed people in their home countries, of the creation of the CBP One app, which made it possible for people to actually get an appointment so they could actually meet with an asylum officer rather than just flooding across the border randomly.

I mean, all of these things were put in place by people who wanted a less chaotic system and who didn’t think it was good that people were just showing up and asking for it. But I think the critical distinction between, I think, our positions here is that there are going to be some situations, unforeseen situations, that are very, very bad, and that are in our national interest to be able to respond to quickly.

And we should develop systems, like the asylum system, where we can have legal ways of adjudicating whether someone fits into this. It is not just completely subject to the ability of Congress to get its shit together in the moment.

And so, that is the distinction here. And I think, also, to say when we’re talking about what’s in America’s national interest here, there is this view that is I think percolating is not just, you know, the specific thing that we’re talking about right now, but it’s a view that’s percolating on a lot of the centrist think tank Borg, which is this idea that you can sort of plan your way to the level of immigration success America has had.

The American success with immigration has largely been founded on open borders. It was Ellis Island, open borders. Unless you had syphilis or something like that, it was open borders for Mexicans to go in and out. And all of these studies that we have showing that the economics of the United States, the culture of the United States, the whatever, the innovation, the dynamism, the Nobel laureates, whatever we’re talking about, are founded on a system in which, of course, no one could select for that, which is something that you believe in many other contexts where it’s very, very difficult to have central planning of these sorts of things.

But now with perhaps the most important market that will ever exist ever, we’re like, “Yeah, we can central plan that and it’ll be fine.” I think that would genuinely harm the national interest.

Matthew Yglesias

But this is what I’m saying. Like, I take this through, like, turn the lens backwards. I think there are lots of people who unambiguously do not qualify for asylum in the United States, who it would be good for them, and it would be good for the United States if they moved here.

You know what I mean? And that makes me less worried about denying a good claim to some random person, because I think there are so many tragic non-immigration blocked migration scenarios under the status quo.

Right? And that’s really bad. And that we ought to be thinking really hard about: How do we get a system that allows for more immigration to the United States in a sustainable way? Which is, a little bit, caring about the electoral politics, but it’s also just a little bit caring about the fact that, you know, we saw this with the Greg Abbott and the bussing stunts that that was a good political stunt that created political backlash.

But the reason it created political backlash is that he created a substantive problem. You know what I mean? It wasn’t just like, “Oh, people in Chicago are freaking out.” They were freaking out because there was a problem.

Jerusalem Demsas

Because they couldn’t handle it.

Matthew Yglesias

Because, you know, there’s a universe in which a large number of immigrants come to Chicago and it’s fine for everybody.

Jerusalem Demsas

Yeah, I mean, it’s literally happened with Ukrainians at the exact same time.

Matthew Yglesias

But there’s also a universe in which a relatively small number of immigrants arrive in Chicago, and it’s a catastrophe. So there’s a dynamic interplay between the substance and the public opinion. Yes. And I think that if you want to solve for like, immigrant-maxxing over a long time horizon, that requires being really strict about anything that smacks of chaos and being tough-minded about these individual sympathetic cases and really trying to assure people and create a system that is based on “What is the maximum number of maximally advantageous immigration interactions…”

Jerusalem Demsas

But Matt, we are agreeing about the fact that there needs to be clear — like, 80% of these people, of Mexicans, are being sent back — that the system that I am supporting is not like, man, we’re just like, letting anyone in. Even before the Biden years, the problem was the backlog, right? It wasn’t that the judges were just letting anybody in. There’s a variety of admittance rates per judge.

Matthew Yglesias

And it’s the quantity, I think, of countries being traversed. You know what I mean? Like, creating chains of problems globally.

Jerusalem Demsas

I think that the history of humanity has been that people are going to try to cross places to get to better opportunity, and we’re not going to eliminate that when there’s such massive differences in people’s life outcomes depending on a border that they are across. And so, we’re not going to solve that. That’s not a thing to be solved. That’s a thing to fix.

But I think, more broadly, the substantive problem that I still think you’re not really addressing is that there are going to be many, many situations in the national interest that you cannot plan for.

It’s not a small problem if we just don’t have a system one day to let in this important Chinese dissident who — whatever. And they get back and they get murdered by the Chinese government, or they disappear; that’s a big issue. AndI agree, there are problems in both worlds, but you’re not even explaining how you would solve for that.

Matthew Yglesias

I mean, I just think we’re flipping really fast from Chinese dissidents to cartel journalists in Mexico or…

Jerusalem Demsas
